I'll conduct this design as a formal systems architecture exercise, building from first principles with explicit invariants and proof obligations. No code will be presentedΟΔβ²§βÄùonly specifications, state machines, invariants, and proof sketches.

PART I ΟΔβ²§βÄù FORMAL FAILURE MODEL
1.1 Crash Model: Fail-Recover with Amnesia
AspectDefinitionProcess crashImmediate halt. Volatile state lost. Persistent state (WAL on disk) survives iff fsync acknowledged.RecoveryProcess restarts with empty memory, replays WAL from last checkpoint. No assumption of stable identity across restarts.AmnesiaRecovered process has no memory of in-flight work. Must reconstruct state from durable log.Disk crashIndividual block device may experience torn writes (power loss mid-sector). Detected via checksums.
Critical invariant: All decisions with external effect must be WAL-persisted before effect visibility.
1.2 Network Model: Asynchronous with Message Loss and Duplication
PropertySpecificationTimingNo bounds on message delay. No synchronized clocks.PartitionArbitrary subset of nodes may be unreachable from others indefinitely.DeliveryMessages may be lost, duplicated, reordered. No exactly-once delivery guarantee from network.FIFONot guaranteed. TCP provides byte-stream order, but connections may reset.
Implication: All "at-least-once" delivery properties must be built atop this using explicit acknowledgments and deduplication.
1.3 Storage Guarantees: Strict Durability with Torn Write Awareness
PropertySpecificationfsync contractfsync() returns success ΟΔβÄ†βÄô data durable on power loss.Atomic writeWrites ΟΔβÄΑ¬Λ 512 bytes (atomic sector) are atomic. Larger writes may tear at 512-byte boundaries.ChecksumEvery WAL entry carries CRC32C. Torn write detected via checksum mismatch + length validation.Write orderSame file descriptor: writes observable in program order. No guarantee across fds.
Torn write model: For N-byte write where N > 512, any prefix [0,k*512] may be durable, suffix garbage.
1.4 Memory Model: x86-64 TSO with Explicit Fences
AspectSpecificationBase modelTotal Store Order (TSO) ΟΔβ²§βÄù loads may be reordered with earlier stores to different addresses.Fencessfence, lfence, mfence available. LOCK prefix provides full barrier.Relaxed atomicsmemory_order_relaxed provides no ordering guarantees.Acquire/Releasememory_order_acquire/release map to LOCK or MFENCE on x86.
Design constraint: All inter-thread communication uses release/acquire or seq_cst atomics. No data races.
1.5 Adversary Model: Non-Byzantine (Crash-Fault) with Network Adversary
CapabilityDescriptionCrash timingAny process may crash at any instruction boundary.Network delayArbitrary, unbounded. May partition indefinitely.No Byzantine faultsCorrect processes follow protocol. No malicious corruption of messages.No omniscienceAdversary does not know future random values (fencing tokens derived from cryptographically secure RNG).

PART II ΟΔβ²§βÄù SAFETY AND LIVENESS SPECIFICATION
2.1 Safety Properties
S1: No Task Lost
DefinitionInvariantΟΔΥÜβ²§ task t: if Submit(t) acknowledged by system, then Execute(t) ΟΔΥÜ¬® t in persistent queue.ViolationAcknowledged task never executes; acknowledged task deleted without execution.PreventionAcknowledgment sent only after WAL append with fsync. Task recovered by any node replaying WAL.
S2: Exactly-Once Execution
DefinitionInvariantΟΔΥÜβ²§ task t: Execute(t) occurs at most once in system's observable history.ViolationSame task executed on two nodes; same task executed twice on one node across recovery.Prevention(a) Idempotency key ΟΔβÄ†βÄô deterministic shard assignment ΟΔβÄ†βÄô single owner epoch; (b) Fencing tokens prevent stale owners from committing; (c) WAL-recorded "executed" bit prevents replay.
S3: Dependency Ordering Preserved
DefinitionInvariantΟΔΥÜβ²§ tasks tΟΔβÄö¬¹, tΟΔβÄöβÄö: if Depends(tΟΔβÄö¬¹, tΟΔβÄöβÄö) in DAG, then Commit(tΟΔβÄö¬¹) before Begin(tΟΔβÄöβÄö).ViolationtΟΔβÄöβÄö executes before tΟΔβÄö¬¹ despite declared dependency.PreventionDependency edges materialized as blocking records in WAL. tΟΔβÄöβÄö's "ready" condition requires observable Commit(tΟΔβÄö¬¹) in shard's replicated state.
S4: Deterministic Replay
DefinitionInvariantRecovery produces identical system state as original execution would have.ViolationNon-deterministic choices (RNG without seed, timestamp dependency, concurrency races) cause divergence.PreventionAll external inputs logged to WAL; task scheduling order by deterministic comparison of (epoch, sequence); no wall-clock dependencies in core logic.
2.2 Liveness Properties
L1: Eventually Executes (if dependencies resolvable)
DefinitionInvariantΟΔΥÜβ²§ task t: if all dependencies ΟΔΥÜβÄΠ or committed, and system has ΟΔβÄΑ¬Ξ 1 correct node, then ΟΔβÄîβÄΓ Execute(t).ViolationTask orphaned by crashed owner, no failover; deadlock in dependency resolution; infinite retry backoff.Progress(a) Membership protocol detects owner failure; (b) Epoch advancement triggers rebalancing; (c) Exponential backoff capped at 30s with jitter.
L2: Progress Under Partial Failure
DefinitionInvariantIf majority of shard owners correct and connected, system continues processing tasks for those shards.ViolationMinority failure halts all processing; leader election deadlocks.ProgressShard-based parallel consensus. Failed shards stall; healthy shards progress independently.

PART III ΟΔβ²§βÄù DETERMINISTIC STATE MACHINE
3.1 Task Lifecycle State Transition Table
State := PENDING | BLOCKED(deps) | READY | ASSIGNED(epoch, owner) | 
         EXECUTING | COMPLETED(result) | FAILED(error, retry_count)

Transitions:
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PENDING --[Submit]--> BLOCKED(deps)      if deps ΟΔβÄΑ¬† ΟΔΥÜβÄΠ
PENDING --[Submit]--> READY              if deps = ΟΔΥÜβÄΠ

BLOCKED(deps) --[DepCommitted(d)]--> BLOCKED(deps \ {d})
BLOCKED(ΟΔΥÜβÄΠ) --[Sanity]--> READY

READY --[Assign]--> ASSIGNED(epoch, owner)   // owner is node_id, epoch is logical time

ASSIGNED(e,o) --[LeaseExpire]--> READY       // fencing token invalid
ASSIGNED(e,o) --[BeginExec]--> EXECUTING     // only if fencing token matches current epoch

EXECUTING --[Success, result]--> COMPLETED(result)
EXECUTING --[Failure, retryable]--> READY   if retry_count < max
EXECUTING --[Failure, fatal]--> FAILED(error, final)

COMPLETED --[GC]--> ΟΔ≈†¬Ξ                        // log compaction, snapshot advance
FAILED --[Archive]--> ΟΔ≈†¬Ξ

3.2 WAL Record Structure
pythonDownloadCopy codeWALRecord := {
  epoch:          u64,           # logical epoch of owning shard
  sequence:       u64,           # monotonic per-shard, strictly increasing
  checksum:       u32,           # CRC32C of serialized record
  type:           enum {
                    SUBMIT,      # new task creation
                    DEP_ADD,     # dynamic dependency injection
                    DEP_SAT,     # dependency satisfied notification
                    ASSIGN,      # task assigned to node
                    BEGIN,       # execution starting
                    COMPLETE,    # execution finished, result recorded
                    FAIL,        # execution failed, retry state
                    EPOCH_ADV,   # shard epoch advancement (fencing)
                  },
  task_id:        u128,          # global unique (hi=shard_id, lo=sequence)
  idempotency_key: bytes[32],    # client-provided, collision-resistant
  payload_hash:   bytes[32],     # SHA-256 of opaque task payload
  dependencies:   [u128],        # for SUBMIT, DEP_ADD: task IDs this blocks on
  result_hash:    bytes[32],     # for COMPLETE: hash of result
  node_id:        u64,           # for ASSIGN, BEGIN: claiming executor
  fencing_token:  u64,           # for BEGIN: must match current epoch
  timestamp:      u64,           # logical sequence number, NOT wall-clock
}
3.3 Commit Point Definition
The commit point is fsync completion of WAL append with matching epoch and sequence.
Formally: for operation op on task t, Commit(op) occurs at wall-clock time t_c where:

* WAL record R for op has been appended to shard's log
* fsync(R.offset) returned success at t_c
* R.sequence = prev_sequence + 1 (verified by atomic increment)

Invariants at commit point:
I_commit: ΟΔΥÜβ²§ record R committed at sequence s:
  (1) All records with sequence < s are committed
  (2) R.epoch ΟΔβÄΑ¬Ξ previous_record.epoch  [epoch monotonic]
  (3) R.fencing_token = current_epoch if R.type ΟΔΥÜΥÜ {ASSIGN, BEGIN}

3.4 Replay Algorithm
function Recover(shard_id):
  1. Read last checkpoint (if any) ΟΔβÄ†βÄô snapshot at sequence S_checkpoint
  2. Open WAL files from S_checkpoint onward
  3. For each record R in sequence order:
       a. Verify checksum(R)
       b. If checksum fails: truncate at previous record, halt recovery
       c. Apply R to in-memory state machine (deterministic function)
       d. Track max_epoch seen
  4. Set current_epoch = max_epoch + 1  # fence all prior leases
  5. Broadcast EPOCH_ADV(current_epoch) to other nodes
  6. Enter normal operation

State application (deterministic):
  SUBMIT:      insert task to PENDING or BLOCKED
  DEP_ADD:    add edges to dependency graph, recompute readiness
  DEP_SAT:    mark dependency satisfied, potentially unblock
  ASSIGN:     update task.owner, set ASSIGNED
  BEGIN:      verify fencing_token == current_epoch; set EXECUTING
  COMPLETE:   set COMPLETED, record result, trigger downstream DEP_SAT
  FAIL:       increment retry_count, reset to READY or FAILED
  EPOCH_ADV:  update current_epoch (must be > previous)

3.5 Idempotency Boundary
Idempotency domain: (idempotency_key, shard_id, epoch_created)

* Client submits (key, payload) ΟΔβÄ†βÄô system computes shard_id = hash(key) % N_shards
* Duplicate SUBMIT with same key in same epoch_created ΟΔβÄ†βÄô detected via in-memory or checkpoint-resident key index
* Cross-epoch detection: WAL scanned for COMPLETED or FAILED(final) with matching key

Exactly-once guarantee: At most one COMPLETE record exists per idempotency_key in all history of a shard.
3.6 Dependency Resolution Algorithm
Dependency graph G = (V_tasks, E_depends_on)

function AddDependency(child, parent):
  # Called when DEP_ADD or SUBMIT(with deps) committed
  Edge e = (child ΟΔβÄ†βÄô parent)
  if parent ΟΔΥÜΥÜ CompletedSet:
    Immediately enqueue DEP_SAT(child, parent)
  else:
    Add e to G_waiting
    parent.waiters.add(child)

function OnComplete(parent):
  for child in parent.waiters:
    Enqueue DEP_SAT(child, parent) to WAL

Readiness condition for child:
  ΟΔΥÜβ²§ p ΟΔΥÜΥÜ child.dependencies: p ΟΔΥÜΥÜ CompletedSet
  ΟΔΥÜ¬ß no cycle detected (via incremental DFS on G_waiting)

Cycle detection:
  - On each DEP_ADD: run DFS from child following ΟΔβÄ†βÄô edges
  - If reach child: cycle exists, reject with CYCLE_DETECTED
  - Complexity bounded: max dependency depth = 16 (enforced)

3.7 Deadlock Detection
No wait-for cycles possible by construction:

* Tasks only wait for completed dependencies (DAG edges are await-completion, not await-lock)
* Dependency addition is static at task creation or injected before execution begins
* Once EXECUTING, no new incoming dependencies allowed

Liveness hazard: Circular dependency creation at runtime.

* Detected at DEP_ADD commit time via DFS (see 3.6)
* Rejected before task becomes READY

3.8 Minimal Invariant Set & Inductive Proof
Core Invariants
IDInvariantTypeI1Sequence numbers strictly increasing per shardSafetyI2Epoch monotonic: epoch_{t+1} > epoch_tSafetyI3Fencing token validity: BEGIN only succeeds with token = current_epochSafetyI4Dependency satisfaction: task t in EXECUTING ΟΔβÄΓβÄô all deps in COMPLETEDSafetyI5Exactly-once: at most one COMPLETE per idempotency_keySafetyI6WAL durability: committed record survives crashSafetyI7Task liveness: READY task eventually ASSIGNED (if healthy owner)Liveness
Inductive proof sketch for I1 (sequence monotonicity):

* Base: Empty shard, first record has sequence 0.
* Step: Assume record at sequence k committed. Next append reads max_sequence = k, increments atomically (CAS loop), writes k+1. Atomic increment ensures no duplicate sequence. fsync before acknowledgment ensures durability.

Inductive proof sketch for I5 (exactly-once):

* Base: Key not in index. First SUBMIT with key K creates record. Index updated only on COMPLETE commit.
* Step: Duplicate SUBMIT observes key in index (either PENDING or terminal). System returns existing task_id without new WAL entry. For COMPLETE: shard-owned index ensures atomic check-and-set. If two COMPLETE race, first to commit wins; second sees existing COMPLETE, becomes no-op.


PART IV ΟΔβ²§βÄù DISTRIBUTED COORDINATION
4.1 Membership Protocol: Gossip-based with Failure Suspicion
NodeState := { ACTIVE, SUSPECT, FAILED, LEFT }

Protocol:
1. Each node maintains heartbeat table: node_id ΟΔβÄ†βÄô (seq, last_heard, incarnation)
2. Periodic (100ms) gossip to K random peers (K=3)
3. Push-Pull: send local table, receive remote, merge max(seq, incarnation)
4. Failure suspicion: if last_heard > T_fail (10s), mark SUSPECT
5. Confirmation: SUSPECT propagated; if not refuted in T_confirm (5s), mark FAILED
6. Incarnation increment on restart (distinguishes new process from zombie)

Properties:
- No central registry
- Eventual consistency of membership view
- Strong partition resistance: minority partition continues independently

4.2 Shard Ownership Consensus: Multi-Paxos per Shard
No global leader. Each shard has independent consensus group (default: 3 replicas).
Shard group: replica set R = {r0, r1, r2}
Role per shard: LEADER, FOLLOWER, CANDIDATE

Leader election:
- When FOLLOWER timeout (randomized 150-300ms) expires without AppendEntries from leader
- Increment term, convert to CANDIDATE, request votes from majority
- Voter rejects if: term < current, or log behind (last_log_index, last_log_term)

Log replication:
- Leader appends client SUBMIT to local WAL
- Broadcast AppendEntries to followers
- Majority ACK ΟΔβÄ†βÄô commit, respond to client
- Followers apply committed entries to state machine

4.3 Epoch and Fencing Token Scheme
Epoch definition:
  shard.epoch = max(term, recovery_count)

Fencing token generation:
  token = (epoch << 32) | leader_start_time_nonce

Token validation:
  - On ASSIGN: token must match current leader's epoch
  - On BEGIN: executor presents token; state machine verifies epoch match
  - Token rejected if epoch < current_epoch

Epoch advancement triggers:
  1. New leader elected (higher term)
  2. Recovery completes (detected uncommitted entries, must advance)
  3. Explicit fence request from higher-priority node (rebalancing)

4.4 Split-Brain Resolution
ScenarioResolutionTwo leaders in same termImpossible by Paxos: leader requires majority, two majorities intersectTwo leaders in different termsLower-term leader steps down on seeing higher term in any messagePartition with leader in minorityMinority leader steps down (cannot replicate); majority partition elects new leader; epochs divergeRe-mergeTerm comparison resolves; divergent logs: newer term overwrites, same term: longer log wins
Dual execution prevention:

* Only leader can ASSIGN
* ASSIGN carries fencing token from that leader's epoch
* Old leader's ASSIGNs rejected by executors (epoch check)
* Even if two leaders temporarily think they're active, fencing tokens distinguish epochs

4.5 Rebalancing on Node Failure
Failure detected: node N marked FAILED in gossip

For each shard S where N was leader:
  1. Remaining replicas initiate election (randomized timeout)
  2. New leader elected (must have most recent log)
  3. New leader broadcasts EPOCH_ADV(epoch+1)
  4. Tasks in ASSIGNED(N, old_epoch) ΟΔβÄ†βÄô reset to READY
  5. New assignments use fresh fencing tokens

For shards where N was follower:
  1. Leader detects follower loss via heartbeat timeout
  2. If replication factor < target, add replacement from pool
  3. Log catch-up via standard snapshot + incremental

Ownership transfer (rebalancing for load):
  1. Source leader: STOP_ACCEPTING new SUBMIT
  2. Wait for in-flight to complete or timeout
  3. Transfer WAL tail to destination
  4. Destination: replay, catch up to source
  5. Atomic handoff: source writes LEADER_TRANSFER, steps down
  6. Destination becomes leader with epoch+1

4.6 CAP Classification
CP with available partition tolerance for subsets.
PropertyStatusConsistencyStrong (linearizable per shard via Paxos)AvailabilityPartition-tolerant for majority partitions; minority partition unavailable for writesPartition toleranceMaintains safety under all partitions; liveness only for majority
Formal statement: For each shard, if a majority of replicas are correct and mutually reachable, the shard remains available. Under partition, at most one majority-side leader exists; safety preserved.

PART V ΟΔβ²§βÄù EXACTLY-ONCE SEMANTICS
5.1 Exact Commit Point
COMMIT_POINT(task execution):
  1. Client submits task with idempotency_key K
  2. Shard leader appends SUBMIT record to WAL
  3. Majority replication ACK received
  4. fsync(local WAL) confirmed
  5. SUBMIT acknowledged to client  ΟΔβÄ†¬ê EXTERNAL COMMIT POINT
  
  6. (later) ASSIGN record committed
  7. (later) BEGIN record committed with fencing token
  8. Task executes (side effects occur)
  9. COMPLETE record committed  ΟΔβÄ†¬ê EXECUTION COMMIT POINT
  
  10. Result acknowledged to client / next tasks triggered

External effect visibility: After step 9. Before step 9, effects are tentative and rolled back on crash.

5.2 Side-Effect Isolation Strategy
Two-phase external commit:
Phase 1 (Prepare):
  - Execute task logic
  - Capture all external effects as pending operations
  - Write PENDING_EFFECTS { ops, preimage } to WAL
  - Acquire idempotency lease from external system (if supported)

Phase 2 (Commit):
  - Append COMMIT_EFFECTS to WAL
  - Apply effects to external systems
  - External systems must support:
      * idempotent application (dedupe via lease/preimage), OR
      * two-phase commit (prepare/confirm)

Fallback for non-idempotent external systems:
  - Effect application logged with unique sequence
  - On recovery, check external system state vs logged sequence
  - Re-apply if absent, skip if present

5.3 Retry Duplicate Prevention
MechanismScopeIdempotency key indexPrevents duplicate SUBMITFencing tokensPrevents stale executor from committingWAL sequencePrevents duplicate COMPLETE (same or different executor)External leasePrevents duplicate external effects
Retry flow:
Client timeout / error ΟΔβÄ†βÄô retry with same K
Server receives: K exists in PENDING/ASSIGNED/EXECUTING
  ΟΔβÄ†βÄô Return current status (no re-execution)
Server receives: K in COMPLETED
  ΟΔβÄ†βÄô Return cached result (no re-execution)
Server receives: K not found (log truncation / new shard)
  ΟΔβÄ†βÄô Full replay from snapshot; index reconstruction finds K or not
  ΟΔβÄ†βÄô If not found: treat as new SUBMIT (key truly new or very old GC'd)

5.4 Crash Window Analysis
(a) Before WAL append
State: Client submitted, no record durable.
Detection: Client retry.
Resolution: Duplicate detection fails (no record), re-SUBMIT. Idempotency key ensures single eventual entry.
(b) After WAL append but before ACK
State: Record durable, client unaware.
Detection: Client retry.
Resolution: Server sees existing SUBMIT with matching K, returns success (may need to re-replicate if leader changed).
(c) After execution but before COMPLETE commit
State: Effects possibly applied externally, no durable record.
Detection: Recovery scans WAL; finds BEGIN but no COMPLETE.
Resolution: Critical window. Must determine: did effects occur?
Handler:

* If external system has idempotency lease from our fencing token: effect applied, write COMPLETE on recovery
* Else: re-execute (external system must be idempotent or detectable)

Determinism requirement: Task must be deterministic, or external state must be reconstructible.
(d) During dependency resolution
State: DEP_ADD or DEP_SAT in flight, graph partially constructed.
Detection: Recovery replays WAL; incomplete graph detected.
Resolution: Replay reconstructs exact same graph state. Dependency edges are pure function of WAL records. No divergence.
(e) During failover
State: Old leader crashed, new leader electing.
Detection: Gossip + Paxos timeout.
Resolution:

* Uncommitted entries from old leader: not visible (no majority ACK)
* New leader's epoch fence prevents old leader from completing
* Tasks in ASSIGNED(old_epoch): reset to READY, re-scheduled


PART VI ΟΔβ²§βÄù CONCURRENCY & MEMORY DISCIPLINE
6.1 Concurrency Model: Sharding + Actor-per-Shard
LevelModelCross-shardMessage-passing (gRPC/TCP), no shared stateIntra-shardSingle event-loop (io_uring/epoll) per shard leader; followers use thread-per-connection for replicationCPU parallelismOne thread per physical shard; NUMA-aware pinning
No global mutex: Each shard's state machine is single-threaded. Cross-shard operations (rare: dependency across shards) use async message passing with callback completion.
6.2 Shared State Strategy
DataOwnershipAccess PatternShard state machineShard thread exclusivelyNo shared accessGossip membership tableBackground thread + RCUReaders lock-free, writer copies & atomics swapMetrics (counters)Shard thread local, periodic aggregationfetch_add relaxed, batchedWAL file handlesShard thread exclusivelyAsync io_uring, no blocking
6.3 Deadlock Prevention

* Wait-for graph: No locks held across I/O (async completion-based)
* Resource ordering: If cross-shard needed, shard_id ordering enforced
* Timeout: All external operations (replication, dependency wait) have deadline

6.4 Memory Ordering
PatternOrderingRationaleShard state updatesSeq_cst within threadSingle thread, compiler barrier sufficientGossip table swapRelease (write) + Acquire (read)RCU patternCounter incrementRelaxedStatistical, no ordering dependencyWAL commit sequenceLOCK prefix / fetch_add seq_cstTotal order required
6.5 ABA Protection
StructureProtectionLock-free queues (cross-thread, rare)Tagged pointers (48-bit pointer + 16-bit sequence in 64-bit)Epoch counters64-bit, overflow impossible in system lifetime
6.6 Backpressure Propagation
Flow control levels:
  L1: Single-shard queue depth > 10k ΟΔβÄ†βÄô stop accepting SUBMIT, return UNAVAILABLE
  L2: Total memory > 80% ΟΔβÄ†βÄô shed non-critical tasks, prioritize COMPLETE
  L3: Disk utilization > 90% ΟΔβÄ†βÄô read-only mode, halt new SUBMIT
  L4: Cross-shard dependency chain depth > 16 ΟΔβÄ†βÄô reject DEP_ADD

Backpressure signal: HTTP 503 + Retry-After header
Client behavior: exponential backoff with jitter

6.7 Bounded Memory Enforcement
MechanismLimitPer-shard task queue1M tasks (configurable)Dependency graph16-depth, 64 outgoing edges per taskIn-flight executionsconcurrency_limit per shardWAL retention48 hours or 100GB, whichever first (compaction)Snapshot intervalEvery 10M records or 1 hour
OOM prevention: Pre-allocated buffers for hot paths. mlock critical WAL buffers.

PART VII ΟΔβ²§βÄù PERFORMANCE MODEL
7.1 Throughput Math for 100K/sec
Assumptions:
Task size: 1KB payload average
WAL record size: ~256 bytes overhead + 1KB payload = 1.25KB
Replication: 3x (1 leader + 2 followers)
Network: 10 Gbps = 1.25 GB/s
Disk: NVMe SSD, 500K IOPS, 3 GB/s sequential write

Per-shard throughput:
Paxos round-trip: 2 messages (AppendEntries + majority ACK)
Latency budget: 1ms RTT ΟΔβÄ†βÄô 500 rounds/sec per shard (conservative)
Batching: 100 tasks per AppendEntries ΟΔβÄ†βÄô 50,000 tasks/sec per shard

Required shards for 100K/sec: ceil(100K / 50K) = 2 shards minimum
Safety margin: 10 shards for parallelism, hotspot mitigation

Disk bandwidth:
100K tasks/sec ΟÉβÄî 1.25KB ΟÉβÄî 3 replicas = 375 MB/sec write
Well within NVMe capacity (3 GB/s). 
With compression (zstd-3): ~150 MB/sec.

7.2 Bottleneck Analysis
ComponentTheoretical MaxDesign LimitHeadroomNetwork (10G)1.25 GB/s375 MB/s3.3ΟÉβÄîDisk sequential3 GB/s150 MB/s (compressed)20ΟÉβÄîDisk IOPS500K1K (batched writes)500ΟÉβÄîCPU (task exec)-User-definedN/APaxos latency-1ms-
Actual bottleneck: Task execution time (user code). System overhead < 5%.
7.3 Contention Analysis
ResourceContention LevelMitigationShard leadershipPer-shard10 shards, independentGlobal sequenceNonePer-shard sequenceIdempotency indexHash-partitioned within shard256 buckets, lock-free per bucketDependency graphPer-shard, rare cross-shardDepth limit, cycle detectionGossip tableRead-heavyRCU, copy-on-write
7.4 Hot Key Mitigation
Problem: Single idempotency_key ΟΔβÄ†βÄô single shard ΟΔβÄ†βÄô hotspot.
Mitigation:
Key space: idempotency_key || timestamp_nonce
Shard assignment: hash(key) with rendezvous hashing for rebalancing

Auto-sharding: If shard > 50K tasks/sec sustained:
  1. Split shard: create S' with half key range
  2. Migrate records in key range [a,b) to S'
  3. Update routing table (gossip-propagated)
  4. Drain and retire old shard

7.5 Tail Latency Control (p99 < 50ms)
SourceBudgetControlNetwork RTT5msSame-DC deployment, RDMA optionalPaxos replication10msBatching 100 tasks, pipeliningDisk fsync5msNVMe with polling, SPDK optionalQueueing25msAdaptive batch sizing, load sheddingExecution5msUser SLA enforcement, timeout
Techniques:

* io_uring with polling mode for sub-Οé¬Φs syscall overhead
* Task execution isolation: separate thread pool, preemption via deadline
* Priority queue: COMPLETE records prioritized over SUBMIT

7.6 Failure-Mode Degradation
FailurePerformance ImpactRecoverySingle node crash1/N shards stall for 1-2sAutomatic failoverNetwork partition (minority)Partitioned nodes unavailableMajority continuesDisk degradationAffected shard latency spikeAutomatic re-replicationHot spotSingle shard > 50K/sec ΟΔβÄ†βÄô latency ΟΔβÄ†βÄ‰Auto-sharding trigger

PART VIII ΟΔβ²§βÄù STORAGE ENGINE REQUIREMENTS
8.1 WAL Durability Guarantees
Write path:
  1. Serialize WAL record to thread-local buffer
  2. Copy to O_DIRECT aligned buffer (512B multiple)
  3. pwritev2 with RWF_SYNC (or fsync after batch)
  4. Wait for completion (io_uring CQ)
  5. Update in-memory sequence, release ACK

Guarantees:
  - fsync failures: retry with exponential backoff, alert if persistent
  - Power loss: records up to last completed fsync durable
  - No write caching: O_DIRECT | O_DSYNC or explicit fdatasync

8.2 Log Compaction Strategy
Level-triggered compaction:

L0: Recent WAL files (uncompacted, 64MB each)
L1: SST-like format, sorted by task_id, zstd-compressed
L2: Snapshot format, full state machine serialization

Trigger: L0 size > 1GB OR time since last snapshot > 1 hour

Compaction process:
  1. Scan L0 files, build index: task_id ΟΔβÄ†βÄô latest record
  2. For each task: if COMPLETED/FAILED(final), emit only terminal record
  3. Write L1 files, update manifest atomically (rename)
  4. Delete L0 files referenced in new L1

Snapshot (L2):
  1. Stop the world for shard (milliseconds)
  2. Serialize state machine to checkpoint file
  3. Record snapshot sequence number
  4. Truncate WAL before snapshot

8.3 Snapshotting Model
Snapshot S at sequence s contains:
  - All tasks with sequence ΟΔβÄΑ¬Λ s
  - Dependency graph state at s
  - Idempotency index at s
  - Current epoch

Format: Cap'n Proto (zero-copy serialization, schema evolution)

Integrity: SHA-256 of snapshot, stored in separate .sha256 file

8.4 Recovery Algorithm Step-by-Step
function Recover(shard_path):
  1. List files: find newest checkpoint (*.chk) and WAL files (*.wal)
  2. Verify checkpoint checksum
     - If fail: try previous checkpoint
     - If all fail: crash, manual intervention required
  3. Load checkpoint ΟΔβÄ†βÄô state machine at sequence s_chk
  4. For each WAL file with start_sequence > s_chk:
     a. Verify file header magic + version
     b. Scan records in sequence order
     c. For each record:
        i.   Verify checksum
        ii.  If checksum fail: truncate here, log warning
        iii. If sequence != expected: gap detected, halt (corruption)
        iv.  Apply to state machine (deterministic function)
        v.   Track max_epoch, max_sequence
  5. If gaps or unrecoverable checksum failures:
     a. Attempt to contact peer replicas for log repair
     b. If majority have intact log: fetch missing segment
     c. Else: promote warning to fatal, require manual recovery
  6. Set current_epoch = max_epoch + 1
  7. Open new WAL file at max_sequence + 1
  8. Replay any in-progress tasks (ASSIGNED without COMPLETE):
     a. Check external effect status (see 5.4c)
     b. Write COMPLETE or reset to READY
  9. Begin accepting requests

8.5 Corruption Detection and Mitigation
Corruption TypeDetectionMitigationTorn write (power loss)Checksum mismatch + length validationTruncate at last valid recordBit rot (silent)Checksum mismatch on readReplicate from peer; if quorum corrupt, manual restore from backupMisdirected write (wrong offset)Sequence number jumpGap detection halts; peer repairMetadata corruptionManifest parse failPrevious manifest + WAL replayCheckpoint corruptionSHA-256 mismatchPrevious checkpoint

PART IX ΟΔβ²§βÄù OBSERVABILITY & CORRUPTION DETECTION
9.1 Metrics (Prometheus exposition)
# Safety-critical counters
task_submitted_total{shard}
task_completed_total{shard, status="success|failure|timeout"}
task_executed_duplicates_prevented_total  # should be 0
fence_token_rejections_total              # old leader attempts

# Liveness indicators
task_queue_depth{shard}
paxos_leader_elections_total{shard, reason="timeout|step_down|transfer"}
dependency_resolution_cycles_detected_total

# Performance
task_latency_seconds_bucket{le, phase="submit|assign|execute|complete"}
wal_fsync_latency_seconds

# Resource
memory_bytes{type="heap|mlock|cache"}
disk_bytes{type="wal|snapshot|free"}

9.2 Invariant Violation Detection
Continuous validators (async, each 1s):
  V1: ΟΔΥÜβ²§ shard, committed_sequence == max(applied_sequence)
  V2: ΟΔΥÜβ²§ task, COMPLETE ΟΔβÄΓβÄô all dependencies COMPLETED (graph check)
  V3: idempotency_index.entries == count(COMPLETE) + count(FAILED_final)
  V4: epoch_monotonic: current_epoch >= all historical epochs

Alert: CRITICAL if any violation detected
Action: Halt shard, trigger automatic recovery

9.3 Replay Audit Tools
Tool: wal-audit shard-path [--from sequence] [--to sequence]

1. Replays WAL into in-memory state machine
2. Compares final state to declared checkpoint
3. Reports: sequence gaps, checksum failures, state divergence

Tool: determinism-check task-id

1. Re-executes task with logged inputs
2. Compares result hash to logged result
3. Reports: deterministic or not

9.4 Determinism Verification
Strategy: Shadow execution

Production: executes normally, logs all external calls
Shadow: replay on idle node with same inputs
Comparison: external call sequence + results must match

Frequency: 0.1% of tasks (configurable)
Alert: mismatch indicates non-determinism bug

9.5 Divergence Detection Across Replicas
Background process (per shard, every 30s):
  1. Leader computes state hash: Merkle tree of task records
  2. Requests state hash from followers
  3. Mismatch triggers:
     a. Binary search for divergence point
     b. Log repair from leader
     c. If leader corrupt, step down, new election


PART X ΟΔβ²§βÄù TEST STRATEGY
10.1 Crash Injection Testing
Framework: deterministic simulation (TigerBeetle-style)

Technique:
  1. Record all non-deterministic inputs (RNG, timing, network)
  2. Replay with crash injected at every syscall boundary
  3. Verify: safety invariants hold, system recovers, no divergence

Coverage: every WAL operation, every network send/recv, every fsync

10.2 Partition Simulation
Tool: iptables/tc wrapper or nsenter network namespace

Scenarios:
  - Leader isolated (minority partition)
  - Follower isolated from leader but not each other
  - Complete network blackout 5s
  - Slow network (100ms added latency)
  - Packet loss 1%

Verification: 
  - Safety: no duplicate execution, no committed loss
  - Liveness: majority partition continues

10.3 Fuzz Testing State Transitions
Generator: QuickCheck/Hypothesis style
  - Random valid task DAGs
  - Random injected dependencies at runtime
  - Random crash/recovery timing

Oracle: Linearizability checker (Porcupine or custom)
  - History of client operations
  - Verify existence of sequential execution equivalent to observed

10.4 Deterministic Replay Validation
Procedure:
  1. Run workload for 1 hour, record all I/O
  2. Replay recording on fresh cluster
  3. Compare final state: task states, external effects, metrics
  4. Must be bit-identical (except timestamps)

10.5 Load Testing
Profile:
  - Sustain 100K tasks/sec for 24 hours
  - Burst to 150K for 5 minutes
  - Node failure every 10 minutes (random)
  - Network partition every 30 minutes (10s duration)

Success criteria:
  - Zero safety violations
  - p99 latency < 50ms sustained
  - Recovery time < 5s per failure

10.6 Formal Model Checking
Model: TLA+ specification of core protocol

Properties checked:
  - Safety: TypeInvariant, NoDuplication, DependencyOrder
  - Liveness: TaskEventuallyCompletes, LeaderEventuallyElected

State space: Bounded (3 nodes, 2 shards, 5 tasks)
Tool: TLC model checker

10.7 Specific Test: Crash Between WAL Write and Execution
Scenario:
  T1: Client submits task
  T2: Server appends BEGIN to WAL, fsyncs
  T3: Crash (SIGKILL)
  T4: Recovery

Verification:
  - State machine shows task ASSIGNED, not EXECUTING or COMPLETE
  - Recovery detects: BEGIN without COMPLETE
  - External effect check: none applied (lease not acquired)
  - Decision: re-execute (safe, deterministic)
  - Final state: COMPLETE with result
  - Idempotency check: second identical result accepted, no duplicate execution


PART XI ΟΔβ²§βÄù FORMAL CHALLENGE
11.1 Proof Sketch: Exactly-Once Safety
Theorem: For any task t with idempotency key k, at most one execution of t has observable side effects.
Proof:
Lemma 1 (Unique Assignment): At any epoch e, at most one node n holds valid fencing token for t's shard.
Proof: Paxos leader election guarantees single leader per term; epoch = max(term, recovery_count) advances only on leader change or recovery; fencing token derived from epoch. ΟΔΥÜ≈Ϋ
Lemma 2 (Idempotent SUBMIT): The idempotency index I ensures at most one SUBMIT record exists for k in shard history.
Proof: SUBMIT accepted only if k ΟΔΥÜβÄΑ I. I updated atomically with SUBMIT WAL append. WAL sequence monotonic, fsync before ACK. ΟΔΥÜ≈Ϋ
Lemma 3 (Execution Commit): COMPLETE record for t exists ΟΔβÄΓβÄô t's effects are committed.
Proof: COMPLETE appended only after (a) fencing token validated, (b) effects prepared, (c) external lease acquired if needed. WAL fsync before ACK to client. ΟΔΥÜ≈Ϋ
Main Proof:

1. By Lemma 2, at most one task record t exists for key k.
2. By Lemma 1, at any time at most one node can BEGIN t.
3. BEGIN requires fencing token valid for current epoch.
4. If node crashes after BEGIN but before COMPLETE, recovery finds no COMPLETE.
5. New leader (higher epoch) resets t to READY; new BEGIN uses new token.
6. External effect lease (acquired at prepare phase) tied to fencing token.
7. Old token invalid; re-execution safe because lease/preimage detects prior effect.
8. If COMPLETE exists, idempotency index prevents re-BEGIN.

Therefore, at most one execution's effects are visible. ΟΔΥÜ≈Ϋ
11.2 Proof Sketch: No Task Loss
Theorem: If SUBMIT(t) acknowledged, then eventually COMPLETE(t) or FAILED_final(t).
Proof:
Assumption: Client receives ACK only after SUBMIT record fsync'd with majority replication.
Case analysis:

1. 
Leader survives: Leader eventually assigns, executes, completes (task queue drains unless external dependency blocks; liveness ensures dependencies resolve or timeout).

2. 
Leader crashes after SUBMIT: Majority replication ensures SUBMIT present on at least one follower. New leader elected, replays SUBMIT, task in PENDING/BLOCKED, proceeds as case 1.

3. 
Leader crashes during execution: Recovery detects incomplete execution (BEGIN, no COMPLETE). Task reset to READY, re-executed.

4. 
Infinite retry exhaustion: After max retries, FAILED_final recorded. Task not lost, terminal state reached.


In all cases, terminal state recorded. ΟΔΥÜ≈Ϋ
11.3 Why Split-Brain Cannot Cause Duplication
Scenario: Network partition separates {A, B} from {C}, where A was leader.

* A continues as leader in minority partition (cannot replicate, no progress on new tasks)
* C, D, E in majority partition elect new leader with higher term
* New leader advances epoch, invalidates A's fencing tokens
* A's subsequent ASSIGN/BEGIN attempts rejected by executors (epoch check)
* A steps down on seeing higher term in any message

Maximum hazard: A may have uncommitted BEGIN in flight. If network heals before A steps down:

* Executor receives BEGIN from A (old epoch), compares to current epoch (higher)
* Rejection: FENCE_REJECTED
* A aborts task, steps down

No execution occurs on stale authority. ΟΔΥÜ≈Ϋ
11.4 Behavior Under Indefinite Partition
Partition ScenarioBehaviorMajority partition existsContinues processing for those shardsMinority partition onlyUnavailable for writes; nodes in SUSPECT state; clients receive UNAVAILABLE with hint to retry on healthy nodesEqual split (rare with odd replicas)Tie-break via incarnation (higher wins); if equal, both sides stall until partition heals
Liveness guarantee: Once partition heals, gossip reconciliation < 10s, leader election < 1s, normal operation resumes.
11.5 Minimal Invariant Set
IDInvariantEnforcesI_seqPer-shard sequence strictly increasingTotal order of recordsI_epochEpoch monotonic across leader changesFencing token validityI_fenceBEGIN only with token = current_epochSingle executor at a timeI_depEXECUTING ΟΔβÄΓβÄô all deps COMPLETEDOrdering correctnessI_idemIdempotency index ΟΔβÄΑ¬Λ 1 entry per keyNo duplicate tasksI_walCommitted record survives crashDurabilityI_ackNo ACK before fsyncExternal commitment safety
11.6 Commit Point Identification
OperationCommit PointSUBMITfsync of SUBMIT record, majority replication ACKASSIGNfsync of ASSIGN record (local durability sufficient, already replicated)BEGINFencing token validation + fsync of BEGINCOMPLETEfsync of COMPLETE, external effect confirmationExternal effectIdempotency lease acknowledgment from external system
11.7 Linearizability and Serializability
PropertyStatusScopeLinearizabilityPer-shardOperations on single shard are linearizable (Paxos provides total order)Sequential consistencyCross-shardDependency edges provide partial order; no global real-time guaranteeSerializabilityTask executionSingle-shard task execution serializable by WAL order; cross-shard dependencies enforce happens-before
Formal statement: The system provides shard-linearizable task lifecycle operations with causal consistency for cross-shard dependencies (dependency edge = causal link). This is the strongest guarantee achievable without global coordination, which would violate the "no global locks" constraint.

CONCLUSION
This design satisfies all stated constraints:

before tΟΔβÄöβÄö transitions READY.

    EXEC_COMMIT(p) is quorum-committed in shard(p)
This provides causal consistency across shards without global consensus.

Auto-sharding previously introduced unsafe key-range migration.

Shard split protocol:

Quorum commit.

New shard replays only records ΟΔβÄΑ¬Ξ s_split for its key-range.

Invariant:

Exactly one shard owns t at any sequence number.
Ownership function:

    if seq < s_split ΟΔβÄ†βÄô S
    else ΟΔβÄ†βÄô hash(t.key, new_shard_layout)
Thus ownership is sequence-dependent and deterministic.

dependency graph

epoch

V. Zombie Executor Elimination (Formal Closure)
Add invariant:

If executor begins EXECUTING under epoch e,
and system advances to epoch e+1,
then executor must observe epoch mismatch before
external Commit phase.
Mechanism:

BEGIN record includes fencing_token.

On recovery, epoch bumped before accepting new BEGIN.

Executors check current_epoch via shared atomic before Commit.

Formal guarantee:

No execution begun under old epoch can reach EXEC_COMMIT under new epoch.

VI. Leader Transfer Formal Safety Lemma
Lemma (Safe Transfer):

If leader L writes LEADER_TRANSFER at sequence s,
and follower F applies all records ΟΔβÄΑ¬Λ s,
then F can become leader without violating:

Sequence monotonicity

Epoch monotonicity

Single-writer property

Proof sketch:

Transfer only allowed if F.matchIndex ΟΔβÄΑ¬Ξ s.

L steps down only after quorum commit of LEADER_TRANSFER.

Any concurrent SUBMIT after s must use new term > term(L).

The safety argument assumes sideΟΔβ²§βÄ‰effects are only after a committed EXEC_COMMIT.Move all external sideΟΔβ²§βÄ‰effects behind the EXEC_COMMIT record and make EXEC_START a pure log entry (no I/O).Raft guarantees that any entry not committed on a majority is invisible to later terms. By making the term bump a prerequisite for any write, duplicate commits are impossible. The change does not affect task ordering because term monotonicity is already used in the commit point definition.
1.2 Deterministic Replay
#TraceInvariantGapCorrectionProofD11. Task T reads current wallΟΔβ²§βÄ‰clock (now()) during execution.<br>2. EXEC_COMMIT stores result rc=0 but does not store the observed timestamp.<br>3. Crash ΟΔβÄ†βÄô replay reads log, reΟΔβ²§βÄ‰executes T, obtains a different timestamp, writes to external DB a different row.Deterministic replay ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ replay must produce identical external state.No capture of all sources of nondeterminism (time, RNG, external service responses).Enforce pureΟΔβ²§βÄ‰function execution: every observable input (including now, RNG seed, external request payload) must be recorded in the log entry (e.g., deterministic_context field). The executor must be a sandbox that refuses any system call not whitelisted.By construction the replay engine reΟΔβ²§βÄ‰injects the recorded context, guaranteeing that the pure function yields identical outputs. The invariant that the log fully determines sideΟΔβ²§βÄ‰effects is now provable by induction on log entries.D21. Task T performs a conditional write to a KV store based on a readΟΔβ²§βÄ‰modifyΟΔβ²§βÄ‰write that is not logged (read is done outside the WAL).<br>2. During replay the KV store has a different value, causing a different write path.Same as D1.Missing readΟΔβ²§βÄ‰set in the log.Extend the WAL record to include a readΟΔβ²§βÄ‰set hash and the value read (or a version number). The executor must abort if the current store version mismatches the logged version, forcing a deterministic retry.The readΟΔβ²§βÄ‰set inclusion makes the log a complete transaction description; replay either reproduces the same write or aborts, preserving the invariant that the external state after a committed transaction is unique.
1.3 CrossΟΔβ²§βÄ‰Shard Causality
#TraceInvariantGapCorrectionProofC11. Task A (shardΟΔβ²§¬·0) commits EXEC_COMMIT(A).<br>2. Network partition isolates shardΟΔβ²§¬·0 from shardΟΔβ²§¬·1.<br>3. ShardΟΔβ²§¬·1, unaware of A, schedules dependent task B and commits EXEC_COMMIT(B) before receiving AΟΔβ²§β³Δs commit.Causal ordering ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ B must not commit before A.Dependency propagation is asynchronous (dependencyΟΔβ²§βÄ‰resolved records are sent as bestΟΔβ²§βÄ‰effort messages). No quorum enforcement across shards.Introduce a twoΟΔβ²§βÄ‰phase global commit for crossΟΔβ²§βÄ‰shard edges: PhaseΟΔβ²§¬·1 ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ shardΟΔβ²§¬·0 sends prepare to shardΟΔβ²§¬·1; PhaseΟΔβ²§¬·2 ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ shardΟΔβ²§¬·1 acknowledges before allowing B to transition to READY. The prepare must be persisted on a quorum of both shards (dualΟΔβ²§βÄ‰raft joint consensus).Joint consensus guarantees that either both shards see the prepare or neither does. Therefore B cannot be committed without AΟΔβ²§β³Δs commit being durably known, preserving causal ordering. The extra phase does not affect intraΟΔβ²§βÄ‰shard ordering because it is only taken for crossΟΔβ²§βÄ‰shard edges.C21. AutoΟΔβ²§βÄ‰sharding moves task T from shardΟΔβ²§¬·2 to shardΟΔβ²§¬·3 while T is in RUNNING state.<br>2. The move is performed by copying the inΟΔβ²§βÄ‰memory state without persisting a moveΟΔβ²§βÄ‰commit record.CrossΟΔβ²§βÄ‰shard causality ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ tasks must not be lost or reordered during reΟΔβ²§βÄ‰sharding.No durable marker that the move succeeded; a crash after copy but before delete leaves duplicate logical tasks.Require a moveΟΔβ²§βÄ‰transaction logged as three records: MOVE_START, MOVE_COMMIT (on destination quorum), MOVE_DONE (deleting source entry). The source entry is only removed after MOVE_COMMIT is durably replicated.The threeΟΔβ²§βÄ‰phase protocol is a classic distributed transaction; by persisting each phase on a quorum, any crash can be recovered to a consistent state (either preΟΔβ²§βÄ‰move or postΟΔβ²§βÄ‰move). No duplicate execution can arise, preserving causality.
1.4 Snapshot Correctness
#TraceInvariantGapCorrectionProofS11. Leader initiates a snapshot at log index i.<br>2. Crash occurs after some follower has persisted entries i+1 ΟΔβ²§¬Π i+K but before they are included in the snapshot.<br>3. New leader restores snapshot (up to i) and reΟΔβ²§βÄ‰applies i+1 ΟΔβ²§¬Π i+K from its own log, but the follower that missed them loses those entries forever.Snapshot consistency ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ restored state must be a prefix of the committed log.Snapshot creation does not wait for all replicas to acknowledge inclusion of entries up to i+K.Make snapshot joint: the leader must obtain acknowledgments from a majority that they have all entries up to i+K before truncating the log. The snapshot metadata must record the highest index i_max that is known to be on a quorum.By requiring quorum acknowledgment, any entry that could be lost on a minority is never truncated. The restored state is therefore a prefix of the globally committed log.S21. Compaction removes log entries older than i while a slow follower still needs them for replay after a crash.Same as S1.Compaction is driven solely by leaderΟΔβ²§β³Δs local waterΟΔβ²§βÄ‰mark.Compaction must be coordinated: a leader may truncate only after receiving replication acknowledgments from a quorum for each entry to be removed.This is exactly RaftΟΔβ²§β³Δs logΟΔβ²§βÄ‰truncation rule; it guarantees that any entry that could be needed for recovery is retained on a majority.
1.5 External SideΟΔβ²§βÄ‰Effect Atomicity
#TraceInvariantGapCorrectionProofX11. Task T writes to external DB outside the WAL transaction (e.g., via async HTTP).<br>2. Crash after DB write but before EXEC_COMMIT is persisted.<br>3. Replay reΟΔβ²§βÄ‰executes T, causing a duplicate DB row.Atomic sideΟΔβ²§βÄ‰effect ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ sideΟΔβ²§βÄ‰effects must appear iff the corresponding log entry is committed.No outΟΔβ²§βÄ‰ofΟΔβ²§βÄ‰band coordination between external system and WAL.Enforce twoΟΔβ²§βÄ‰phase commit with the external system: the task first writes a prepare record to the external DB (idempotent), then after EXEC_COMMIT is durably stored, it sends a finalize message. The external DB must only make the effect visible after finalize.The external system becomes a participant in the atomic transaction; its state transition mirrors the WAL commit point. By the atomicity of 2PC, the sideΟΔβ²§βÄ‰effect appears iff the log entry is committed.X21. SideΟΔβ²§βÄ‰effect is a message sent to a downstream queue that provides atΟΔβ²§βÄ‰leastΟΔβ²§βÄ‰once delivery.<br>2. Crash after sending but before commit.Same as X1.No guarantee that downstream queue can be rolled back.Wrap the downstream queue in a deduplication layer keyed by the taskΟΔβ²§β³Δs task_id and epoch. The queue consumer must be idempotent; the producer must include the key in every message.Idempotent consumption guarantees that duplicate messages do not change external state, satisfying the atomicity invariant without requiring rollback.
1.6 AutoΟΔβ²§βÄ‰Sharding Safety
#TraceInvariantGapCorrectionProofA11. Load balancer decides to split shardΟΔβ²§¬·S into SΟΔβÄö¬¹,SΟΔβÄöβÄö based on CPU usage.<br>2. It moves half the task IDs to SΟΔβÄöβÄö without updating the global hash ring atomically.<br>3. Clients concurrently hash the same task_id to different shards, causing duplicate submissions.Uniqueness of task placement ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ a logical task belongs to exactly one shard at any time.ReΟΔβ²§βÄ‰sharding is not performed as an atomic operation on the routing metadata.Use a consistentΟΔβ²§βÄ‰hash ring versioned by epoch; any reΟΔβ²§βÄ‰sharding must first publish a new version, then wait for all clients to observe the new version (via a quorum read) before moving any tasks. The move itself is performed as a moveΟΔβ²§βÄ‰transaction (see C2).Versioned routing guarantees that no client can observe two different shard assignments for the same key simultaneously. The moveΟΔβ²§βÄ‰transaction ensures that the logical task never exists in two shards concurrently, preserving uniqueness.A21. AutoΟΔβ²§βÄ‰sharding triggers while a leader transfer is in progress.<br>2. New leader assumes the old shardΟΔβ²§β³Δs metadata (including taskΟΔβ²§βÄ‰toΟΔβ²§βÄ‰shard map) is stable, but the map is being rewritten.Leader transfer safety ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ leader must have a consistent view of shard metadata.No ordering guarantee between leader election and metadata update.Serialize metadata updates (including reΟΔβ²§βÄ‰sharding) through the Raft log of a metadata shard that is independent of data shards. Leader election may only proceed after the metadata shardΟΔβ²§β³Δs latest entry is committed.By making metadata a firstΟΔβ²§βÄ‰class replicated state machine, any leader must first apply the latest metadata entry before acting, eliminating race conditions.
1.7 Fencing Token Soundness
#TraceInvariantGapCorrectionProofF11. Leader LΟΔβÄö¬¹ (termΟΔβ²§¬·t) issues epoch token eΟΔβÄö¬¹ to a worker.<br>2. LΟΔβÄö¬¹ crashes before revoking eΟΔβÄö¬¹.<br>3. New leader LΟΔβÄöβÄö (termΟΔβ²§¬·t+1) issues token eΟΔβÄöβÄö (higher).<br>4. Worker holding eΟΔβÄö¬¹ mistakenly believes it is still valid because it does not verify term on each operation.Fencing token monotonicity ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ a later token must invalidate all earlier tokens.Token validation is performed only at acquisition, not on every sideΟΔβ²§βÄ‰effect.Require perΟΔβ²§βÄ‰operation token verification: every write to external state must include the current term (or epoch) and the external system must reject any operation with a stale term.Since term is strictly increasing and external systems enforce the check, any operation using eΟΔβÄö¬¹ after eΟΔβÄöβÄö is issued will be rejected, guaranteeing that only the latest token can cause sideΟΔβ²§βÄ‰effects.F21. Tokens are stored in memory only; a crash loses the tokenΟΔβ²§βÄ‰toΟΔβ²§βÄ‰term mapping.Same as F1.No durable record of issued tokens.Persist token issuance records in the WAL (type TOKEN_ISSUE(term, token_id)). Workers must read the latest token record before proceeding.Persistence ensures that after a crash the system can reconstruct the highest valid token, preventing stale tokens from being used.
1.8 Compaction Refinement
#TraceInvariantGapCorrectionProofCmp11. Compaction removes a DEP_RESOLVED record that is the last evidence that a predecessor has committed.<br>2. A follower that was lagging restarts, replays up to the truncated index, and believes the dependency is still unresolved, blocking the child task forever.DependencyΟΔβ²§βÄ‰resolution visibility ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ every node must be able to infer that a predecessor is committed from its retained log.Compaction criteria are based solely on log index, not on dependency graph completeness.Compaction must be dependencyΟΔβ²§βÄ‰aware: before truncating any DEP_RESOLVED entry, ensure that all tasks that depend on it have either (a) a later READY record or (b) a snapshot that includes the resolved flag.By guaranteeing that any truncated dependency is already reflected in a later state, a lagging node can reconstruct the dependency status from the retained prefix, preserving progress.Cmp21. Log compaction runs concurrently with a leader transfer; the new leaderΟΔβ²§β³Δs log truncation point is stale, causing it to propose entries that conflict with the old leaderΟΔβ²§β³Δs compacted suffix.Leader transfer safety ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ new leader must not propose entries that have been compacted.No coordination of compaction waterΟΔβ²§βÄ‰marks across terms.During a leader transfer, the outgoing leader must transfer its compaction watermark to the incoming leader via a COMPACTION_POINT record that is committed before the transfer completes.The incoming leader will only propose entries with index > COMPACTION_POINT, guaranteeing no conflict with alreadyΟΔβ²§βÄ‰compactable entries.
1.9 Leader Transfer Safety
#TraceInvariantGapCorrectionProofL11. Leader LΟΔβÄö¬¹ steps down after committing up to index i but before replicating i+1 to a majority.<br>2. New leader LΟΔβÄöβÄö starts from its own log (which ends at i).<br>3. LΟΔβÄöβÄö receives a client request that depends on the missing i+1 entry, leading to lost work.No loss of committed work ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ any entry that reached a quorum must survive leader change.Transfer protocol does not verify that the outgoing leaderΟΔβ²§β³Δs commit index is known to a quorum before stepping down.Enforce preΟΔβ²§βÄ‰stepΟΔβ²§βÄ‰down quorum check: LΟΔβÄö¬¹ must obtain acknowledgments from a majority that they have stored entry i+1 (or higher) before relinquishing leadership. If not possible, LΟΔβÄö¬¹ must remain leader until it can.This is identical to RaftΟΔβ²§β³Δs ΟΔβ²§≈™leader must have committed entry at its commitIndex before stepping downΟΔβ²§¬ù. It guarantees that any entry that could be lost is first made durable on a quorum, preserving safety.L21. Transfer occurs while a metadata shard (holding routing tables) is being compacted.<br>2. New leader reads stale routing info, misΟΔβ²§βÄ‰routes tasks, violating crossΟΔβ²§βÄ‰shard causality.Metadata consistency ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ routing tables must be stable across leader changes.No explicit coupling between leader transfer and metadata snapshot.Make metadata snapshot a prerequisite for leader transfer: the outgoing leader must commit a METADATA_SNAPSHOT record that includes the full routing table; the incoming leader must load this snapshot before accepting client traffic.The snapshot is persisted on a quorum, so any leader after the transfer sees a consistent view of routing, eliminating misΟΔβ²§βÄ‰routing.
1.10 Partition Behavior Under Adversarial Timing
#TraceInvariantGapCorrectionProofP11. Network partitions into two halves, each with a minority of nodes for a given shard (e.g., 2/5 vs 3/5).<br>2. Both halves start independent election timers with zero backΟΔβ²§βÄ‰off, causing both to think they have a majority (because they ignore the missing nodes).<br>3. Two leaders emerge, each accepting writes, violating exactlyΟΔβ²§βÄ‰once.Partition tolerance + consistency ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ no two leaders may exist concurrently.Election timer is not bounded below; adversary can force rapid flapping.Enforce a minimum election timeout (e.g., 150ΟΔβ²§¬·ms) and require majority of the configured replica set, not of the observed live set. Nodes must refuse to become candidates if they cannot see a majority of the static configuration.The lower bound prevents an adversary from arbitrarily accelerating elections; the staticΟΔβ²§βÄ‰majority rule guarantees that a minority cannot form a quorum, thus preserving the singleΟΔβ²§βÄ‰leader invariant.P21. Partition isolates a leader from a majority; the leader continues to accept client writes (it believes it still has a quorum because it counts its own votes).<br>2. The majority elects a new leader and also accepts writes.Same as P1.Leader does not step down when it loses quorum.Implement leader lease: the leader must receive periodic heartbeat acknowledgments from a majority; missing two consecutive heartbeats forces it to step down and reject new client writes.Lease expiration is a safety mechanism proven in Raft (leader steps down on loss of quorum). It prevents a splitΟΔβ²§βÄ‰brain where both sides accept writes.

2. Minimal Sound Core Specification
2.1 Essential Invariants (the core)
#InvariantReason for NecessityI1Linearizable commit ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ a log entry is committed iff it is stored on a majority of replicas in the same term.Guarantees exactlyΟΔβ²§βÄ‰once, leader safety, and snapshot correctness.I2Deterministic sideΟΔβ²§βÄ‰effect ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ every external effect is a pure function of the deterministic_context recorded in the log.Enables deterministic replay and atomic sideΟΔβ²§βÄ‰effect semantics.I3Dependency closure ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ a task may transition to READY only after all predecessor entries are committed.Enforces crossΟΔβ²§βÄ‰shard causality and prevents lost work.I4Fencing monotonicity ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ each epoch token carries a strictly increasing term; external participants reject stale terms.Prevents duplicate sideΟΔβ²§βÄ‰effects after leader change.I5Metadata quorum ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ routing tables and sharding metadata are themselves replicated via a metadata shard with the same commit rule.Guarantees autoΟΔβ²§βÄ‰sharding safety and leaderΟΔβ²§βÄ‰transfer correctness.I6Compaction safety ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ truncation may only occur after a quorum acknowledges the truncated prefix (including all dependencyΟΔβ²§βÄ‰resolution records).Preserves ability of lagging nodes to recover.I7TwoΟΔβ²§βÄ‰phase external commit ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ any sideΟΔβ²§βÄ‰effect that cannot be rolled back must be performed only after the corresponding EXEC_COMMIT is durably stored.Guarantees external sideΟΔβ²§βÄ‰effect atomicity.
All other mechanisms (e.g., bestΟΔβ²§βÄ‰effort gossip, optimistic dependencyΟΔβ²§βÄ‰resolved messages) are optimizations that can be removed without breaking the core safety proof.
2.2 StrippedΟΔβ²§βÄ‰Down State Machine

1. Submit ΟΔβÄ†βÄô TASK_SUBMIT (persisted).
2. Store dependencies ΟΔβÄ†βÄô TASK_DEPS (persisted).
3. Dependency resolution ΟΔβÄ†βÄô DEP_RESOLVED (persisted).
4. Execute ΟΔβÄ†βÄô EXEC_START (pure log entry).
5. Commit ΟΔβÄ†βÄô EXEC_COMMIT (persisted, triggers sideΟΔβ²§βÄ‰effect).
6. Snapshot ΟΔβÄ†βÄô SNAPSHOT (persisted after quorum).

No asynchronous messages, no speculative execution, no inΟΔβ²§βÄ‰memory caches that affect safety.
2.3 Proof of Minimality
If any invariant IΟΔβÄö¬¹ΟΔβ²§βÄ‰IΟΔβÄöβÄΓ is removed, a counterΟΔβ²§βÄ‰example can be constructed from the adversarial traces above. Therefore each is strictly necessary. All other components (e.g., gossipΟΔβ²§βÄ‰based failure detection, optimistic task stealing) are optional because they do not appear in the safety proof and can be layered on top without affecting the core invariants.

3. Formal Boundary Statement
AspectProvided GuaranteeNot ProvidedExactlyΟΔβ²§βÄ‰onceEvery committed task produces at most one external effect.No guarantee against client duplicate submissions; idempotent clientΟΔβ²§βÄ‰side deduplication is required.Deterministic ReplayReplay of the persisted log reproduces the exact external state.Replay does not guarantee identical wallΟΔβ²§βÄ‰clock timestamps unless explicitly recorded.CrossΟΔβ²§βÄ‰Shard CausalityAll dependency edges (including crossΟΔβ²§βÄ‰shard) are respected globally.No guarantee of realΟΔβ²§βÄ‰time ordering; only logical causality.Snapshot CorrectnessRestored state is a prefix of the globally committed log.No guarantee that a snapshot reflects all inΟΔβ²§βÄ‰flight sideΟΔβ²§βÄ‰effects that have not yet been committed.External AtomicitySideΟΔβ²§βÄ‰effects appear iff the corresponding EXEC_COMMIT is durably stored.No guarantee for external systems that do not support twoΟΔβ²§βÄ‰phase commit or idempotent writes.AutoΟΔβ²§βÄ‰ShardingSharding changes are atomic with respect to task placement.No guarantee of instant loadΟΔβ²§βÄ‰balancing; rebalancing may be delayed until a new epoch.Fencing TokensTokens are monotonic and validated by all participants.No guarantee that a malicious client cannot replay an old token if it bypasses validation.CompactionTruncation never discards information required for recovery of any committed task.No guarantee of space bound; compaction may be delayed under heavy load.Leader TransferNo committed entry is lost during a transfer.No guarantee of zero pause in client request processing.Partition BehaviorA majority partition continues to make progress; minority makes no progress (CP).No guarantee of availability under permanent minority partition.
CAP TradeΟΔβ²§βÄ‰offs

* Consistency (C) ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ linearizable commits, exactlyΟΔβ²§βÄ‰once sideΟΔβ²§βÄ‰effects.
* Partition tolerance (P) ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ system continues under network splits (majority side).
* Availability (A) ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ sacrificed in minority partitions; the system blocks writes until a quorum is reachable.

Thus the design is CP; the impossibility of achieving AP while retaining exactlyΟΔβ²§βÄ‰once semantics under the crashΟΔβ²§βÄ‰recover model is wellΟΔβ²§βÄ‰known (the FLP impossibility extended to exactlyΟΔβ²§βÄ‰once).

* dep(t) ΟΔ≈†βÄ† T be the finite predecessor set (acyclic).
* exec(t) = (s_t, e_t, rc_t, ctx_t) be the execution record stored in the WAL, where s_t and e_t are logical start/finish timestamps (monotonic counters, not wallΟΔβ²§βÄ‰clock).

Contract: For any two tasks a,

You said:
I'll conduct a hostile audit of this architecture, identifying critical gaps and transforming it into a defensible commercial specification.

HOSTILE AUDIT: CRITICAL WEAKNESSES AND CORRECTIONS
WEAKNESS 1: Cross-Shard Causality Violation
Current Design Gap
The architecture shards by hash(task_id) mod S with independent Raft groups. No mechanism ensures causal ordering across shards. A task in shard A depending on a task in shard B can execute before B's commit is visible.
Adversarial Execution Trace
T=0: Client submits task t1 to shard A, t2 to shard B
T=1: t1 has dependency on t2 (runtime injection via DEPS record)
T=2: Leader of A processes t1's DEPS, sees t2 in "PENDING" (local cache miss)
T=3: Network partition: A's leader cannot reach B's leader
T=4: A's leader assumes t2 is "stuck", promotes t1 to READY (violates S3)
T=5: t1 executes while t2 uncommitted ΟΔβÄ†βÄô causality violation

Violated Invariant
S3 (Dependency ordering): exec(t1) ΟΔβÄ†βÄô exec(t2) when t2 ΟΔΥÜΥÜ deps(t1).
Correction: Cross-Shard Dependency Protocol
Protocol: CROSS_SHARD_DEP(t, dep_shard, dep_task_id)

1. When task t in shard S_t depends on task d in shard S_d:
   a. S_t leader sends DEP_VOTE(t.id, d.id) to S_d leader
   b. S_d leader checks: if d is COMMITTED ΟΔβÄ†βÄô reply DEP_COMMIT
      else if d is PENDING/READY/RUNNING ΟΔβÄ†βÄô reply DEP_PENDING
      else (d unknown) ΟΔβÄ†βÄô reply DEP_UNKNOWN

2. S_t leader:
   - On DEP_COMMIT: clears dependency bit, proceeds
   - On DEP_PENDING: queues t, sets up WATCH on d
   - On DEP_UNKNOWN: treats as PENDING (may be race with submission)

3. WATCH mechanism: S_d leader publishes commit notifications
   to subscribers; S_t receives and triggers re-evaluation.

4. CRITICAL: S_t leader MUST NOT promote t to READY until
   DEP_COMMIT received OR S_d is partitioned away AND
   a timeout (logical, event-count based) expires with
   S_d marked FAILED in membership view.

Proof Sketch
Lemma: If t depends on d and d is in shard S_d, then t executes only after d is committed on a quorum of S_d.
Proof:

* t's promotion to READY requires DEP_COMMIT from S_d or S_d marked FAILED.
* DEP_COMMIT is sent only when d is COMMITTED on S_d quorum.
* If S_d is FAILED, by definition no quorum exists; any uncommitted d will never commit, so t remains pending (liveness violation acceptable under CAP).

Thus t executes only after d commit. ΟΔΥÜ≈Ϋ

WEAKNESS 2: Snapshot Correctness Under Concurrent Compaction
Current Design Gap
The snapshot description lacks formal guarantee that snapshot + subsequent log replay yields identical state to full replay. No mechanism prevents snapshot capture during a torn write or mid-commit.
Adversarial Execution Trace
T=0: Leader L has log indices 0..1000, commitIndex=998
T=1: L begins snapshot at index 1000 (state S1000)
T=2: Concurrently, L appends EXEC_COMMIT for task t at index 1001,
     fsync in progress (torn write possible)
T=3: Snapshot writer reads memory state including partial t commit
     (t marked COMMITTED in memory, but not yet fsynced to log)
T=4: Crash before fsync completes
T=5: Recovery: snapshot S1000' contains t as COMMITTED, but
     log replay from 1000 shows no EXEC_COMMIT for t
T=6: State divergence: t appears committed but has no commit record

Violated Invariant
S4 (Deterministic replay): Replay yields identical state.
Correction: Snapshot Isolation Protocol
Protocol: ATOMIC_SNAPSHOT

1. Leader initiates snapshot at log index I:
   a. Pause new EXEC_COMMIT appends (allow EXEC_START, SUBMIT, DEPS)
   b. Wait for all in-flight fsyncs to complete (barrier)
   c. Record current commitIndex C (C ΟΔβÄΑ¬Λ I)
   d. Serialize state machine at C (not I)
   e. Atomically write: (snapshot_data, last_included_index=C,
      last_included_term=T, checksum)
   f. Resume normal operation

2. Snapshot metadata includes:
   - last_included_index: C (highest committed index included)
   - last_included_term: term of C
   - configuration: current membership at C

3. Recovery:
   a. Load snapshot S
   b. Replay log from S.last_included_index + 1
   c. Verify that first replayed entry has term ΟΔβÄΑ¬Ξ S.last_included_term
      (detects log corruption / fork)

4. CORRECTNESS: Snapshot captures state at a committed point C.
   Any entry > C is not included; replay adds them back.
   No partial state can be captured because fsync barrier
   ensures all memory state ΟΔβÄΑ¬Λ C is durable.

Proof Sketch
Lemma: Snapshot at C plus replay of entries C+1..ΟΔΥÜ≈Ψ yields state identical to full replay of 0..ΟΔΥÜ≈Ψ.
Proof:

* By construction, snapshot captures state machine after applying exactly entries 0..C.
* Entries C+1..ΟΔΥÜ≈Ψ are applied in order during replay.
* State machine transitions are deterministic (pure functions of log entries).
* Therefore final state = apply(apply(...apply(S0, e0)...eC), eC+1...) = apply(S_snapshot, eC+1...). ΟΔΥÜ≈Ϋ


WEAKNESS 3: Fencing Token Soundness Under Clock Skew
Current Design Gap
The design mentions "epoch tokens" but does not specify how workers verify tokens without synchronized clocks. A stale leader with an old epoch could issue tokens that appear valid to a slow worker.
Adversarial Execution Trace
T=0: Leader L1 with epoch E1=5 assigns task t to worker W
T=1: Network partition isolates L1; L1 remains leader in minority
T=2: Majority partition elects L2 with epoch E2=6
T=3: L2 re-assigns t to worker W' (same task, new attempt)
T=4: W (slow, in minority partition) finally receives assignment
    from L1 (epoch 5), executes side-effects with token E1=5
T=5: Partition heals; W' executes with token E2=6
T=6: External service sees two executions with different tokens
    ΟΔβÄ†βÄô DUAL EXECUTION (exactly-once violated)

Violated Invariant
S2 (Exactly-once execution): |{e | exec_id(e)=t.id}| = 1.
Correction: Strict Epoch Validation with Monotonic Token Store
Protocol: STRICT_FENCING

1. External side-effect service maintains:
   - Map: task_id ΟΔβÄ†βÄô (max_epoch_seen, committed_flag)
   - All updates are atomic (single-row transaction or CAS)

2. Worker execution flow:
   a. Receive (task_id, epoch_e, side_effect_payload) from leader
   b. Perform deterministic computation (pure function)
   c. BEFORE external call, invoke:
      compare_and_set(task_id, expected_epoch=epoch_e, new_flag=COMMITTED)
   d. If CAS succeeds ΟΔβÄ†βÄô perform side-effect, return success
   e. If CAS fails (epoch_e < stored_epoch) ΟΔβÄ†βÄô side-effect already done
      by higher epoch; return "already committed" to leader
   f. If CAS fails (epoch_e > stored_epoch) ΟΔβÄ†βÄô IMPOSSIBLE by protocol
      (leader epochs monotonic); treat as error, abort

3. Leader behavior:
   - On receiving "already committed" for epoch_e < current_epoch:
     the task is committed; mark as SUCCESS
   - On timeout or worker crash: retry with NEW attempt_id,
     SAME epoch (epoch does not change for same leader term)
   - Only on leader election (new term) does epoch increment

4. CORRECTNESS: External service acts as the "ground truth"
   for exactly-once. The CAS operation is atomic and idempotent.
   Lower epochs are rejected; higher epochs cannot occur because
   leaders monotonically increase epochs and workers only accept
   the current leader's epoch.

Proof Sketch
Lemma: No two workers with different epochs can both commit sideΟΔβ²§βÄ‰effects for the same task.
Proof:

* External service stores max_epoch_seen.
* CAS succeeds only if expected_epoch > stored_epoch.
* Epochs are monotonic per leader term; new leader has higher epoch than old.
* Therefore if worker W1 with epoch E1 commits, any subsequent worker W2 has epoch E2 ΟΔβÄΑ¬Ξ E1.
* If E2 = E1, W2 is a retry of same leader; CAS will see stored_epoch = E1 and reject (already committed).
* If E2 > E1, CAS will reject (E1 < stored_epoch). ΟΔΥÜ≈Ϋ


WEAKNESS 4: AutoΟΔβ²§βÄ‰Sharding Safety Under Membership Churn
Current Design Gap
The design mentions "reΟΔβ²§βÄ‰sharding" for hot keys but does not specify the safety invariant for task migration. A task could be doubleΟΔβ²§βÄ‰executed if its ownership moves during execution.
Adversarial Execution Trace
T=0: Task t assigned to shard S1 (hash(t.id) mod 256 = 47)
T=1: Leader L1 of S1 appends EXEC_START, assigns to worker W1
T=2: HotΟΔβ²§βÄ‰key detection triggers reΟΔβ²§βÄ‰sharding: t moved to S2
T=3: S2's leader L2 (unaware of W1's execution) appends EXEC_START,
     assigns to worker W2
T=4: W1 and W2 both execute sideΟΔβ²§βÄ‰effects ΟΔβÄ†βÄô DUAL EXECUTION

Violated Invariant
S2 (ExactlyΟΔβ²§βÄ‰once) and S3 (Dependency ordering) if reΟΔβ²§βÄ‰sharding races with execution.
Correction: ShardΟΔβ²§βÄ‰Ownership Lease with Fencing
Protocol: SHARD_LEASE

1. Each shard S has an *ownership epoch* E_S, stored in a
   *global metadata shard* (shard 0, replicated via Raft).

2. Task t is owned by shard S only if:
   - hash(t.id) mod 256 = S, AND
   - Current ownership epoch for S is E_S, AND
   - The leader of S holds a *lease* for E_S.

3. Lease acquisition:
   a. Leader L of S proposes LEASE_ACQUIRE(S, E_S, L, timeout=T)
      to the global metadata shard.
   b. Global shard appends if no active lease for S with E_S.
   c. Lease is valid for T logical time units (event count).

4. Task execution:
   a. Before EXEC_START, worker verifies lease is still valid
      (query global shard or cached value with freshness bound).
   b. EXEC_COMMIT includes (S, E_S, lease_token).
   c. External sideΟΔβ²§βÄ‰effect service validates lease_token against
      global shard (or cached valid leases).

5. ReΟΔβ²§βÄ‰sharding:
   a. Increment global epoch for S: E_S' = E_S + 1.
   b. Old lease becomes invalid; any EXEC_COMMIT with old token
      is rejected by external service.
   c. Tasks are *drained* (wait for pending commits) before
      ownership transfer.

6. CORRECTNESS: A task can only execute under a valid lease.
   ReΟΔβ²§βÄ‰sharding invalidates the lease, preventing dual execution.
   The global metadata shard is a single point of coordination
   but is itself faultΟΔβ²§βÄ‰tolerant (Raft) and only manages leases,
   not task data (no hotΟΔβ²§βÄ‰path bottleneck).

Proof Sketch
Lemma: No task executes under two different shard ownerships.
Proof:

* Task execution requires valid lease (S, E_S).
* Lease is granted by global metadata shard G (linearizable).
* ReΟΔβ²§βÄ‰sharding increments E_S atomically in G.
* Old lease (S, E_S) becomes invalid.
* Any worker with old lease cannot commit (external service rejects).
* New lease (S, E_S+1) is required for new execution.
* Therefore at most one execution can commit per task. ΟΔΥÜ≈Ϋ


WEAKNESS 5: External SideΟΔβ²§βÄ‰Effect Atomicity Gap
Current Design Gap
The design assumes external services provide atomic CAS, but does not specify how the system behaves when external CAS succeeds but the system crashes before acknowledging, or when external service is unavailable.
Adversarial Execution Trace
T=0: Worker W executes task t, computes side-effect SE
T=1: W calls external_service.CAS(t.id, epoch=E, SE)
T=2: External service applies SE, returns SUCCESS
T=3: Network partition: W cannot receive SUCCESS
T=4: W times out, crashes
T=5: New worker W' retries t with same epoch E
T=6: W' calls CAS; external service returns "already committed"
T=7: W' reports SUCCESS to leader
T=8: Leader marks t SUCCESS

Problem: SE was applied, but system was uncertain until T=8.
If external service had failed permanently at T=2, SE would be
applied but system would never know ΟΔβÄ†βÄô liveness violation (task
stuck) or safety violation (if we mark SUCCESS without confirmation).

Violated Invariant
L1 (Eventual execution) and S2 (Exactly-once) boundary case.
Correction: External Effect Confirmation Protocol
Protocol: EXTERNAL_CONFIRM

1. Side-effect execution is split into:
   a. PREPARE: Compute effect, store in local "pending effects" table
   b. COMMIT_EXTERNAL: Idempotent apply to external service
   c. CONFIRM: External service acknowledgment durable in WAL

2. State machine extension:
   - RUNNING ΟΔβÄ†βÄô EXTERNAL_PENDING (after PREPARE)
   - EXTERNAL_PENDING ΟΔβÄ†βÄô COMMITTED (after CONFIRM)
   - EXTERNAL_PENDING ΟΔβÄ†βÄô FAILED (after timeout + max retries)

3. WAL records:
   - EFFECT_PREPARE(t, attempt, effect_hash, external_service_id)
   - EFFECT_CONFIRM(t, attempt, external_txn_id)

4. Recovery:
   - If EFFECT_PREPARE exists without EFFECT_CONFIRM:
     * Query external service with (t.id, attempt) to check status
     * If external service has record ΟΔβÄ†βÄô append EFFECT_CONFIRM
     * If not ΟΔβÄ†βÄô re-execute PREPARE (idempotent) or mark FAILED

5. External service contract:
   - Must support idempotent operations keyed by (task_id, attempt_id)
   - Must support status query: "has transaction X been applied?"
   - Must provide atomic "apply if not exists" operation

6. CORRECTNESS: The two-phase external protocol ensures that
   the system state and external state are reconciled after any crash.
   The EFFECT_CONFIRM record is the commit point for external effects.

Proof Sketch
Lemma: External effects are applied exactly once, and system state converges with external state after any crash.
Proof:

* At-most-once: External service's "apply if not exists" ensures duplicate attempts with same (task_id, attempt_id) are rejected.
* At-least-once: If EFFECT_PREPARE is persisted but EFFECT_CONFIRM is not, recovery queries external service. If applied, confirm is appended; if not, re-attempt is made.
* Convergence: The EFFECT_CONFIRM record in WAL is the source of truth. External service state may temporarily diverge, but recovery reconciles. Once EFFECT_CONFIRM is persisted, states are consistent. ΟΔΥÜ≈Ϋ


WEAKNESS 6: Leader Transfer Safety
Current Design Gap
The design mentions Raft leader transfer but doesn't specify how in-flight executions are handled during transfer. A task could be double-executed if leadership changes mid-execution.
Adversarial Execution Trace
T=0: Leader L1 is at term T=5, assigns task t to worker W1
T=1: W1 appends EXEC_START(t, attempt=1, epoch=5)
T=2: L1 initiates leader transfer to L2 (term remains 5 during transfer)
T=3: L2 becomes leader, but W1 is still executing t
T=4: L2 doesn't see EXEC_COMMIT for t, times out, re-assigns t to W2
T=5: W2 appends EXEC_START(t, attempt=2, epoch=5)  // same term!
T=6: W1 completes, appends EXEC_COMMIT(t, attempt=1)
T=7: W2 completes, appends EXEC_COMMIT(t, attempt=2)
T=8: Both commits reach quorum ΟΔβÄ†βÄô DUAL EXECUTION

Violated Invariant
S2 (Exactly-once execution)
Correction: Leader Transfer with Execution Fencing
Protocol: SAFE_LEADER_TRANSFER

1. Leader transfer sequence:
   a. Current leader L1 sets transfer_in_progress flag
   b. L1 stops assigning NEW tasks
   c. L1 broadcasts STOP_EXECUTING to all workers
   d. Workers acknowledge with IN_FLIGHT list
   e. L1 waits for all IN_FLIGHT tasks to either:
      - Complete with EXEC_COMMIT, or
      - Timeout and be marked FAILED
   f. L1 appends LEADER_TRANSFER_COMPLETE record
   g. L1 steps down; L2 can now be elected

2. New leader L2 behavior:
   a. On election, scan log for LEADER_TRANSFER_COMPLETE
   b. If found at index > last_applied, resume normal operation
   c. If not found (transfer interrupted), treat as incomplete:
      - Mark all tasks with EXEC_START but no EXEC_COMMIT as FAILED
      - Allow client retry

3. Worker behavior:
   - On receiving STOP_EXECUTING, finish current instruction, then halt
   - Reject any EXEC_START from old leader after acknowledging stop

4. CORRECTNESS: The LEADER_TRANSFER_COMPLETE record acts as a
   barrier. Any task started before the barrier must complete before
   it; any task after must start after. The atomicity of log append
   ensures no interleaving.

Proof Sketch
Lemma: No task executes under two different leaders with the same or different terms without detection.
Proof:

* Case 1: Transfer completes normally: LEADER_TRANSFER_COMPLETE is the barrier. All pre-transfer tasks committed; post-transfer tasks start fresh.
