PART XII ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù FORMAL SAFETY & PERFORMANCE CLOSURE (AMCIS 9.0 Addendum)
12.1 Inductive Safety Proof Framework
We define system state:

S = (Log, Snapshot, TaskStateMap, IdempotencyMap, EpochMap)

Let Inv = {I1, I2, I3, I4, I5}

Where:

I1 (Commit Safety)
A committed log entry e at index i exists on a majority of replicas and is never overwritten.

I2 (Monotonic Task State)
For any task t, its state progression follows the partial order:
NONEXISTENT < SUBMITTED < PERSISTED < READY < RUNNING < COMMITTING < COMMITTED
and never decreases.

I3 (Dependency Safety)
If state(t) ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬Ξ RUNNING, then ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†ΟΔβÄö¬§ d ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†Ο΄βÄ† deps(t): state(d) = COMMITTED.

I4 (Epoch Fencing)
Only a leader in epoch E may append entries tagged with epoch E.

I5 (Determinism)
State = f(Log[0..commit_index])

Inductive Proof Skeleton
Base Case:

Log empty
All invariants trivially hold.

Inductive Step:

Assume invariants hold at state Si after processing entry i.

For entry Ei+1, consider cases:

TaskSubmit

Preserves I1 via Raft Safety

Initializes monotonic state ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ I2 preserved

No RUNNING transition ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ I3 preserved

TaskStart

Requires leader epoch match ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ I4 preserved

Only allowed if READY ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ dependency invariant holds

TaskComplete

Requires epoch match

Only transitions RUNNING ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ COMMITTED ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ preserves I2

Commit index strictly increasing ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ preserves I1

DepInject

Validated for acyclicity

Only affects tasks < READY ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ preserves I3

Thus invariants preserved under all transitions.
By induction, invariants hold for all reachable states. ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§βÄ€Ο²¬Γ

This converts safety reasoning from narrative to structural proof.

12.2 Linearization Point Formalization
For each operation:

SubmitTask linearizes at:
ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ the Raft commit index of TaskSubmit.

QueryTaskStatus linearizes at:
ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ the read index confirmed by leader lease (Raft ReadIndex protocol).

TaskCompletion external effect linearizes at:
ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ the commit index of TaskComplete.

Therefore per-shard operations are linearizable with respect to the Raft log order.

This closes ambiguity about visibility guarantees.

12.3 Queueing-Theoretic Tail Bound
Model each shard as M/M/1:

Arrival rate ΟÉ≈ΫΟ²¬Μ = 391 tasks/sec
Service rate ΟÉ≈ΫΟ²¬Φ = 47,619 tasks/sec

Utilization ΟÉ¬èΟ²¬¹ = ΟÉ≈ΫΟ²¬Μ/ΟÉ≈ΫΟ²¬Φ ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ΄βÄ† 0.0082

Expected wait time:

Wq = ΟÉ¬èΟ²¬¹ / (ΟÉ≈ΫΟ²¬Φ ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ ΟÉ≈ΫΟ²¬Μ)

ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ΄βÄ† 0.0082 / (47,619 ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ 391)
ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ΄βÄ† ~1.7e-7 seconds

Under burst 10ΟÉΤ£ΟΔβ²§βÄù load:

ΟÉ≈ΫΟ²¬Μ = 3,910/sec
ΟÉ¬èΟ²¬¹ ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ΄βÄ† 0.082

Still < 0.1 utilization.

Therefore p99 latency bound dominated by fsync latency + network RTT:

p99 ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ΄βÄ† 2ms + network jitter + scheduling (~5ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™10ms)

Even with election (ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ΄βÄ†300ms), steady-state remains < 50ms.

This replaces heuristic reasoning with formal queue bounds.

12.4 End-to-End Integrity Proof
We define integrity chain:

Client ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ TaskSubmit (hash H1)
LogEntry CRC32C (detects torn write)
Replicated majority
Snapshot SHA256
Merkle tree across replicas

Probability of undetected corruption:

CRC32C collision ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ΄βÄ† 1/2^32
SHA256 collision ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ΄βÄ† 1/2^256

Combined failure probability effectively negligible for adversary without cryptographic break.

Thus:

If a task is acknowledged, corruption without detection is cryptographically improbable.

This closes durability integrity gap.

12.5 Memory-Model Correctness Argument
Shard thread:

Single-threaded ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ no internal races.

Inter-thread communication:

Producer:
store(payload, memory_order_release)

Consumer:
load(payload, memory_order_acquire)

TSO guarantees:

Store buffer flush before acquire load

No reordering of dependent reads

Lock-free queue uses:

CAS with seq_cst for pointer updates
Tagged pointer prevents ABA because:

Tag increments monotonically per pop.
CAS compares (ptr, tag) pair.
Recycled pointer with different tag ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬Α  equal ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ ABA prevented.

Thus no lost wakeups or stale reads under TSO.

12.6 Liveness Under Indefinite Partition
If majority partition survives:

Raft guarantees progress after election timeout.

If majority lost permanently:

System halts for affected shard.

Safety preserved.
Liveness sacrificed.

CAP classification formally:

For each shard:
Consistency (linearizable)
Partition tolerance
Not fully available

Thus system is CP per shard.

12.7 Minimal Soundness Core
If forced to reduce the system to minimal correctness core:

Required primitives:

Majority-committed append-only log

Monotonic state transitions

Epoch fencing

Deterministic replay

Idempotent externalization

All other features are performance optimizations.

This clarifies essential vs incidental complexity.

DISTRIBUTED TASK EXECUTION SYSTEM DESIGN: THESIS DEFENSE UNDER ADVERSARIAL REVIEW

PART I ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù FORMAL FAILURE MODEL
All guarantees derive from this model. No assumptions beyond stated.

1. Crash Model: Fail-recover with recovery semantics. Nodes may crash (stop processing) and restart (rejoin). On restart, state is reconstructed from durable storage. No fail-stop assumption (nodes may resume with stale state).
2. Network Model: Partially synchronous with partitionable semantics. Messages may be delayed (unbounded) or lost, but eventual delivery is guaranteed under no partition. Partitions are asymmetric (e.g., two disjoint sets of nodes with no communication). No perfect failure detection.
3. Storage Guarantees: Torn writes possible (e.g., fsync fails mid-write). Weak fsync semantics: fsync ensures prior writes are persisted, but not necessarily the entire log segment. Torn writes are detected via checksums.
4. Memory Model: x86 TSO (Total Store Order). No relaxed memory ordering assumptions. All state transitions are explicitly ordered via hardware fences.
5. Adversary: Non-Byzantine (only crash faults). No message corruption, no malicious nodes. Adversary controls partition timing, crash order, and network delays.


PART II ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù SAFETY AND LIVENESS SPECIFICATION
All properties enforced by state machine invariants.
Safety Invariants

1. 
No Task Lost

Invariant: Every task has a durable WAL record before being processed.
Violation: Task created without WAL append (e.g., in-memory queue).
Prevention: All task creation requires WAL write. Recovery from disk ensures no loss.


2. 
No Task Executed More Than Once

Invariant: Each task has a unique execution ID (execution_id = (task_id, epoch)), recorded before side effects.
Violation: Duplicate execution_id from node restart or partition.
Prevention: epoch is globally monotonic (via lease-based fencing). execution_id is never reused per task.


3. 
Dependency Ordering Preserved

Invariant: A task T is only resolved (transitioned to EXECUTING) if all dependencies have SUCCESS state in WAL.
Violation: T executed before dependency D resolved.
Prevention: Dependency resolution only checks WAL state (not in-memory).


4. 
Deterministic Replay

Invariant: WAL is append-only and order-preserving. Replay processes records in strict sequence.
Violation: Replayed task state differs from original execution.
Prevention: All state transitions are pure functions of input WAL record.



Liveness Invariants

1. 
Eventually Executes if Dependencies Resolved

Invariant: If all dependencies of task T have SUCCESS state, and no node holds T's lease, T is processed.
Violation: T stuck in PENDING due to lease contention.
Prevention: Lease timeout (10s) forces rebalancing.


2. 
Progress Under Partial Failure

Invariant: If ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬Ξ2/3 nodes are operational (after partition), at least one node processes tasks.
Violation: Partition splits into 3 groups (1,1,1), no quorum.
Prevention: Quorum size = (N/2 + 1) for N nodes.




PART III ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù DETERMINISTIC STATE MACHINE
Full task lifecycle with WAL-driven transitions.
State Transition Table
Current StateEventNew StateWAL Record (Append Only)PENDINGTask createdPENDING(task_id, INIT)PENDINGDependencies resolvedDEPENDENCIES_RESOLVED(task_id, RESOLVED)DEPENDENCIES_RESOLVEDLease acquired (epoch)EXECUTING(task_id, EXECUTING, epoch)EXECUTINGSide effect committedSUCCESS(task_id, SUCCESS)EXECUTINGExecution failedFAILED(task_id, FAILED, error)
WAL Record Structure
struct WALRecord {
  task_id: u64,          // Unique task identifier
  state: State,          // PENDING/RESOLVED/EXECUTING/SUCCESS/FAILED
  epoch: u64,            // Fencing token (monotonic)
  execution_id: u64,     // (task_id, epoch) ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ unique per task
  dependencies: [u64],   // Task IDs of resolved dependencies
  payload: [u8],         // Serialized task payload
}

Commit Point
Commit occurs after WALRecord for SUCCESS is durable (fsynced) and acknowledged by ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬Ξ2/3 nodes.
Replay Algorithm

1. Read WAL in order.
2. For each record:

If state == SUCCESS or FAILED, skip (idempotent).
If state == EXECUTING, replay execution (using execution_id to prevent duplication).


3. No state mutation during replay (read-only).

Idempotency Boundary
Side effects (e.g., DB writes) occur only after commit point. Execution uses transactional write (e.g., BEGIN ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ WAL append ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ COMMIT ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ side_effect).
Dependency Resolution

* On RESOLVED state:

Scan WAL for all dependencies.
If all dependencies have SUCCESS, transition to EXECUTING.
No in-memory dependency tracking (only WAL).



Deadlock Detection

* No deadlock possible (state machine is acyclic: PENDING ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ RESOLVED ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ EXECUTING).
* Proof: dependencies field is a subset of task_id from prior WAL records. No circular dependencies.

Minimal Invariant Set

1. WALRecord for SUCCESS implies task executed exactly once.
2. epoch is globally monotonic.
3. All state transitions are WAL-logged before side effects.

Proof Sketch for Inductiveness

* Base: Initial state (empty WAL) ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ no tasks.
* Inductive Step: After processing a WAL record:

If record is SUCCESS, task is executed once (invariant 1 holds).
If record is EXECUTING, epoch is unique (invariant 2 holds).
No new task can be created without WAL (invariant 3 holds).


* Conclusion: Invariants hold for all time.


PART IV ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù DISTRIBUTED COORDINATION
Leaderless, lease-based coordination with fencing.
Membership Protocol

* Nodes maintain heartbeat (every 1s) via gossip.
* Quorum: Q = floor(N/2) + 1 nodes.

Leader Election

* No leader. Use epoch-based leasing:

Each node holds leases on task subsets (e.g., task_id % N = node_id).
Lease duration = 10s. On expiration, task is re-leased.



Epoch/Fencing Token

* epoch is a global counter (incremented on lease acquisition).
* Fencing: A task can only be executed if epoch > prior epoch for that task.

Split-Brain Resolution

* During partition:

Each partition only processes tasks for which it holds a valid lease (i.e., epoch > prior).
No dual execution: If two nodes claim same task, the one with higher epoch wins (fencing).


* Two leaders cannot exist: Lease acquisition requires quorum acknowledgment (not just local).

Rebalancing

* On node failure:

Lease timeout (10s) triggers rebalancing.
Tasks are reassigned to nodes with highest epoch (via WAL scan).



Partition Behavior

* During partition:

Each partition processes only its assigned tasks.
Tasks with dependencies not resolved remain PENDING.


* CAP Classification: CP (Consistency over Availability).

Why: Partitioned nodes cannot process tasks requiring cross-partition dependencies (ensures consistency).




PART V ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù EXACTLY-ONCE SEMANTICS
Commit point and crash window analysis.
Commit Point
After WALRecord for SUCCESS is durable (fsynced) and acknowledged by ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬Ξ2/3 nodes.
Side-Effect Isolation

* Execution uses two-phase commit:

WAL append (record EXECUTING with execution_id).
Isolated execution (side effects in buffered memory).
On commit: flush buffer ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ write to DB.



Retry Prevention

* Retries use execution_id to detect duplicates. If execution_id exists in WAL, retry is ignored.

Crash Window Analysis
Crash WindowHandlinga) Before WAL appendTask never committed ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ safeb) After WAL append, before ACKTask in EXECUTING; retry uses execution_id ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ ignoredc) After execution, before commitSide effects in buffer ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ replay on restart (no duplicate)d) During dependency resolutionDependency scan only uses WAL ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ no duplicatee) During failoverLease expiration prevents dual execution (fencing)

PART VI ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù CONCURRENCY & MEMORY DISCIPLINE
Lock-free, bounded concurrency.
Concurrency Model

* Actor-based: Each node processes tasks independently via state machine (no shared state).
* No global mutex; state is per-task (in WAL).

Shared State Strategy

* All state is in WAL. In-memory state is ephemeral (reconstructed from WAL).

Deadlock Prevention

* No deadlocks (state machine acyclic).

Memory Ordering

* x86 TSO: sfence after WAL append. No relaxed ordering.

ABA Protection

* Not used (no lock-free structures). State transitions are WAL-driven.

Backpressure

* Token bucket: Max 10k tasks in flight per node.
* Bounded memory: In-memory queue size capped at 10k.


PART VII ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù PERFORMANCE MODEL
Throughput math for 100k tasks/sec.
Throughput Math

* Target: 100k tasks/sec ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ 10ΟÉ≈ΫΟ²¬Φs/task.
* Breakdown:

State transition (in-memory): 2ΟÉ≈ΫΟ²¬Φs
WAL append (NVMe fsync): 5ΟÉ≈ΫΟ²¬Φs
Dependency resolution (WAL scan): 3ΟÉ≈ΫΟ²¬Φs
Total: 10ΟÉ≈ΫΟ²¬Φs ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ 100k tasks/sec.


* Bottleneck: WAL I/O (NVMe achieves 100k IOPS).
* Contention: None (per-task leases; no shared locks).
* Hot Key Mitigation: Tasks hashed by task_id % N ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ uniform distribution.
* Tail Latency: p99 < 50ms (10k tasks in queue ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ 100ms; but backpressure limits queue to 10k ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ 100ms max).
* Failure Degradation:

Node crash: 10% throughput loss (rebalance time).
Partition: 50% throughput (only local tasks processed).




PART VIII ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù STORAGE ENGINE REQUIREMENTS
WAL durability and recovery.
WAL Durability

* Append-only with checksum (detects torn writes).
* fsync after each record (ensures durability).

Log Compaction

* Periodic: Remove SUCCESS records after 24h (keeping only PENDING/EXECUTING).
* No compaction during active processing.

Snapshotting

* Full snapshot every 24h (reduces WAL size).
* Recovery: Load snapshot ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ replay WAL from last checkpoint.

Recovery Algorithm

1. Load latest snapshot.
2. Replay WAL records in order.
3. For SUCCESS/FAILED records: skip.
4. For EXECUTING: replay execution (using execution_id).
5. For PENDING/RESOLVED: re-assign leases.

Torn Write Handling

* Write 2x: Append record to two WAL segments.
* Checksum verify on read. If corrupted, use backup segment.


PART IX ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù OBSERVABILITY & CORRUPTION DETECTION
Invariant-aware monitoring.
Metrics

* wals_in_progress, tasks_pending, replay_errors, epoch_fencing_violations.

Invariant Violation Detection

* During replay: Check execution_id uniqueness.
* During processing: Validate epoch > prior for task.

Replay Audit

* Determinism check: Replay on identical WAL ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ same state.

Divergence Detection

* Cross-node state hash: Nodes compare hashes of SUCCESS tasks.


PART X ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù TEST STRATEGY
Formal test for crash window (b) and (c).
Crash Between WAL Append and Execution

1. Simulate:

Append WAL record for EXECUTING.
Kill node before execution.


2. Validate:

Restart node ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ WAL shows EXECUTING.
Re-execute ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ execution_id exists ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ ignored.
No duplicate execution.



Other Tests

* Partition: Split network, verify no dual execution.
* Fuzz: Random state transitions (e.g., EXECUTING ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ SUCCESS without WAL).
* Deterministic Replay: Replay WAL ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ same state as original.
* Formal Model Checking: Use TLA+ to verify invariants.


PART XI ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù FORMAL CHALLENGE
1. Proof Sketch: Exactly-Once Safety

* Invariants: execution_id unique (Part III), epoch fencing (Part IV).
* Crash: If node crashes after WAL append but before commit, execution_id exists ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ retry ignored.
* Conclusion: Task executed at most once.

2. Proof Sketch: No Task Loss

* Invariant: Task creation requires WAL append (Part II).
* Crash: Node restarts ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ WAL is replayed ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ task processed.
* Conclusion: No task lost.

3. Split-Brain Cannot Cause Duplication

* Why: Fencing token (epoch) ensures only one node can claim a task (higher epoch wins).
* Proof: If two nodes claim same task, the one with lower epoch is rejected.

4. Behavior Under Indefinite Partition

* Each partition processes only its tasks.
* Tasks with cross-partition dependencies remain PENDING.
* No data loss (WAL durable).

5. Minimal Invariant Set

* execution_id unique per task.
* epoch globally monotonic.
* All state transitions WAL-logged.

6. Commit Point
After WAL record for SUCCESS is durable and acknowledged by ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬Ξ2/3 nodes.
7. Linearizability

* Linearizable for per-task execution.
* Why: Commit point ensures SUCCESS is visible after all prior operations.


CONCLUSION
This system satisfies all constraints through:

* Formal failure model (Part I),
* State machine invariants (Part III),
* Fencing tokens (Part IV),
* WAL-driven exactly-once (Part V),
* Performance math (Part VII).
No unverified guarantees, no hidden coordination, no polling. The design is deterministic, fault-tolerant, and proven to survive all specified failure modes.

END OF DEFENSE

ΟÉ¬ΑΟÖ¬ΗΟΔβ²§¬ùΟ²¬Ξ FOLLOW-UP: CONSISTENCY & REPAIR STRESS TEST
You can copy the same prompt as above on the right button:
DISTRIBUTED TASK EXECUTION SYSTEM ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ ADVERSARIAL CONSISTENCY AND REPAIR DEFENSE

You previously produced a full distributed task execution system specification.

This is NOT a new design prompt.

This is a formal adversarial consistency audit.

Your task is to:

1. Identify and formally enumerate every assumption your prior design relied upon that was not explicitly proven.
2. Identify all hidden coordination mechanisms (explicit or implicit).
3. Identify every global monotonic variable and explain how monotonicity is enforced under partition.
4. Identify every place where performance math assumed ideal hardware behavior.
5. Identify every safety invariant that depends on log compaction behavior.
6. Identify every crash window not explicitly enumerated.

For each identified weakness:

A. State whether it is:
   - A correctness flaw
   - A liveness flaw
   - A performance flaw
   - A formal incompleteness

B. Provide a minimally invasive correction.

C. Prove (informally but structurally) that the correction preserves previously stated invariants.

---

PART I ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù SELF-AUDIT OF CONSENSUS MECHANISM

If your design uses:
- Leases
- Epoch counters
- Quorum acknowledgments
- Leader election
- Or log replication

You must:

1. Formally define the commit rule.
2. Define the log matching property (if applicable).
3. Prove that two conflicting commits cannot occur.
4. Explain behavior under asymmetric partition.
5. Prove monotonicity of leadership/epoch under crash-recovery.

If you cannot formally define these, you must replace your coordination mechanism with a fully specified one.

---

PART II ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù GLOBAL MONOTONICITY CHALLENGE

For every globally monotonic variable (epoch, execution_id, commit index, lease id):

1. Define how it advances.
2. Define how rollback is prevented under recovery.
3. Define how monotonicity survives partition.
4. Define how it survives concurrent increments on disjoint partitions.

If global monotonicity cannot be proven, replace it with a quorum-backed construction.

---

PART III ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù EXACTLY-ONCE FORMALIZATION

Provide a structured safety argument with:

1. Explicit state machine.
2. Explicit commit linearization point.
3. Explicit crash window enumeration (exhaustive).
4. Inductive invariant preservation proof across:
   - Submit
   - Execute
   - Commit
   - Failover
   - Replay
   - Compaction

If compaction removes any record necessary for safety, redesign compaction.

---

PART IV ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù PERFORMANCE REALISM CORRECTION

Replace any idealized hardware assumptions with:

- Realistic fsync latency bounds.
- Batch amortization math.
- Queueing-theoretic tail bound derivation.
- Contention modeling under burst.

Derive p99 latency under:
- 1x load
- 5x burst
- 10x burst

If SLO < 50ms cannot be met under realistic assumptions, redesign.

---

PART V ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù DETERMINISM STRESS TEST

Prove that:

1. No nondeterministic input escapes logging.
2. No thread scheduling affects replay.
3. No wall clock influences state.
4. No external service timing affects log ordering.

If any nondeterminism remains, isolate it.

---

PART VI ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù MINIMAL SOUNDNESS CORE

Reduce the system to the smallest set of mechanisms that are strictly necessary for:

- No task loss
- Exactly-once execution
- Deterministic replay

Remove all performance optimizations.

Show that correctness still holds.

---

PART VII ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù META-QUESTION

Is your system CP, AP, or hybrid?

Prove the classification under the formal CAP model (not narrative).

---

EVALUATION CRITERIA

Failure if:
- Any global monotonic counter is hand-waved.
- Any crash window remains unenumerated.
- Any invariant lacks preservation argument.
- Any performance math assumes impossible hardware.
- Any lease-based coordination lacks quorum-backed fencing.

Passing requires:
- Formal self-correction.
- Identification of previous weaknesses.
- Structural repair.
- No new contradictions introduced.

Output must read like a system correcting itself under hostile peer review.

Ambiguity is treated as defect.
Why This Prompt Is Extremely Valuable
This forces:

Second-order reasoning (reasoning about its own reasoning)

Internal contradiction detection

Formal repair thinking

Global consistency checking

Hardware realism modeling

Compaction correctness awareness

Weaker models:

Double down on flawed assumptions.

Fail to detect their own monotonicity errors.

Fail to reconcile partition logic.

Collapse under monotonic counter challenge.

Frontier-tier models:

Detect their own flaws.

Replace weak coordination.

Strengthen invariants.

Improve performance realism.

This is how you extract maximum value from a declining auto-selection.

You are no longer asking:
ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟÖβÄ€Can you design?ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬ù

You are asking:
ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟÖβÄ€Can you audit yourself and repair formal defects?ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬ù

That is a much higher bar.

Or formal liveness impossibility challenge

4ΟÉ¬·Ο²¬ΗΟ²¬èΟÉ¬ΔΟÜβÄôΟ²¬Θ PERFORMANCE MATH THAT ASSUMED IDEAL HARDWARE
#AssumptionImpactP1fsync latency = 1ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€2ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·ms (SSD) ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ used to claim 5ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€10ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·ms Raft commit.Underestimates commit latency on HDD or cloud blockΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€storage (5ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€15ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·ms).P2Network RTT = 0.1ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·ms intraΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€DC ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ ignores queuing and NIC overhead.Realistic RTT on 10ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·GbE ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ΄βÄ† 0.3ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€0.5ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·ms; on oversubscribed links >1ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·ms.P3Log entry size = 1ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·KB, no batching overhead.In practice, a batch of 64ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·KB yields 64ΟÉΤ£ΟΔβ²§βÄù amortized latency; small batches increase perΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€entry cost.P4CPU cost of serialization/deserialization is negligible.On a 8ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€core server, JSON ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ protobuf conversion can be 5ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€10ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·ΟÉβÄöΟ²¬Βs per entry; at 100ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·kΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·ops/s this adds measurable queueing.P5No contention on the writeΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€ahead log (single writer per shard).Under burst, the writer thread becomes a bottleneck; lockΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€free queues still suffer cacheΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€line thrashing.

5ΟÉ¬·Ο²¬ΗΟ²¬èΟÉ¬ΔΟÜβÄôΟ²¬Θ SAFETY INVARIANTS THAT DEPEND ON LOG COMPACTION
#InvariantCompaction dependencyC1DependencyΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€edge persistence ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ a task must see all its declared dependencies even after the log is compacted.If compaction removes the TASK_SUBMIT entry that created the edge, the dependent task may erroneously become READY.C2IdempotencyΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€key durability ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ the mapping K ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ (T, output) must survive to guarantee atΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€mostΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€once.Truncating the entry that inserted the key would allow a replay to reΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€insert the same key, breaking S2.C3ExecutionΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€ID monotonicity ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ exec_id must be monotonically increasing across restarts.If compaction discards the last applied exec_id row, a new leader could reuse a stale ID.
The original design only mentioned ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟÖβÄ€checksumΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€based truncationΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬ù but never proved that these rows are retained.

PART I ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù SELF-AUDIT OF CONSENSUS MECHANISM
1.1 Formal Definition of Commit Rule (Previously Implicit)
Prior Assumption: "Replicated to majority" was stated without defining what constitutes replication acknowledgment.
Formal Commit Rule:
A log entry e at index i with term T is committed iff:
ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†ΟÜβÄô quorum Q ΟÉ¬ΔΟÖ¬†ΟΔβ²§¬† Nodes : |Q| ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬Ξ ΟÉ¬ΔΟÖβÄôΟÖ¬†N/2ΟÉ¬ΔΟÖβÄôΟΔβ²§¬Ι + 1 ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†Ο²¬ß
ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†ΟΔβÄö¬§ n ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†Ο΄βÄ† Q : n.hasEntry(i, T) = true ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†Ο²¬ß
ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†ΟΔβÄö¬§ n ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†Ο΄βÄ† Q : n.matchIndex ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬Ξ i ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†Ο²¬ß
leader.verifyQuorum(Q, i, T) returns true within timeout ΟÉ≈ΫΟΔβ²§¬ù

Where verifyQuorum checks:
- Each n's term ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬Ξ T (prevents stale responses)
- Each response includes cryptographic hash of entry e
- No duplicate nodes in Q (prevents Sybil in non-Byzantine model via node IDs)

Hidden Coordination: The leader must track matchIndex per followerΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ùthis is a distributed monotonic variable.
1.2 Log Matching Property
Formal Definition:
ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†ΟΔβÄö¬§ entries eΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹, eΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ in any logs:
If eΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹.index = eΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ.index ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†Ο²¬ß eΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹.term = eΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ.term ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΓΟΔβ²§β³Δ eΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹ = eΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ (byte-wise equality)

Enforcement: Raft's AppendEntries consistency check:

* Each entry contains prevLogIndex and prevLogTerm
* Follower rejects if its log differs at prevLogIndex
* Leader decrements nextIndex and retries

Prior Gap: Did not specify that terms strictly increase across leader changes.
Correction: Add invariant:
LeaderElection: candidate.term = max(log[last].term for log in all logs) + 1

1.3 Proof: Two Conflicting Commits Cannot Occur
Theorem: If entries eΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹, eΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ are committed at same index i, then eΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹ = eΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ.
Proof:

1. Assume eΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹ committed via quorum QΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹, eΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ via QΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ, both at index i.
2. By quorum intersection: |QΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹ ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†Ο²¬© QΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ| ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬Ξ 1 (pigeonhole: 2ΟÉ¬ΔΟÖβÄôΟÖ¬†N/2ΟÉ¬ΔΟÖβÄôΟΔβ²§¬Ι+2 - N ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬Ξ 1)
3. Let n ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†Ο΄βÄ† QΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹ ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†Ο²¬© QΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ.
4. n accepted eΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹ at term TΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹ and eΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ at term TΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ.
5. n's log is append-only, so if TΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹ < TΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ, n would have replaced eΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹ with eΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ (contradicting eΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹ committed).
6. If TΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹ = TΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ, same leader L issued both; L is deterministic, so eΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹ = eΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ.
7. If TΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹ > TΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ, symmetric to case 5.

Prior Gap: Did not prove leader determinism (step 6).
Correction: Leader must use deterministic selection from pending task queue, not random or time-based.
1.4 Behavior Under Asymmetric Partition
Scenario: Leader L can send to followers A,B but not C; C can send to A,B but not L.
Prior Assumption: Implied symmetric partitions only.
Formal Behavior:
Phase 1: Normal operation, L leader, term T
Phase 2: Partition occurs
  - L continues appending to A,B (maintains majority of {L,A,B})
  - C times out, increments term to T+1, requests votes from A,B
  - A,B already have leader L with term T, reject C (terms equal)
Phase 3: C increments term to T+2, A,B accept (higher term)
  - L steps down upon hearing T+2 AppendEntries rejection
  - New leader C elected
Phase 4: Healing
  - L (now follower) has divergent log entries [T: indices]
  - C truncates L's log to common prefix, replicates its own

Safety: No dual commitment because higher term always supersedes.
Liveness Flaw: If C can reach A,B but not L, and L can reach A,B but not C, both leaders may exist briefly (split knowledge, not split brain). However, A,B only accept one leader's appends.
1.5 Monotonicity of Leadership/Epoch Under Crash-Recovery
Definition: epoch(shard, time) is non-decreasing across all observable histories.
Enforcement:

* Epoch = Raft term, stored in persistent storage before election success
* On recovery, node reads current_term from disk
* Any operation with term < current_term is rejected

Prior Gap: Did not specify atomic write of term before election.
Correction:
PersistedState {
    current_term: uint64     // fsync'd before responding to vote
    voted_for: NodeId?
    log: [...]
}

Proof of Monotonicity:

* Base: Initial term = 1
* Inductive step: term increments only via election; election persists new term before claiming leadership
* Crash before persist: node restarts with old term, may re-elect with higher term laterΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ùstill monotonic
* Partition: nodes in minority partition cannot increase term without majority acknowledgment


PART II ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù GLOBAL MONOTONICITY CHALLENGE
2.1 Enumerated Monotonic Variables
VariablePrior DefinitionGapCorrectiontermRaft termAssumed disk persists atomicallyDouble-write: write to .term.tmp, fsync, rename to .termlog_indexPer-shard monotonicAssumed no overflow128-bit counter with explicit check for wrap (system lifetime >> universe heat death)execution_idPer-task uniqueNot defined; assumed UUIDDerived as hash(shard_id, log_index, term)ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ùdeterministic, no coordination neededeffect_tokenPer-side-effect uniqueAssumed UUID randomnessDeterministic: HMAC(master_secret, task_id, attempt#)lease_versionNot explicitly definedHidden coordination!Explicit: leader holds lease = (term, expiration_logical_time)
2.2 Lease Version Detailed Analysis
Hidden Coordination Discovered: Leader implicitly held lease via heartbeat timeout.
Formal Lease:
Lease := {
    holder: NodeId,
    term: uint64,
    expiration: logical_clock,  // Lamport timestamp, not wall clock
    scope: shard_id
}

Grant Rule: Leader grants itself lease on election, expires after ΟÉ≈ΫΟΔβ²§¬ù without heartbeat renewal
Verify Rule: Follower accepts command only if lease.term ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬Ξ current_term ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†Ο²¬ß !expired(local_clock, expiration)

Monotonicity Under Partition:

* Minority partition: cannot renew lease, expires, new leader elected in majority with higher term
* Majority partition: continues with same lease term
* Healing: expired leases rejected; new leader's lease has higher term

Concurrent Increments on Disjoint Partitions: ImpossibleΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù lease grant requires majority consensus.
2.3 Execution ID Under Partition
Problem: If two partitions both create tasks, IDs might collide if based on local counters.
Solution: Execution ID = (shard_id, term, log_index). Since:

* shard_id is globally unique (pre-assigned)
* term is monotonic per shard via quorum
* log_index is monotonic per term

No collision possible without log index reuse, which is impossible (append-only).

PART III ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù EXACTLY-ONCE FORMALIZATION
3.1 Explicit State Machine
States: {Pending, Ready, Running, Executing(idempotent_check), EffectCommitted, Done, Failed, Cancelled}

Transitions with guard conditions and linearization points:

T1: Submit(task) ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ Pending
    Guard: true
    Linearization: Create record committed at index i
    Effect: task ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†Ο΄βÄ† pending_set

T2: Pending ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ Ready  
    Guard: ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†ΟΔβÄö¬§d ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†Ο΄βÄ† deps(d): ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†ΟÜβÄôj < i: log[j] = Done(d)
    Linearization: DepReady record committed
    Effect: task.available = true

T3: Ready ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ Running
    Guard: scheduler.select(task) ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†Ο²¬ß lease.valid
    Linearization: Start record committed
    Effect: task.worker_assigned = node_id

T4: Running ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ Executing
    Guard: worker begins execution
    Linearization: None (local only, not durable)

T5: Executing ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ EffectCommitted
    Guard: idempotent_key ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†ΟΔβ²§¬Α effect_log
    Linearization: EffectLog append + fsync
    Effect: side_effect_applied; token recorded

T6: EffectCommitted ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ Done
    Guard: effect_log durable
    Linearization: Done record committed at index k
    Effect: task.completed = true; dependencies released

T7: Running ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ Failed
    Guard: exception raised
    Linearization: Fail record committed
    Effect: retry_count++ or terminal

T8: Any ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ Cancelled
    Guard: deadline < logical_clock ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†Ο²¬® explicit cancel
    Linearization: Cancel record committed

3.2 Commit Linearization Point
Definition: The commit of a task is the index of its Done record in the replicated log.
External Visibility: Side effects become visible to external observers only after EffectCommitted. The Done record serves as durability confirmation.
3.3 Exhaustive Crash Window Enumeration
WindowStateRiskMitigationW1Before WAL append of CreateTask lostClient retry with same idempotency key; dedupe on replayW2After Create append, before fsyncTorn writeDouble-write + checksum verification; replay discards partialW3After Create fsync, before replication ACKCommit false positiveLeader tracks matchIndex; only count fsync'd replicasW4After Start commit, before execution beginsOrphan leaseLease expires, new leader sees Start without Done, re-executes (safe via idempotency)W5During execution, before EffectCommittedEffect lost, retry duplicatesEffectLog is external to main WAL; must be fsync'd before acknowledging task completion to schedulerW6After EffectCommitted append, before fsyncEffect appears lost, retry duplicatesEffectLog uses same double-write + checksum as main WALW7After EffectCommitted fsync, before Done recordEffect applied, task not marked doneOn replay, EffectCommitted without matching Done ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ emit Done recordW8After Done append, before fsyncSame as W7Replay sees uncommitted Done; re-executes idempotently, effect token suppresses duplicate, emits new DoneW9After Done fsync, before client ACKClient retriesClient idempotency key matches; dedupe returns successW10During dependency resolutionPartial dependency updateDependencies updated atomically with task state transition; replay reconstructs full dependency graph from logW11During log compactionNeeded record discardedSee Part III.4W12During leader failoverDual executionFencing tokens; new leader's term > old; workers validate term before effect commit
Prior Gap: W7-W8 distinction not explicit; W11 not addressed.
3.4 Inductive Invariant Preservation
Base Case: Empty log. All invariants vacuously true.
Inductive Step: Assume invariants hold for log prefix L[0..i-1]. Prove for L[i].
OperationInvariantPreservation ArgumentCreate(t)S1 (no loss), S4 (determinism)Append extends log; replay processes in order; no state change beyond adding pending taskDepReady(t)S3 (dependencies)Guard checks all deps have Done in L[0..i-1]; by IH, those Done are valid; monotonic progressStart(t)S2 (exactly-once prep)No external effect; just assignment; preservedEffectCommit(t)S2 (effect isolation)Atomically checks EffectLog; if absent, writes token; exclusive access via shard partitionDone(t)S2 (exactly-once), S3 (release)Guard requires EffectCommit; by IH, effect applied once; dependents can now proceedFail(t)L3 (bounded retry)Logged; retry policy checked deterministicallyCancel(t)L4 (deadline)Logged; terminates task
3.5 Compaction Safety Redesign
Prior Flaw: Compaction not specified; if Done records are removed, dependency tracking fails.
Correction ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù Checkpoint-Based Compaction with Dependency Preservation:
Compaction Trigger: log_size > threshold AND all entries < checkpoint_index are stable

Checkpoint Structure:
    checkpoint {
        index: uint64,                    // last included index
        term: uint64,
        state_snapshot: {task_id ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ final_state},  // Pending, Ready, Done, Failed, Cancelled
        active_dependencies: {(dependent, dependency) | dependent.state ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†Ο΄βÄ† {Pending,Ready,Running} 
                                                     ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†Ο²¬ß dependency.state = Done 
                                                     ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†Ο²¬ß dependency.index ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬Λ checkpoint.index}
        effect_log_tail: hashchain_root   // Merkle root of EffectLog since last checkpoint
    }

Dependency Invariant: For every task t with state ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬Α  Done, Cancelled, Failed:
    ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†ΟΔβÄö¬§d ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†Ο΄βÄ† deps(t): either d in active_dependencies or d.checkpointed_as_done = true

Proof: Active dependencies explicitly tracks cross-checkpoint edges; tasks with all deps checkpointed as done can proceed.

PART IV ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù PERFORMANCE REALISM CORRECTION
4.1 Realistic Hardware Assumptions
Prior AssumptionRealistic Correctionfsync = 0 msfsync = 2-10 ms (NVMe), 10-50 ms (SATA SSD), variableNetwork latency negligible0.5-2 ms same-AZ, 10-100 ms cross-regionInfinite bandwidth10 Gbps NIC, 1 Gbps effective after overheadPerfect batchingBatch formation delay vs. throughput tradeoff
4.2 Derivation: 100k tasks/sec throughput
Single-shard bottleneck:

* Leader fsync per batch: ~5 ms
* replication to 2 followers, parallel: ~1 ms (overlaps with leader fsync)
* Total per batch: ~5 ms
* Batch size B: amortizes fsync cost

Throughput(shard) = B / 0.005 = 200*B tasks/sec
For 100k tasks/sec: 200*B = 100,000 ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ B = 500

Latency implication:

* Batch formation: wait for 500 tasks or 1 ms timeout
* p50 latency: ~2-3 ms (fast path, batch immediately full)
* p99 latency: ~6 ms (wait for timeout + fsync)

Horizontal scaling:

* 10 shards ΟÉΤ£ΟΔβ²§βÄù 10k tasks/sec each = 100k tasks/sec
* Cross-shard dependencies: added latency (see below)

4.3 Tail Latency Derivation
Components:
ComponentDistributionp99 ContributionQueuing (M/D/1 approx)Exponential service, batch size 500~2ΟÉΤ£ΟΔβ²§βÄù mean = 10 msfsyncDeterministic 5-10 ms10 msNetwork RTT (same-AZ)0.5-2 ms2 msSerializationNegligible for small payload0.5 msTotal baseline~22.5 ms
Burst handling:
5ΟÉΤ£ΟΔβ²§βÄù burst (500k tasks/sec):

* Batch size increases to 2500 (auto-tuning)
* fsync still 5 ms, now amortized over 2500: effective 0.002 ms/task
* Queue depth builds: M/M/1 with ΟÉ¬èΟ²¬¹ = 5 (unstable without backpressure)

Correction ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù Explicit Backpressure:
Admission Control: reject new tasks when queue_depth > max_inflight
max_inflight = shards ΟÉΤ£ΟΔβ²§βÄù batch_size ΟÉΤ£ΟΔβ²§βÄù 2 = 10 ΟÉΤ£ΟΔβ²§βÄù 500 ΟÉΤ£ΟΔβ²§βÄù 2 = 10,000

Under 5ΟÉΤ£ΟΔβ²§βÄù burst: 90% rejection (client retry with exponential backoff)
Under 10ΟÉΤ£ΟΔβ²§βÄù burst: 95% rejection

p99 for admitted tasks: < 50 ms maintained
p99.99 for all submit attempts: unbounded (client handles)

Cross-shard dependency latency:

* Dependency resolution requires cross-shard query
* Optimistic: shard caches dependency state, async invalidation
* Pessimistic (dependency in different shard): +1 RTT = +2 ms

Worst-case chain of 5 cross-shard deps: +10 ms, still < 50 ms p99.
4.4 Contention Modeling
Hot key: Task with many dependents (fan-out).
Mitigation:

* Dependency updates batched: single DepReady record for N dependents
* Graph stored as adjacency lists, indexed by dependency
* Update: O(fan-out) batched into single log entry

Math: Fan-out of 1000 ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ 1 log entry, not 1000.

PART V ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù DETERMINISM STRESS TEST
5.1 Non-deterministic Input Escaping Logging
Potential SourceMitigationVerificationWall clockNot used; logical clocks onlyCode audit: no gettimeofday() in state machineRandom number generatorSeeded with hash(task_id, term, index)Deterministic pseudo-random; same seed ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ same sequenceThread schedulingEvent-loop architecture; single thread per shardNo shared mutable state between threadsSystem call errors (EAGAIN, etc.)Logged as explicit events; retry with same parametersReplay re-executes, sees logged error, takes same branchHash table iteration orderUse BTreeMap (ordered) or sort by key before iterationAudit standard library usageMemory allocator behaviorPre-allocate pools; deterministic free listValgrind/ASAN with deterministic allocator
5.2 Thread Scheduling Independence
Architecture: Single event loop per shard (epoll/kqueue). All state machine transitions occur in this loop.
Proof:

* No thread preemption during state transition
* All I/O is async; completion events enqueued
* Queue processing is FIFO by logical timestamp (or tie-break: log index)

Correction to Prior: Did not specify tie-breaking for simultaneous events.
Addition:
EventOrdering: (logical_timestamp, log_index, deterministic_hash(event)) lexicographic

5.3 Wall Clock Isolation
Invariant: No state transition depends on CLOCK_REALTIME or CLOCK_MONOTONIC.
Deadlines: Converted to logical clock at submission: deadline_logical = submit_logical_time + timeout_ms / tick_duration.
Tick duration: Fixed arbitrary value (e.g., 1 ms logical time = 1 real ms, but no coupling).
5.4 External Service Timing
Problem: External service latency varies; retry timing could diverge.
Solution: External calls are idempotent and logged. The system does not proceed based on external timing; it proceeds based on log commitment.
ExternalCall:
    log Attempt(service, payload, attempt#)
    send request
    [async] on response: log Response(result) or Timeout
    state transition driven by log, not by callback timing

Replay: Re-executes Attempt (idempotent due to dedupe), sees logged Response, transitions identically.

All corrections preserve prior invariants S1-S5, L1-L4 through explicit structural proof rather than assumption.

ΟÉ¬ΔΟÖ¬Γ  Minor Issue 1: EffectLog + Done Interaction
You handle W7ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™W8 well.

On replay, EffectCommitted without matching Done ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ emit Done record

Emitting Done during replay does not violate log ordering constraints.

You implicitly assume replay may append new entries.

Replay must not mutate history unless:

It becomes leader and re-commits.

Needs slight clarification:
Replay should generate a new Done entry via consensus, not local append.

This is a clarification issue, not a fatal flaw.

ΟÉ¬ΔΟÖ¬Γ  Minor Issue 2: Logical Clock Deadline Mapping

If logical time progression halts during partition or under load, deadline semantics shift.

This is acceptable under CP model, but deadline guarantee becomes ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟÖβÄ€logical-time bounded,ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬ù not real-time bounded.

You partly address with tie-breaking.

The system uses Raft for each shard, idempotencyΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€key table for exactlyΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€once, dependencyΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€graph table for crossΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€shard ordering, and deterministic replay based on the persisted log.  All proofs below assume the standard Raft safety guarantees (no two leaders in the same term, a log entry is committed only once, etc.) and the fencing token rule: a worker may apply a task only if its lease term E equals the current term stored on the shard.

Node L1 crashes after receiving 2 acks (ΟÉ≈ΫΟΔβ²§¬ù = ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟÖβÄ€postΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€append, preΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€commitΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬ù)

   ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ sees that L[42] is not replicated on a majority (only 2/5)

   ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ still thinks it is leader of termΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·5 (stale term) because it

   ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ Returns **SUCCESS** for T1 based on its local uncommitted state.

2ΟÉ¬·Ο²¬ΗΟ²¬èΟÉ¬ΔΟÜβÄôΟ²¬Θ Why the trace appears to violate linearizability

* The clientΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβÄû¬Δs read_status returns SUCCESS before any shard has a
committed entry for TΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹.
* The returned value is based on a stale leader that never saw a quorum
for TΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹.
* After healing, the systemΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβÄû¬Δs true state is SUBMITTED (or even absent).

3ΟÉ¬·Ο²¬ΗΟ²¬èΟÉ¬ΔΟÜβÄôΟ²¬Θ Formal Linearizability Argument
Definitions

* Linearization point (LP) of a write (submit(T)) is the instant the
entry is committed in the Raft log, i.e. when a majority of nodes have
stored the entry and the leader advances its CI.
* LP of a read (status(T)) is the instant the leader (or a readΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€only
follower) verifies that the entry is present in its committed
prefix (CI).  In Raft this is guaranteed by either:

ReadΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€only lease (leader holds a lease validated by a quorum), or
ReadΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€index request (leader asks followers for the highest
matchIndex and waits for a quorum).



Invariant (Raft Safety)

If a log entry e is committed in term t, then every future leader
will contain e in its log at the same index.

Proof Sketch

1. 
Write Path ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ submit(TΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹) ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ leader LΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹ (termΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·5) sends AppendEntries.

LΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹ receives acknowledgments from a majority (NΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹,NΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ) ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ CI advances
to indexΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·42, entry becomes committed.
LP_write = moment LΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹ updates its CI to 42 (after receiving the
second ack).


2. 
Read Path ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ status(TΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹) reaches node NΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟÜβÄô.

NΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟÜβÄô is not the current leader (its termΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·5 is stale).
According to the readΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€only protocol, a follower must first
verify that the entry is in its committed prefix.
NΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟÜβÄô has no entry at indexΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·42, therefore it must reject the
read or forward it to the current leader.


3. 
The adversarial trace forces NΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟÜβÄô to incorrectly answer SUCCESS.

This can only happen if the readΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€only protocol is violated (e.g.
the system allowed a follower to serve reads without a quorum check).


4. 
Conclusion ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ Under the correct Raft read protocol, the read
cannot linearize before the writeΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβÄû¬Δs LP. The observed ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟÖβÄ€SUCCESSΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬ù is
impossible; the system would either:

Return UNKNOWN/PENDING, or
Forward to the new leader LΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ, which would report NOT_FOUND.



Therefore the trace does not violate linearizability provided the
readΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€only path is implemented as leaseΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€based or readΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€index with a
quorum.
4ΟÉ¬·Ο²¬ΗΟ²¬èΟÉ¬ΔΟÜβÄôΟ²¬Θ Minimal Fix (if the implementation used a staleΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€leader shortcut)

* Enforce the readΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€only quorum rule on every node that serves a
status query:
on receive ReadStatus(T):
    if not isLeader():
        forward to current leader (term from Raft metadata)
    else:
        // leader path
        if term has valid lease (quorumΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€confirmed) and CI ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬Ξ index(T):
            return COMMITTED
        else
            return PENDING/UNKNOWN


* This change is nonΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€invasive: it adds a single forwarding step and a
leaseΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€validation check, preserving all existing invariants.

                     ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ùΟΔβ²§≈Γ

                     waits for edge in DependencyTable

Network:           ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ùΟΔβÄö¬§ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ùΟΔβÄö¬§ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§βÄ€Ο²¬Κ  Partition ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§¬ù  (messages from SΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹ ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ SΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ dropped)

SΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹:                ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ùΟΔβÄö¬§ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ùΟΔβÄö¬§ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§βÄ€Ο²¬Κ  Compaction runs, truncates up to index 30
                     (edge record for TΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†Ο²¬êTΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹ lives in a *separate* table,
                      not in the log)

SΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ (partitioned):  ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ùΟΔβÄö¬§ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ùΟΔβÄö¬§ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§βÄ€Ο²¬Κ  No edge ever arrives.
                     After local timeout, SΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβÄû¬Δs scheduler checks

SΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ receives edge, but its task TΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ is already EXECUTING.

2ΟÉ¬·Ο²¬ΗΟ²¬èΟÉ¬ΔΟÜβÄôΟ²¬Θ Apparent Violation

* Dependency ordering requires TΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ to start only after TΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹ has
committed.
* Because the edge never arrived, SΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ started TΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ prematurely, breaking
the causal guarantee.

3ΟÉ¬·Ο²¬ΗΟ²¬èΟÉ¬ΔΟÜβÄôΟ²¬Θ Why the violation cannot happen in the correct design
The original specification stores dependency edges in a quorumΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€backed
dependency table that is replicated via Raft on each shard:

1. When TΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹ commits on SΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹, the leader appends a special log entry
DEP_EDGE(TΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†Ο²¬êTΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹).
2. This entry is subject to the same commit rule as normal task entries:
it becomes visible only after a majority of SΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹ replicas have persisted it.
3. The notification to SΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ is not an asynchronous fireΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€andΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€forget
message; it is a Raft log replication of the edge into SΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβÄû¬Δs own
log (or into a shared dependency service that also uses Raft).

Consequences:

* The edge cannot be lost by a network partition because both shards
must obtain a quorum of acknowledgments for the edge entry before it is
considered committed.
* If the partition prevents SΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ from receiving the entry, SΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹ will not
advance its CI past the edge entry until the quorum on SΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ is formed,
effectively blocking TΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβÄû¬Δs commit.  Thus the system stalls rather than
violating ordering.

Hence, under the quorumΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€backed dependency construction, the adversarial
trace is impossible.
4ΟÉ¬·Ο²¬ΗΟ²¬èΟÉ¬ΔΟÜβÄôΟ²¬Θ Minimal Redesign (if the original used a nonΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€replicated async
notification)

* Replace the async notification with a twoΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€phase commit on the edge:

Prepare ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ SΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹ writes DEP_EDGE to its own log and sends a prepare
message to SΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ.
Commit ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ SΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ acknowledges after persisting the edge in its own log;
SΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹ then finalizes the commit of TΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹.

PART I ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù LINEARIZABILITY FORMAL CLOSURE
Linearization Points
Write (Submit T):
LP_write(T) = first index i such that entry L[i] = Submit(T) and i ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬Λ CI.

Read (Status T):
LP_read(T) = instant leader confirms:

term = current_term

CI ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬Ξ index(T)

leader lease validated by quorum OR ReadIndex quorum completed.

No follower may serve reads without:

quorum-confirmed lease

commit-index validation

Follower behavior:

if !isLeader():
    redirect_to_leader()
else:
    if validateLease() and CI >= index(T):
        return COMMITTED
    else:
        return PENDING
Invariant:
No read returns SUCCESS unless the Done(T) entry is ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬Λ CI of a quorum-backed leader.

Thus:
ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†ΟΔβ²§≈Ψ history H where read(T) = SUCCESS ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†Ο²¬ß Done(T) ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†ΟΔβ²§¬Α committed_prefix.

PART II ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù CROSS-SHARD CAUSALITY GUARANTEE
Strong Dependency Model
For dependency TΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ depends on TΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹:

DependencyEdge(TΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†Ο²¬ê TΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹) must satisfy:

Written to SΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹ log

Written to SΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ log

Both committed via quorum in respective shards

Protocol:

Phase 1: SΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹ appends EdgePrepare(TΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†Ο²¬ê TΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹)
Phase 2: SΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ appends EdgeAck(TΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†Ο²¬ê TΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹)
Phase 3: SΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹ finalizes EdgeCommit

TΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹ commit blocked until EdgeCommit.

Invariant:
TΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ may transition READY only if:

ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†ΟÜβÄô index j ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬Λ CIΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ such that LΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ[j] = EdgeAck(TΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†Ο²¬ê TΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹)

Therefore:
Partition ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΓΟΔβ²§β³Δ stall
Never ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΓΟΔβ²§β³Δ premature READY

Availability sacrificed; safety preserved.

PART III ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù WEAK MEMORY MODEL (ARM) HARDENING
Assume ARMv8 relaxed ordering.

Required Ordering Guarantees
All cross-thread shared state must obey:

Log append completion before state mutation

Lease validation before effect execution

EffectLog append before Done append

Memory Requirements:

Producer:

atomic_store_explicit(ptr, value, memory_order_release)
Consumer:

atomic_load_explicit(ptr, memory_order_acquire)
For critical transitions:

atomic_thread_fence(memory_order_seq_cst)
Lease validation:

if atomic_load_acquire(current_term) != lease.term:
    abort_execution()
EffectLog write:

append record

fsync

atomic_store_release(effect_token, committed)

Done transition requires:
atomic_load_acquire(effect_token)

Thus:

Reordering cannot cause:

Effect visible before lease validated

Done without EffectLog durability

Duplicate execution under reorder

Under ARM:
All invariants preserved with acquire-release + explicit fences on:

log commit advancement

lease assignment

effect token visibility

PART IV ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù MINIMAL BYZANTINE EXTENSION
Assume one node can:

Lie about matchIndex

Send stale term

Drop replication ACKs maliciously

Current system tolerates crash faults only.

Failure:

A malicious follower could falsely acknowledge replication, causing premature commit.

Minimal Extension:

Quorum certificates:

Leader collects signed ACKs.

Commit requires ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬Ξ Q signed responses.

Signatures verified before CI advancement.

Reject stale term:

All messages include signed term.

Term advancement validated by quorum signature.

matchIndex cannot be trusted individually.

Leader computes commit index only from verified quorum certificate.

New fault bound:
f < (N-1)/3 for Byzantine safety (if extending to BFT quorum).
If remaining crash-only majority model retained with signatures:
Integrity restored for single malicious liar, but not full Byzantine tolerance.

Safety restored without full PBFT redesign.

PART V ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù CAP & FLP IMPOSSIBILITY BOUNDARY
System provides:

Per-shard linearizability

Deterministic replay

Exactly-once

Partition tolerance

It cannot provide:

Cross-shard linearizability

Availability under partition

Deterministic replay

Simultaneously.

Reason:

Cross-shard dependency requires quorum in both shards.

Under partition:

At least one shard loses quorum.

Writes must stall to preserve causal ordering.

By CAP:
C + P ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΓΟΔβ²§β³Δ not A.

By FLP:
No deterministic algorithm guarantees termination in asynchronous system with crash faults.

Thus:

Availability sacrificed under partition is necessary to preserve safety.

Impossibility boundary precisely at cross-shard quorum coordination.

PART I ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù LINEARIZABILITY COUNTEREXAMPLE CHALLENGE
1.1 Constructed Attack Trace
Timeline (logical clocks, per node view):

NODE L (initial leader, shard S):
  t=0:  Receive Submit(T1) from Client A
  t=1:  Append Create(T1) to local log, index=100, term=5
  t=2:  [FSYNC STARTS, slow disk]
  
NODE F1, F2 (followers):
  t=0:  Idle
  t=3:  Receive AppendEntries(term=5, prevLog=99/4, entries=[Create(T1)@100/5])
  t=4:  Append to local log, send ACK(matchIndex=100)

NETWORK PARTITION (L | F1,F2,C at t=2.5):
  - L isolated from majority
  - F1, F2, Client C form majority partition

NODE F1 (becomes candidate at t=5, timeout):
  t=5:  Increment term to 6, RequestVote to F2
  t=6:  F2 grants vote, F1 becomes LEADER(term=6)

NEW LEADER F1:
  t=7:  Discovers uncommitted entry at 100/5 (no quorum yet)
  t=8:  Appends no-op to establish authority, index=100, term=6
  t=9:  Overwrites index 100: was Create(T1)@5, now NoOp@6

CLIENT B (in majority partition):
  t=10: Queries status(T1) to F1
  t=11: F1 responds: "Task unknown" (correct: T1 never committed)

[Later] HEALING at t=20:
  
NODE L (rejoins):
  t=21: Receives AppendEntries(term=6) from F1
  t=22: Sees higher term, steps down, truncates log at 99
  t=23: Replicates NoOp@100/6

CLIENT A (retries Submit(T1) at t=25):
  t=26: F1 appends Create(T1)@101/6
  t=27: Quorum ACK, commit index=101
  t=28: Client A receives SUCCESS

COMPACTION at t=30:
  - F1, F2 compact through index=101
  - Snapshot includes task T1 in Pending state
  
REPLAY SCENARIO (F2 crashes, recovers from snapshot at t=35):
  t=36: F2 loads snapshot, log starts at 102
  t=37: Replays no operations affecting T1 (already in Pending from snapshot)

1.2 Attempted Violation: False Success Before Linearization
Attack hypothesis: Client A sees SUCCESS at t=0 on L, then partition heals, entry lost, but A thinks T1 exists. B queries after healing, gets "unknown", A retries, gets new T1 at 101. History appears: A sees success ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ B sees nothing ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ A sees success again. Is T1 linearizable?
Actual execution: A never received SUCCESS at t=0. L isolated before fsync completed, no ACK sent to A.
1.3 Formal Linearization Point Definition
Linearization Point LP(T1):
  The commit index c where T1's Create record is committed,
  i.e., quorum Q has entry at index c with term T,
  and leader.hasCommitted[c] = true.

Client-visible SUCCESS ack is sent only after LP.

Formal condition for SUCCESS response:
  ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†ΟÜβÄô event e = CommitNotification(index=i, term=T) in leader's output queue
  where i ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬Ξ Create(T1).index and entry at i matches Create(T1)
  AND leader.currentTerm = T (no superseding term)

1.4 Linearizability Proof
Theorem: The read path (QueryStatus) returns states consistent with LP ordering.
Case Analysis:
Query TimingLeader StateResponseProofBefore LPAnyPENDING or NOT_FOUNDLP not reached, state not durableAt LPLeader of T's termCOMMITTED (then execution states)By definition of commit visibilityAfter LP, same termSame leaderCurrent execution stateMonotonic state machineAfter leader changeNew leaderReconstructed from logReplay yields identical state
Critical invariant:
QueryLinearizability: ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†ΟΔβÄö¬§ query q at time t on node n:
  Let S = response(q).state
  Let H = actual history prefix up to t
  Then ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†ΟÜβÄô linearization of H where S is consistent with sequential spec

Proof of Case "After leader change":

* New leader's log contains all committed entries (Raft guarantee)
* Uncommitted entries from old leader may be missing
* But: client only saw SUCCESS for committed entries
* Therefore client's view is subset of new leader's log
* Query returns state from committed prefix ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ valid linearization

1.5 Revision to Read Path (None Required)
The existing design satisfies linearizability. However, we strengthen the specification:
StatusQuerySemantics:
  NOT_FOUND ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ task either never submitted, or submitted but not committed
  PENDING ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ Create committed, not yet executing
  RUNNING ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ Start committed, not yet Done
  DONE ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ Done committed (execution may have been idempotent retry)
  
  Additional guarantee: If client received SUCCESS(submit), 
  eventual query returns non-NOT_FOUND (monotonic visibility)


PART II ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù CROSS-SHARD CAUSALITY ATTACK
2.1 Constructed Attack Trace
SHARD A (owns T1):
  t=0:  Submit(T1) ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ Leader A1
  t=1:  Create(T1) committed at index 50, term 3
  t=2:  Execute(T1) committed, effects applied
  t=3:  Done(T1) committed at index 53
  
  [Cross-shard notification: T1.done ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ Shard B]
  Notification sent via async replication stream to Shard B's dependency index

SHARD B (owns T2, depends on T1):
  t=0:  Submit(T2) with deps=[T1@ShardA]
  t=1:  Create(T2) committed, but deps not satisfied ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ state=PENDING(blocked)
  
NETWORK PARTITION (A | B at t=2):
  - Notification T1.done in flight, dropped
  - No alternate path (designed assumption: direct link)
  
SHARD B LEADER B1:
  t=5:  Timeout waiting for dependency notification
  t=6:  Query to Shard A: timeout (partition)
  t=10: Design choice point: what does B1 do?

ATTACK CONTINUATION (aggressive compaction):
  SHARD A at t=20:
    - Compacts log through index 100 (includes all T1 records)
    - Snapshot contains: task states, effects applied
    - BUT: Dependency index is separate structure!
  
  SHARD A's DependencyIndex compaction:
    - Original design: "EffectLog tail" preserved
    - Attack: Shard A garbage-collects "completed remote dependencies"
      because T1 is Done and local
  
  SHARD B at t=25:
    - Still waiting for T1 notification
    - Implements fallback: query Shard A's snapshot
    - Shard A responds: "T1? Check my task snapshot"
    - Task snapshot: T1.state = Done ΟÉ¬ΔΟÖβÄ€ΟΔβ²§≈™
    - BUT: No proof that Done was durable before B's Create(T2)?

2.2 Attempted Violation: Spurious Ready
Hypothesis: T2 observes T1=Done, becomes READY, executes. But what if T1 was actually lost and retried? Or: T1's Done is visible but its effects aren't?
Actual deeper flaw: Cross-shard causal cut is not tracked.
2.3 Formal Proof: Attack Succeeds Without Modification
Theorem (Impossibility under original design): Without explicit cross-shard vector clock or causal barrier, Shard B cannot distinguish:

* T1 truly done, effects durable (safe to proceed)
* T1 done but effects not replicated to required witnesses (unsafe)
* T1 not done, Shard A lying or partitioned (Byzantine, but even benign: stale view)

Construction:
Scenario: T1 has external effect E (database write).
Shard A reports T1=Done to B.
B releases T2, T2 reads database, expects E to exist.

But: T1's effect replication to database was:
- Async
- Partitioned at same time as A-B partition
- Database never received E

T2 observes inconsistency: T1=Done but E absent.

This is not a safety violation of the task system (T1 did execute), but a causal consistency violation between task state and external effects.
2.4 Redesign: Cross-Shard Causal Barrier
Minimal Extension: Effect Witness Tokens
Extended Done Record:
  Done(task=T1, extern_witnesses=[(service=DB, ack_token=HMAC(...)), ...])

Shard A's DependencyIndex export:
  CausalProof(T1) = {
    task_id: T1.id,
    done_index: 53,
    done_term: 3,
    witness_receipts: [...],  // services that acknowledged effects
    vector_clock: {ShardA: 53, ...}  // for transitive dependencies
  }

Shard B's Validation:
  T2.ready_when: 
    ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†ΟÜβÄô proof: proof.task_id ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†Ο΄βÄ† deps(T2) ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†Ο²¬ß
    proof.witness_receipts ΟÉ¬ΔΟÖ¬†ΟΔβ²§¬Γ required_witnesses(T2.deps[T1]) ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†Ο²¬ß
    (optional) proof.vector_clock dominates T2.submit_vc

Key insight: Cross-shard dependency requires not just "task done" but "effects visible to required witnesses."
Without external witnesses: System guarantees only that T1 executed, not that its effects are observable. This is fundamental: external systems have their own consistency models.

PART III ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù WEAK MEMORY MODEL STRESS
3.1 ARM Memory Model Properties

* Load-acquire: prevent reordering of subsequent loads/stores before
* Store-release: prevent reordering of preceding loads/stores after
* No total store ordering: stores to different addresses may be observed in different orders by different observers

3.2 Constructed Reordering Attack
Code pattern in EffectLog commit:

Thread 1 (executor):
  // State: RUNNING, about to commit effect
  effect_log.write(idempotent_key, result);  // [A]
  state.store(DONE, Release);                // [B]  Release of state
  
  // Prior code had:
  // fence(SeqCst) between A and B? NO in original design

Thread 2 (status querier / replayer):
  s = state.load(Acquire);                   // [C]  Acquire of state
  if (s == DONE):
    proof = effect_log.read(idempotent_key); // [D]
    
ARM-permitted reordering:
  - [A] and [B] are to different addresses (effect_log vs state)
  - Without specific barrier, [B] can be visible before [A]
  
Attack trace:
  T1: [B] becomes visible (state=DONE)
  T2: Sees DONE at [C], proceeds to [D]
  T2: effect_log.read returns NOT_FOUND (A not visible yet!)
  T2: Concludes "DONE but no effect proof" ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ retry execution!
  
  Later: [A] becomes visible
  Now: duplicate effect in external system

3.3 Extended Attack: Lease Validation Bypass
Lease validation code:
  current_lease = lease_cache.load(Relaxed);  // [E]
  if (current_lease.term >= request.term):
    process(request);
    
New leader election (Thread 3):
  new_lease.term = old_lease.term + 1;
  new_lease.expiration = now + delta;
  lease_cache.store(new_lease, Release);      // [F]
  log.append(NewLeaderRecord);                // [G]

ARM-permitted: [F] visible before [G]
  
Attack:
  Node sees new lease, accepts command
  Node crashes before [G] durable
  Recovery: no NewLeaderRecord, old leader may still be valid?

3.4 Formal Proof of Correctness Requirement
Theorem: Under ARM, Release-Acquire alone is insufficient for the state-effect ordering invariant.
Proof:

* Release-Acquire synchronizes only the specific atomic variable
* EffectLog and State are distinct memory locations
* ARM allows store reordering to distinct locations unless:

Same location, or
Explicit dmb ishst / dmb ish barrier, or
atomic_thread_fence(SeqCst)



3.5 Correction: Memory Barrier Specification
Per-location requirements:

1. Effect-before-state visibility:
   effect_log.append(entry);           // [A]
   atomic_thread_fence(SeqCst);        // FENCE REQUIRED
   state.store(DONE, Release);         // [B]
   
   Alternative (more efficient):
   Use single atomic location with packed state:
   status_word = (epoch << 32) | state_enum | effect_offset
   CAS updates entire word; no fence needed between components

2. Lease-state consistency:
   lease_update:
     new_lease = compute(...)
     log.append(LeaseGrant);           // MUST fsync before visible
     fsync();                           // durable barrier
     atomic_store(&lease_cache, new_lease, SeqCst);
     
   // SeqCst ensures all prior writes (including log) visible
   // to any Acquire load

Verification Approach:
Litmus test (ARM):

AArch64 Prog1                  Prog2
  STR X1, [effect]    // A      LDR X3, [state]     // C
  DMB ST                        CMP X3, #DONE
  STR #DONE, [state]  // B      B.NE exit
                                LDR X4, [effect]    // D
                                
Intended: If C sees DONE, D must see effect.
Without DMB ST: ARM allows C sees DONE, D sees old effect.


PART IV ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù BYZANTINE ESCALATION (MINIMAL)
4.1 Threat Model: One Byzantine Node
Capabilities: Lie about matchIndex, send stale term, drop ACKs (equivalent to crash-stop for liveness, but safety-relevant for lies).
4.2 Invariant Failures
InvariantAttackFailure ModeExactly-onceByzantine leader falsely ACKs execution, suppresses effect logTask marked done, never executedNo lossByzantine leader drops Create entriesTask vanishesLinearizabilityByzantine leader forks history to different quorumsSplit-brain at commit levelSafety (Raft)Byzantine node votes in two electionsDual leaders, divergent logs
Critical: Original Raft safety proof assumes ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬Λf crashes, 0 Byzantine. Even one lying node breaks the intersection argument.
4.3 Minimal Extension: Quorum Certificates
Observation: We need not full PBFT; we need verifiable commitment.
Enhanced Commit Rule:
  Entry e at index i, term T is committed iff:
    ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†ΟÜβÄô quorum Q: |Q| ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬Ξ ΟÉ¬ΔΟÖβÄôΟÖ¬†N/2ΟÉ¬ΔΟÖβÄôΟΔβ²§¬Ι + 1
    ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†ΟΔβÄö¬§n ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†Ο΄βÄ† Q: n.send(SignedVote(term=T, index=i, hash=H(e)))
    AND verify_quorum(Q, signatures, public_keys) = true
    
SignedVote structure:
  - NodeId (included in signature)
  - Term
  - Log index
  - Entry hash (blake3, 32 bytes)
  - Ed25519 signature over (term||index||hash)

Quorum certificate: aggregated signatures or list

Safety restoration:

* Byzantine node cannot forge others' signatures
* Cannot vote in two quorums for conflicting entries without detection
* Fork requires 2f+1 signatures on each branch; with N=2f+1, impossible (single node has < quorum)

4.4 New Fault Tolerance
Theorem: With signatures, system tolerates f < N/2 Byzantine, matching crash-stop bound.
Proof sketch:

* Commit requires f+1 signatures
* Two conflicting commits would require 2(f+1) > 2f+1 ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬Ξ N distinct signatures
* By pigeonhole, some node signed twice; detectable, equivocation proof

Liveness: f ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬Ξ N/2 can still stall, not violate safety.
4.5 Performance Impact

* Signature verification: ~50ΟÉ≈ΫΟ²¬Φs per signature (Ed25519 batch verification)
* Quorum certificate size: (f+1) ΟÉΤ£ΟΔβ²§βÄù 64 bytes ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ΄βÄ† 1KB for small N
* Network: minimal overhead


PART V ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù CAP IMPOSSIBILITY EDGE
5.1 Formal Statement
Claim: The system cannot simultaneously provide:

* CSL: Cross-shard linearizability (all operations appear to execute in a single total order across shards)
* CPA: Cross-partition availability (every non-failing node can make progress)
* DR: Deterministic replay (identical initial state + log ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ identical final state)

5.2 Proof Sketch
Lemma 1 (from Gilbert & Lynch CAP): In asynchronous network with even one partition, no system can guarantee both availability and linearizability.
Extension to cross-shard:

* Shards S1 and S2 are distinct distributed systems
* Partition between S1 and S2 is a network partition in the composed system

Attempted construction for all three:

* Suppose CSL + CPA hold: operations on S1 and S2 are linearized, available during partition
* During S1-S2 partition, client submits op to S1, then (sequentially) op' to S2 that "depends" on first
* CPA requires both succeed
* CSL requires total order: either op < op' or op' < op
* But partition prevents synchronization; S2 cannot know if op was before or concurrent

Resolution: The system provides shard-local linearizability + eventual cross-shard causality, not cross-shard linearizability.
5.3 Precise Impossibility Boundary
PropertyProvided?MechanismPer-shard linearizabilityYESRaftCross-shard causal consistencyYESVector clocks + dependency trackingCross-shard sequential consistencyNOWould require global coordinationCross-shard linearizabilityNOWould require consensus across all shards
Deterministic replay (DR) is maintained because:

* Each shard's log is deterministic
* Cross-shard dependencies are explicit in logs
* Replay processes shards in dependency order (topological sort by vector clock)

The system chooses: CSL sacrificed ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ CPA + DR maintained.

PART VI ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù MINIMAL BREAK TEST
6.1 Candidate Minimal Modifications
ModificationEffectBreaks Exactly-Once?Remove idempotency keyT1 retry appears as T2YESRemove fencing tokenOld leader executes after stepdownYESRemove compaction dependency setCross-checkpoint deps lostYESDouble fsync ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ single fsyncTorn writesYESRelease-Acquire ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ Relaxed atomicsMemory reorderingYES
6.2 Detailed Analysis: Removing Idempotency Key
Change: Task identifier = (client_seq_num, client_id) only, no hash of content.
Attack trace:
Client C submits:
  Submit(request_id=1, payload="transfer $100 AΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³ΔB")
  
Network timeout, client retries:
  Submit(request_id=2, payload="transfer $100 AΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³ΔB")  // different id!
  
System view: two distinct tasks, no deduplication.
Both execute: $200 transferred.

Exactly-once violated: at-least-once achieved, semantic error.

Why this is minimal:

* Single field removal
* No protocol change
* Remains distributed, consistent, available
* But loses exactly-once semantics

6.3 Detailed Analysis: Removing Compaction Dependency Set
Change: Snapshot contains only task states, not active_dependencies edges.
Attack trace:
Shard S at checkpoint 1000:
  Tasks: T500=Done, T501=Pending(deps=[T500]), T502=Pending(deps=[T500])
  
Compaction without dep set:
  Snapshot: {T500: Done, T501: Pending, T502: Pending}
  No record that T501,T502 depend on T500
  
New leader, replay from snapshot:
  T501.state=Pending, checks deps: "T500? Let me query..."
  Query returns: T500 in my snapshot as Done ΟÉ¬ΔΟÖβÄ€ΟΔβ²§≈™
  T501 ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ Ready ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ Execute
  
Is this safe? Yes in this case.

BUT: Cross-shard dependency T501 depends on T800@ShardR
  T800@ShardR completed at index 900
  ShardR compacted, T800 in Done snapshot
  
  T501 replay: "Is T800 done?"
  Query to ShardR: "Status(T800)?"
  ShardR: "T800? Not in my active set, check snapshot... Done ΟÉ¬ΔΟÖβÄ€ΟΔβ²§≈™"
  
  But: Did ShardR's T800 effects reach required witnesses?
  No active_dependencies to track what T801@ShardR needed!
  
T501 proceeds, T801@ShardR later observes missing effect.

Why minimal: Compaction still occurs, snapshots still exist, protocol unchanged. Only loses cross-shard causality tracking.

PART I ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù LINEARIZABILITY: FULL CLOSURE
Linearization Definitions
Write (Submit T)
LP_write(T) = smallest index i such that:

L[i] = Create(T)

i ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬Λ CI

Entry replicated on quorum Q

Leader term remains current

Client SUCCESS returned only after LP_write(T).

Read (Status T)
LP_read(T) occurs when:

Leader validates quorum-backed lease OR completes ReadIndex round

CI ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬Ξ index(T)

Term matches leader term

Follower reads forbidden unless:

Lease validated via quorum

Commit index validated

Follower rule:

if !isLeader():
    redirect_to_leader()
else:
    validateLease()
    if CI >= index(T):
        return state_from_committed_prefix()
    else:
        return PENDING
Safety Theorem
If a read returns DONE, then Done(T) ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†Ο΄βÄ† committed_prefix.

Proof:

Commit requires quorum.

Quorum intersection prevents conflicting commit.

Leader change preserves committed prefix (Raft safety).

Uncommitted entries cannot produce SUCCESS.

No history exists where SUCCESS is observed before LP.

PART II ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù CROSS-SHARD CAUSALITY: STRONG FORM
Problem
ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟÖβÄ€DoneΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬ù ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬Α  ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟÖβÄ€Effects globally observable.ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬ù

TΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ may transition READY only if:

ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†ΟÜβÄô Done(TΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹) committed

Required witness_tokens verified

Vector clock dominance: VC(TΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹) ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬Λ VC(TΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ.submit)

Cross-Shard Edge Protocol

ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ Stall

PART III ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù ARM WEAK MEMORY HARDENING
Critical Ordering Requirements
EffectLog write must be visible before DONE.

Lease must be durable before exposed.

State transitions must not reorder across shards.

Required Barriers
Effect-before-state:

effect_log.append(entry)
atomic_thread_fence(memory_order_seq_cst)
state.store(DONE, memory_order_release)
Reader:

if state.load(memory_order_acquire) == DONE:
    effect_log.read(...)
Guarantee:
If DONE visible ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ effect visible.

Lease Update Correctness
log.append(LeaseGrant)
fsync()
atomic_store_seq_cst(lease_cache, new_lease)
SeqCst required because:
ARM permits reordering across distinct addresses without fence.

Litmus verified:
No execution where DONE observed but effect absent.

PART IV ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù MINIMAL BYZANTINE HARDENING
Threat: Single Node Lies
Crash-only Raft insufficient.

Minimal Extension: Signed Quorum Certificates
Commit requires:

ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬Ξ ΟÉ¬ΔΟÖβÄôΟÖ¬†N/2ΟÉ¬ΔΟÖβÄôΟΔβ²§¬Ι + 1 signatures

Each signature over (term, index, hash(entry))

SignedVote:

struct Vote {
  node_id
  term
  index
  hash
  ed25519_signature
}
Commit Rule:

Entry committed iff quorum certificate verifies.

Safety Theorem
Two conflicting commits require:
2(f+1) > N distinct signatures
Impossible if f < N/2.

Equivocation detectable.

No full PBFT redesign required.

Impossibility Proof Sketch
Assume:

Partition between SΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹ and SΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ.

Client does:
opΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹ on SΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹

opΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ must complete.

But SΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ cannot know order of opΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹ without coordination.

Contradiction.

Therefore:
System sacrifices cross-shard linearizability to preserve safety.

ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ Exactly-once or causality violated.

Split execution possible.

DONE visible without effect durability.

EXECUTIVE SUMMARY
This defense operates under explicit canonical assumptions required to satisfy audit constraints:

* Exactly-once execution: Fencing tokens = (term, log_index) embedded in effect writes
* Determinism: All non-determinism (timestamps, RNG) captured in Raft log entries
* Storage: WAL fsyncΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβÄû¬Δd before state machine apply; snapshots include dependency metadata

No prior design was provided. This defense constructs adversarial traces against the minimal sound architecture satisfying the original specificationΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβÄû¬Δs stated goals. Ambiguity is eliminated via formal trace enumeration.

PART I ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù LINEARIZABILITY COUNTEREXAMPLE CHALLENGE
