PARTΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·VII ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ PERFORMANCE MODEL
7.1 Throughput Calculation

* Target: 100ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·k tasksΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·/ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·s.
* Sharding: Assume S = 256 shards. Each shard must sustain ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ΄βÄ†ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·390ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·tasksΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·/ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·s.
* PerΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€shard pipeline:

StageAvg latency (ΟÉβÄöΟ²¬Βs)ParallelismWAL append (fsync quorum)150ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·ΟÉβÄöΟ²¬Βs (SSD)1 (single thread)Dependency bitmap update30ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·ΟÉβÄöΟ²¬Βs1Worker execution (pure compute)200ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·ΟÉβÄöΟ²¬Βs4 (CPU cores)SideΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€effect apply (idempotent DB write)250ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·ΟÉβÄöΟ²¬Βs (network)2 (async I/O)
The critical path is WAL + sideΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€effect ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ΄βÄ†ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·400ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·ΟÉβÄöΟ²¬Βs ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ 2ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·500ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·opsΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·/ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·s per core. With 4 cores per shard we have ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ΄βÄ†ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·10ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·kΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·opsΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·/ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·s, comfortably above 390ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·opsΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·/ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·s.
Thus 256 shards ΟÉΤ£ΟΔβ²§βÄù 10ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·kΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·opsΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·/ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·s = 2.56ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·MΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·opsΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·/ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·s headroom > 100ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·k.
7.2 Bottleneck Analysis

* Disk I/O: fsync on SSD can sustain ~10ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·kΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·fsyncΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·/ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·s per device. Deploy RAIDΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€10 of 4 SSDs per node ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ 40ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·kΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·fsyncΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·/ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·s.
* Network: 10ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·GbE NIC can handle >ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·10ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·MΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·pps; far above requirement.
* CPU: 4 cores per shard, each core ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ΄βÄ†ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·2ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·GHz ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ >ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·10ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·MΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·instructionsΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·/ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·s; enough for compute.

The only possible bottleneck is log compaction; it runs in a background thread with low priority and uses copyΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€onΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€write snapshots to avoid blocking the main pipeline.
7.3 Contention & HotΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€Key Mitigation

* Hot task IDs (e.g., retries of the same task) are sharded by hash(task_id). Collisions are rare; if a hot key appears, the shardΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβÄû¬Δs queue may fill. The scheduler detects >ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·80ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·% occupancy and reΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€shards the key to a secondary shard (consistentΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€hash with virtual nodes).

7.4 TailΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€Latency (p99 < 50ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·ms)

* EndΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€toΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€end latency = WAL append (ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬ΛΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·2ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·ms worstΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€case under load) + execution (ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬ΛΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·10ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·ms) + sideΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€effect (ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬ΛΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·30ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·ms) + network queuing (ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬ΛΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·5ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·ms).
* All components are nonΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€blocking; backΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€pressure ensures queues never exceed a bounded depth, guaranteeing the 50ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·ms bound.

7.5 FailureΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€Mode Degradation
FailureEffect on throughputMitigationOne node crash (loss of 1/9 replicas per shard)No loss of quorum ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ throughput unchanged.Raft reΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€replicates missing entries to remaining nodes.Disk failure on a replicaReplica removed from quorum; remaining 8 still form majority.Automatic reΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€balance adds a fresh replica.Network partition (minority)Affected shards become unavailable (no quorum).System reports unavailable but does not lose tasks (they stay in WAL on majority).CPU overload on a leaderLeader may fall behind; followers can trigger leader transfer to a lessΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€loaded node.Raft supports explicit transfer.

PARTΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·VIII ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ STORAGE ENGINE REQUIREMENTS
8.1 WAL Durability

* AppendΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€only log stored on a writeΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€ahead file per shard.
* Each append is followed by fdatasync() (or fsync() on Linux) on all replicas in the quorum.
* Record format includes CRC32 and length; on recovery any record failing checksum is discarded (torn write detection).

8.2 Log Compaction

* Segmented log: each segment ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ΄βÄ†ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·128ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·MiB.
* When the head index exceeds a configurable retention (e.g., 24ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·h) and a snapshot exists, the segment is compactΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€ed: a new snapshot is written (see 8.3) and the old segment is truncated.

8.3 Snapshotting

* Snapshot = serialized state of the deterministic state machine (task table, dependency bitmaps).
* Created asynchronously by the leader after every N committed entries (e.g., NΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·=ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·10ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·k).
* Snapshot file is written to a separate file, fsynced, and its checksum stored in a manifest file.

8.4 Recovery Algorithm (per shard)

1. Load latest manifest ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ locate latest snapshot S.
2. Load S into memory (verify checksum).
3. Scan WAL segments after S.index in order:

Verify CRC; if corrupted, stop (torn write).
Apply each record to the inΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€memory state machine.


4. After reaching the last committed index (known from RaftΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβÄû¬Δs commitIndex), the node is ready.

If a torn write is encountered, the node truncates the log at the previous good index and requests missing entries from the leader (standard Raft log repair).
8.5 Corruption Detection & Mitigation

* Checksum per record ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ immediate detection.
* Snapshot checksum ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ detect diskΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€wide corruption.
* On detection, the node requests the missing/corrupted range from a quorum of peers (Raft InstallSnapshot).


PARTΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·IX ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ OBSERVABILITY & CORRUPTION DETECTION
MetricDescriptionCollectiontasks_submitted_totalCumulative count of SUBMIT records.Prometheus counter (per shard).tasks_committed_totalCumulative count of EXEC_COMMIT.Prometheus counter.task_latency_secondsHistogram of endΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€toΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€end latency (submit ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ commit).Histogram buckets (0ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€5ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·ms, ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬Π, 100ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·ms).wal_append_latency_secondsTime from append call to fsync ack.Exported per shard.replica_lag_entriesDifference between leaderΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβÄû¬Δs lastLogIndex and followerΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβÄû¬Δs matchIndex.Raft internal metric.dependency_resolution_time_secondsTime from last predecessor commit to task becoming READY.Histogram.replay_duration_secondsTime to replay log on node startΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€up.Exported once per restart.corruption_events_totalCount of detected CRC

Deep Research Prompt Writing Expert said:
1ΟÉ¬·Ο²¬ΗΟ²¬èΟÉ¬ΔΟÜβÄôΟ²¬Θ ANALYSIS (Concise)
Strengths

Clear failure model (crash-only, async network, torn-write aware).

Correct quorum-based commit definition.

Deterministic replay separated from side-effects.

Explicit idempotency boundary.

Proper CAP classification (CP).

Snapshot + WAL recovery is structurally sound.

Actor-per-shard model eliminates intra-shard races.

Good crash-window enumeration.

Structural Weaknesses

Side-effects applied after quorum commit creates a correctness gap if worker crashes before apply.

Idempotency depends on external systems but not formally enforced.

Dependency bitmap CAS approach not formally tied to Raft ordering.

Re-sharding hot keys mid-flight risks violating deterministic sharding invariant.

WAL fsync assumptions optimistic for 100k/sec at scale.

No formal proof of linearizability of external visibility.

No formal invariant tying epoch token to side-effect execution ordering.

Retry semantics not formally modeled as part of state machine.

Performance model assumes perfect batching without modeling queueing theory formally.

Overall: Very strong architecture-level design (ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ΄βÄ† 92ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™94 tier) but not yet ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟÖβÄ€research-paper airtight.ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬ù

2ΟÉ¬·Ο²¬ΗΟ²¬èΟÉ¬ΔΟÜβÄôΟ²¬Θ IMPROVED VERSION (Hardened, Higher-Value, Peer-Review Grade)
Distributed Deterministic Exactly-Once Task Engine
Adversarially Complete ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ Formal Safety Core

I. Strengthened Exactly-Once Model
Revised Commit Protocol (Fixes Side-Effect Gap)
Replace prior ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟÖβÄ€commit then applyΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬ù model with Effect-First Deterministic Commit:

New Phases

EXEC_PREPARE(t, attempt_id)

Deterministic computation only (no external effects).

Produces effect_descriptor (pure description, not executed).

EXEC_COMMIT(t, attempt_id, effect_descriptor_hash)

Persisted and quorum-committed.

Defines exact external effect to apply.

EXEC_APPLIED(t, attempt_id)

Written only after successful external side-effect execution.

Side-effect is idempotent using (task_id, attempt_id) as key.

New Exactly-Once Invariant
For any task t:

|{ e | EXEC_APPLIED(e) for t }| ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬Λ 1
And:

EXEC_APPLIED(t) ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΓΟΔβ²§β³Δ EXEC_COMMIT(t) ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†Ο΄βÄ† WAL ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†Ο²¬ß quorum-committed
Thus:

Crash after commit but before apply ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ replay sees committed descriptor but no EXEC_APPLIED ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ safe to apply once.

Crash after apply but before EXEC_APPLIED write ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ idempotent external service suppresses duplicate; replay re-applies safely.

This closes the correctness gap.

II. Formal Linearizability Boundary
Define external visibility:

Linearization Point = quorum commit of EXEC_COMMIT

External observers see effect only after:

ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†ΟÜβÄô quorum Q: EXEC_COMMIT(t) persisted ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†Ο²¬ß leader.commitIndex ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬Ξ index(t)
All status queries use Raft ReadIndex protocol.

III. Deterministic Dependency Resolution (Corrected)
Replace bitmap CAS approach with log-driven dependency resolution:

New record type:

DEP_RESOLVED(task_id, predecessor_id)
Invariant:

READY(t) ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΓΟΔβ²§¬ù ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†ΟΔβÄö¬§ p ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†Ο΄βÄ† deps(t): EXEC_COMMIT(p) ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†Ο΄βÄ† committed_log
No CAS mutation outside log.
Dependency readiness is derived, not mutated.

This restores replay determinism.

IV. Sharding Invariant (Hot-Key Fix)
Original design allowed dynamic re-sharding.
That breaks determinism.

Correction:
Shard assignment is immutable:

shard(t) = hash(task_id) mod S
Hot-key mitigation is solved via:

Back-pressure

Adaptive batching

Priority queue isolation

Never via shard reassignment.

V. Retry Formalization
Add retry as first-class state:

New record:

EXEC_RETRY(t, attempt_id, backoff_deadline)
Invariant:

attempt_id strictly increases per task
Stored in WAL ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ replay reconstructs retry history exactly.

VI. Strengthened Failure Model Guarantees
Added Invariant: Fencing Safety
If EXEC_COMMIT(t, term = E) exists,
then no worker with term < E may apply side-effects.
Workers validate:

current_term == record.term
before applying effect.

VII. Performance Model (Queue-Theoretic Tightening)
Model each shard as M/D/1 queue:

Arrival rate ΟÉ≈ΫΟ²¬Μ ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ΄βÄ† 390/sec
Service rate ΟÉ≈ΫΟ²¬Φ ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ΄βÄ† 10k/sec

Utilization ΟÉ¬èΟ²¬¹ = ΟÉ≈ΫΟ²¬Μ / ΟÉ≈ΫΟ²¬Φ = 0.039

Expected queue length negligible.
p99 latency dominated by external side-effect network call.

Revised worst-case fsync assumption:

Batched fsync per 64 records

Effective fsync cost amortized to 20ΟÉβÄöΟ²¬Βs

This makes 100k/sec realistic on modern NVMe.

VIII. Stronger Minimal Invariant Set
Log Monotonicity

Quorum Commit Implies Durability

External Effect Requires EXEC_COMMIT

EXEC_APPLIED ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬Λ 1 per task

Dependency Ready Derived from Log Only

Shard Assignment Deterministic

These six are sufficient and necessary.

I'll conduct this design as a formal systems architecture exercise, building from first principles with explicit invariants and proof obligations. No code will be presentedΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ùonly specifications, state machines, invariants, and proof sketches.

PART I ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù FORMAL FAILURE MODEL
1.1 Crash Model: Fail-Recover with Amnesia
AspectDefinitionProcess crashImmediate halt. Volatile state lost. Persistent state (WAL on disk) survives iff fsync acknowledged.RecoveryProcess restarts with empty memory, replays WAL from last checkpoint. No assumption of stable identity across restarts.AmnesiaRecovered process has no memory of in-flight work. Must reconstruct state from durable log.Disk crashIndividual block device may experience torn writes (power loss mid-sector). Detected via checksums.
Critical invariant: All decisions with external effect must be WAL-persisted before effect visibility.
1.2 Network Model: Asynchronous with Message Loss and Duplication
PropertySpecificationTimingNo bounds on message delay. No synchronized clocks.PartitionArbitrary subset of nodes may be unreachable from others indefinitely.DeliveryMessages may be lost, duplicated, reordered. No exactly-once delivery guarantee from network.FIFONot guaranteed. TCP provides byte-stream order, but connections may reset.
Implication: All "at-least-once" delivery properties must be built atop this using explicit acknowledgments and deduplication.
1.3 Storage Guarantees: Strict Durability with Torn Write Awareness
PropertySpecificationfsync contractfsync() returns success ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ data durable on power loss.Atomic writeWrites ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬Λ 512 bytes (atomic sector) are atomic. Larger writes may tear at 512-byte boundaries.ChecksumEvery WAL entry carries CRC32C. Torn write detected via checksum mismatch + length validation.Write orderSame file descriptor: writes observable in program order. No guarantee across fds.
Torn write model: For N-byte write where N > 512, any prefix [0,k*512] may be durable, suffix garbage.
1.4 Memory Model: x86-64 TSO with Explicit Fences
AspectSpecificationBase modelTotal Store Order (TSO) ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù loads may be reordered with earlier stores to different addresses.Fencessfence, lfence, mfence available. LOCK prefix provides full barrier.Relaxed atomicsmemory_order_relaxed provides no ordering guarantees.Acquire/Releasememory_order_acquire/release map to LOCK or MFENCE on x86.
Design constraint: All inter-thread communication uses release/acquire or seq_cst atomics. No data races.
1.5 Adversary Model: Non-Byzantine (Crash-Fault) with Network Adversary
CapabilityDescriptionCrash timingAny process may crash at any instruction boundary.Network delayArbitrary, unbounded. May partition indefinitely.No Byzantine faultsCorrect processes follow protocol. No malicious corruption of messages.No omniscienceAdversary does not know future random values (fencing tokens derived from cryptographically secure RNG).

PART II ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù SAFETY AND LIVENESS SPECIFICATION
2.1 Safety Properties
S1: No Task Lost
DefinitionInvariantΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†ΟΔβÄö¬§ task t: if Submit(t) acknowledged by system, then Execute(t) ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†Ο²¬® t in persistent queue.ViolationAcknowledged task never executes; acknowledged task deleted without execution.PreventionAcknowledgment sent only after WAL append with fsync. Task recovered by any node replaying WAL.
S2: Exactly-Once Execution
DefinitionInvariantΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†ΟΔβÄö¬§ task t: Execute(t) occurs at most once in system's observable history.ViolationSame task executed on two nodes; same task executed twice on one node across recovery.Prevention(a) Idempotency key ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ deterministic shard assignment ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ single owner epoch; (b) Fencing tokens prevent stale owners from committing; (c) WAL-recorded "executed" bit prevents replay.
S3: Dependency Ordering Preserved
DefinitionInvariantΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†ΟΔβÄö¬§ tasks tΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹, tΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ: if Depends(tΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹, tΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ) in DAG, then Commit(tΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹) before Begin(tΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ).ViolationtΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ executes before tΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹ despite declared dependency.PreventionDependency edges materialized as blocking records in WAL. tΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ's "ready" condition requires observable Commit(tΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹) in shard's replicated state.
S4: Deterministic Replay
DefinitionInvariantRecovery produces identical system state as original execution would have.ViolationNon-deterministic choices (RNG without seed, timestamp dependency, concurrency races) cause divergence.PreventionAll external inputs logged to WAL; task scheduling order by deterministic comparison of (epoch, sequence); no wall-clock dependencies in core logic.
2.2 Liveness Properties
L1: Eventually Executes (if dependencies resolvable)
DefinitionInvariantΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†ΟΔβÄö¬§ task t: if all dependencies ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†ΟΔβ²§¬Π or committed, and system has ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬Ξ 1 correct node, then ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§βÄùΟΔβ²§¬Γ Execute(t).ViolationTask orphaned by crashed owner, no failover; deadlock in dependency resolution; infinite retry backoff.Progress(a) Membership protocol detects owner failure; (b) Epoch advancement triggers rebalancing; (c) Exponential backoff capped at 30s with jitter.
L2: Progress Under Partial Failure
DefinitionInvariantIf majority of shard owners correct and connected, system continues processing tasks for those shards.ViolationMinority failure halts all processing; leader election deadlocks.ProgressShard-based parallel consensus. Failed shards stall; healthy shards progress independently.

PART III ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù DETERMINISTIC STATE MACHINE
3.1 Task Lifecycle State Transition Table
State := PENDING | BLOCKED(deps) | READY | ASSIGNED(epoch, owner) | 
         EXECUTING | COMPLETED(result) | FAILED(error, retry_count)

Transitions:
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PENDING --[Submit]--> BLOCKED(deps)      if deps ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬Α  ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†ΟΔβ²§¬Π
PENDING --[Submit]--> READY              if deps = ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†ΟΔβ²§¬Π

BLOCKED(deps) --[DepCommitted(d)]--> BLOCKED(deps \ {d})
BLOCKED(ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†ΟΔβ²§¬Π) --[Sanity]--> READY

READY --[Assign]--> ASSIGNED(epoch, owner)   // owner is node_id, epoch is logical time

ASSIGNED(e,o) --[LeaseExpire]--> READY       // fencing token invalid
ASSIGNED(e,o) --[BeginExec]--> EXECUTING     // only if fencing token matches current epoch

EXECUTING --[Success, result]--> COMPLETED(result)
EXECUTING --[Failure, retryable]--> READY   if retry_count < max
EXECUTING --[Failure, fatal]--> FAILED(error, final)

COMPLETED --[GC]--> ΟÉ¬ΔΟÖ¬†Ο²¬Ξ                        // log compaction, snapshot advance
FAILED --[Archive]--> ΟÉ¬ΔΟÖ¬†Ο²¬Ξ

3.2 WAL Record Structure
pythonDownloadCopy codeWALRecord := {
  epoch:          u64,           # logical epoch of owning shard
  sequence:       u64,           # monotonic per-shard, strictly increasing
  checksum:       u32,           # CRC32C of serialized record
  type:           enum {
                    SUBMIT,      # new task creation
                    DEP_ADD,     # dynamic dependency injection
                    DEP_SAT,     # dependency satisfied notification
                    ASSIGN,      # task assigned to node
                    BEGIN,       # execution starting
                    COMPLETE,    # execution finished, result recorded
                    FAIL,        # execution failed, retry state
                    EPOCH_ADV,   # shard epoch advancement (fencing)
                  },
  task_id:        u128,          # global unique (hi=shard_id, lo=sequence)
  idempotency_key: bytes[32],    # client-provided, collision-resistant
  payload_hash:   bytes[32],     # SHA-256 of opaque task payload
  dependencies:   [u128],        # for SUBMIT, DEP_ADD: task IDs this blocks on
  result_hash:    bytes[32],     # for COMPLETE: hash of result
  node_id:        u64,           # for ASSIGN, BEGIN: claiming executor
  fencing_token:  u64,           # for BEGIN: must match current epoch
  timestamp:      u64,           # logical sequence number, NOT wall-clock
}
3.3 Commit Point Definition
The commit point is fsync completion of WAL append with matching epoch and sequence.
Formally: for operation op on task t, Commit(op) occurs at wall-clock time t_c where:

* WAL record R for op has been appended to shard's log
* fsync(R.offset) returned success at t_c
* R.sequence = prev_sequence + 1 (verified by atomic increment)

Invariants at commit point:
I_commit: ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†ΟΔβÄö¬§ record R committed at sequence s:
  (1) All records with sequence < s are committed
  (2) R.epoch ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬Ξ previous_record.epoch  [epoch monotonic]
  (3) R.fencing_token = current_epoch if R.type ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†Ο΄βÄ† {ASSIGN, BEGIN}

3.4 Replay Algorithm
function Recover(shard_id):
  1. Read last checkpoint (if any) ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ snapshot at sequence S_checkpoint
  2. Open WAL files from S_checkpoint onward
  3. For each record R in sequence order:
       a. Verify checksum(R)
       b. If checksum fails: truncate at previous record, halt recovery
       c. Apply R to in-memory state machine (deterministic function)
       d. Track max_epoch seen
  4. Set current_epoch = max_epoch + 1  # fence all prior leases
  5. Broadcast EPOCH_ADV(current_epoch) to other nodes
  6. Enter normal operation

State application (deterministic):
  SUBMIT:      insert task to PENDING or BLOCKED
  DEP_ADD:    add edges to dependency graph, recompute readiness
  DEP_SAT:    mark dependency satisfied, potentially unblock
  ASSIGN:     update task.owner, set ASSIGNED
  BEGIN:      verify fencing_token == current_epoch; set EXECUTING
  COMPLETE:   set COMPLETED, record result, trigger downstream DEP_SAT
  FAIL:       increment retry_count, reset to READY or FAILED
  EPOCH_ADV:  update current_epoch (must be > previous)

3.5 Idempotency Boundary
Idempotency domain: (idempotency_key, shard_id, epoch_created)

* Client submits (key, payload) ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ system computes shard_id = hash(key) % N_shards
* Duplicate SUBMIT with same key in same epoch_created ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ detected via in-memory or checkpoint-resident key index
* Cross-epoch detection: WAL scanned for COMPLETED or FAILED(final) with matching key

Exactly-once guarantee: At most one COMPLETE record exists per idempotency_key in all history of a shard.
3.6 Dependency Resolution Algorithm
Dependency graph G = (V_tasks, E_depends_on)

function AddDependency(child, parent):
  # Called when DEP_ADD or SUBMIT(with deps) committed
  Edge e = (child ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ parent)
  if parent ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†Ο΄βÄ† CompletedSet:
    Immediately enqueue DEP_SAT(child, parent)
  else:
    Add e to G_waiting
    parent.waiters.add(child)

function OnComplete(parent):
  for child in parent.waiters:
    Enqueue DEP_SAT(child, parent) to WAL

Readiness condition for child:
  ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†ΟΔβÄö¬§ p ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†Ο΄βÄ† child.dependencies: p ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†Ο΄βÄ† CompletedSet
  ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†Ο²¬ß no cycle detected (via incremental DFS on G_waiting)

Cycle detection:
  - On each DEP_ADD: run DFS from child following ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ edges
  - If reach child: cycle exists, reject with CYCLE_DETECTED
  - Complexity bounded: max dependency depth = 16 (enforced)

3.7 Deadlock Detection
No wait-for cycles possible by construction:

* Tasks only wait for completed dependencies (DAG edges are await-completion, not await-lock)
* Dependency addition is static at task creation or injected before execution begins
* Once EXECUTING, no new incoming dependencies allowed

Liveness hazard: Circular dependency creation at runtime.

* Detected at DEP_ADD commit time via DFS (see 3.6)
* Rejected before task becomes READY

3.8 Minimal Invariant Set & Inductive Proof
Core Invariants
IDInvariantTypeI1Sequence numbers strictly increasing per shardSafetyI2Epoch monotonic: epoch_{t+1} > epoch_tSafetyI3Fencing token validity: BEGIN only succeeds with token = current_epochSafetyI4Dependency satisfaction: task t in EXECUTING ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΓΟΔβ²§β³Δ all deps in COMPLETEDSafetyI5Exactly-once: at most one COMPLETE per idempotency_keySafetyI6WAL durability: committed record survives crashSafetyI7Task liveness: READY task eventually ASSIGNED (if healthy owner)Liveness
Inductive proof sketch for I1 (sequence monotonicity):

* Base: Empty shard, first record has sequence 0.
* Step: Assume record at sequence k committed. Next append reads max_sequence = k, increments atomically (CAS loop), writes k+1. Atomic increment ensures no duplicate sequence. fsync before acknowledgment ensures durability.

Inductive proof sketch for I5 (exactly-once):

* Base: Key not in index. First SUBMIT with key K creates record. Index updated only on COMPLETE commit.
* Step: Duplicate SUBMIT observes key in index (either PENDING or terminal). System returns existing task_id without new WAL entry. For COMPLETE: shard-owned index ensures atomic check-and-set. If two COMPLETE race, first to commit wins; second sees existing COMPLETE, becomes no-op.


PART IV ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù DISTRIBUTED COORDINATION
4.1 Membership Protocol: Gossip-based with Failure Suspicion
NodeState := { ACTIVE, SUSPECT, FAILED, LEFT }

Protocol:
1. Each node maintains heartbeat table: node_id ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ (seq, last_heard, incarnation)
2. Periodic (100ms) gossip to K random peers (K=3)
3. Push-Pull: send local table, receive remote, merge max(seq, incarnation)
4. Failure suspicion: if last_heard > T_fail (10s), mark SUSPECT
5. Confirmation: SUSPECT propagated; if not refuted in T_confirm (5s), mark FAILED
6. Incarnation increment on restart (distinguishes new process from zombie)

Properties:
- No central registry
- Eventual consistency of membership view
- Strong partition resistance: minority partition continues independently

4.2 Shard Ownership Consensus: Multi-Paxos per Shard
No global leader. Each shard has independent consensus group (default: 3 replicas).
Shard group: replica set R = {r0, r1, r2}
Role per shard: LEADER, FOLLOWER, CANDIDATE

Leader election:
- When FOLLOWER timeout (randomized 150-300ms) expires without AppendEntries from leader
- Increment term, convert to CANDIDATE, request votes from majority
- Voter rejects if: term < current, or log behind (last_log_index, last_log_term)

Log replication:
- Leader appends client SUBMIT to local WAL
- Broadcast AppendEntries to followers
- Majority ACK ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ commit, respond to client
- Followers apply committed entries to state machine

4.3 Epoch and Fencing Token Scheme
Epoch definition:
  shard.epoch = max(term, recovery_count)

Fencing token generation:
  token = (epoch << 32) | leader_start_time_nonce

Token validation:
  - On ASSIGN: token must match current leader's epoch
  - On BEGIN: executor presents token; state machine verifies epoch match
  - Token rejected if epoch < current_epoch

Epoch advancement triggers:
  1. New leader elected (higher term)
  2. Recovery completes (detected uncommitted entries, must advance)
  3. Explicit fence request from higher-priority node (rebalancing)

4.4 Split-Brain Resolution
ScenarioResolutionTwo leaders in same termImpossible by Paxos: leader requires majority, two majorities intersectTwo leaders in different termsLower-term leader steps down on seeing higher term in any messagePartition with leader in minorityMinority leader steps down (cannot replicate); majority partition elects new leader; epochs divergeRe-mergeTerm comparison resolves; divergent logs: newer term overwrites, same term: longer log wins
Dual execution prevention:

* Only leader can ASSIGN
* ASSIGN carries fencing token from that leader's epoch
* Old leader's ASSIGNs rejected by executors (epoch check)
* Even if two leaders temporarily think they're active, fencing tokens distinguish epochs

4.5 Rebalancing on Node Failure
Failure detected: node N marked FAILED in gossip

For each shard S where N was leader:
  1. Remaining replicas initiate election (randomized timeout)
  2. New leader elected (must have most recent log)
  3. New leader broadcasts EPOCH_ADV(epoch+1)
  4. Tasks in ASSIGNED(N, old_epoch) ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ reset to READY
  5. New assignments use fresh fencing tokens

For shards where N was follower:
  1. Leader detects follower loss via heartbeat timeout
  2. If replication factor < target, add replacement from pool
  3. Log catch-up via standard snapshot + incremental

Ownership transfer (rebalancing for load):
  1. Source leader: STOP_ACCEPTING new SUBMIT
  2. Wait for in-flight to complete or timeout
  3. Transfer WAL tail to destination
  4. Destination: replay, catch up to source
  5. Atomic handoff: source writes LEADER_TRANSFER, steps down
  6. Destination becomes leader with epoch+1

4.6 CAP Classification
CP with available partition tolerance for subsets.
PropertyStatusConsistencyStrong (linearizable per shard via Paxos)AvailabilityPartition-tolerant for majority partitions; minority partition unavailable for writesPartition toleranceMaintains safety under all partitions; liveness only for majority
Formal statement: For each shard, if a majority of replicas are correct and mutually reachable, the shard remains available. Under partition, at most one majority-side leader exists; safety preserved.

PART V ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù EXACTLY-ONCE SEMANTICS
5.1 Exact Commit Point
COMMIT_POINT(task execution):
  1. Client submits task with idempotency_key K
  2. Shard leader appends SUBMIT record to WAL
  3. Majority replication ACK received
  4. fsync(local WAL) confirmed
  5. SUBMIT acknowledged to client  ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†Ο²¬ê EXTERNAL COMMIT POINT
  
  6. (later) ASSIGN record committed
  7. (later) BEGIN record committed with fencing token
  8. Task executes (side effects occur)
  9. COMPLETE record committed  ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†Ο²¬ê EXECUTION COMMIT POINT
  
  10. Result acknowledged to client / next tasks triggered

External effect visibility: After step 9. Before step 9, effects are tentative and rolled back on crash.

5.2 Side-Effect Isolation Strategy
Two-phase external commit:
Phase 1 (Prepare):
  - Execute task logic
  - Capture all external effects as pending operations
  - Write PENDING_EFFECTS { ops, preimage } to WAL
  - Acquire idempotency lease from external system (if supported)

Phase 2 (Commit):
  - Append COMMIT_EFFECTS to WAL
  - Apply effects to external systems
  - External systems must support:
      * idempotent application (dedupe via lease/preimage), OR
      * two-phase commit (prepare/confirm)

Fallback for non-idempotent external systems:
  - Effect application logged with unique sequence
  - On recovery, check external system state vs logged sequence
  - Re-apply if absent, skip if present

5.3 Retry Duplicate Prevention
MechanismScopeIdempotency key indexPrevents duplicate SUBMITFencing tokensPrevents stale executor from committingWAL sequencePrevents duplicate COMPLETE (same or different executor)External leasePrevents duplicate external effects
Retry flow:
Client timeout / error ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ retry with same K
Server receives: K exists in PENDING/ASSIGNED/EXECUTING
  ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ Return current status (no re-execution)
Server receives: K in COMPLETED
  ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ Return cached result (no re-execution)
Server receives: K not found (log truncation / new shard)
  ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ Full replay from snapshot; index reconstruction finds K or not
  ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ If not found: treat as new SUBMIT (key truly new or very old GC'd)

5.4 Crash Window Analysis
(a) Before WAL append
State: Client submitted, no record durable.
Detection: Client retry.
Resolution: Duplicate detection fails (no record), re-SUBMIT. Idempotency key ensures single eventual entry.
(b) After WAL append but before ACK
State: Record durable, client unaware.
Detection: Client retry.
Resolution: Server sees existing SUBMIT with matching K, returns success (may need to re-replicate if leader changed).
(c) After execution but before COMPLETE commit
State: Effects possibly applied externally, no durable record.
Detection: Recovery scans WAL; finds BEGIN but no COMPLETE.
Resolution: Critical window. Must determine: did effects occur?
Handler:

* If external system has idempotency lease from our fencing token: effect applied, write COMPLETE on recovery
* Else: re-execute (external system must be idempotent or detectable)

Determinism requirement: Task must be deterministic, or external state must be reconstructible.
(d) During dependency resolution
State: DEP_ADD or DEP_SAT in flight, graph partially constructed.
Detection: Recovery replays WAL; incomplete graph detected.
Resolution: Replay reconstructs exact same graph state. Dependency edges are pure function of WAL records. No divergence.
(e) During failover
State: Old leader crashed, new leader electing.
Detection: Gossip + Paxos timeout.
Resolution:

* Uncommitted entries from old leader: not visible (no majority ACK)
* New leader's epoch fence prevents old leader from completing
* Tasks in ASSIGNED(old_epoch): reset to READY, re-scheduled


PART VI ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù CONCURRENCY & MEMORY DISCIPLINE
6.1 Concurrency Model: Sharding + Actor-per-Shard
LevelModelCross-shardMessage-passing (gRPC/TCP), no shared stateIntra-shardSingle event-loop (io_uring/epoll) per shard leader; followers use thread-per-connection for replicationCPU parallelismOne thread per physical shard; NUMA-aware pinning
No global mutex: Each shard's state machine is single-threaded. Cross-shard operations (rare: dependency across shards) use async message passing with callback completion.
6.2 Shared State Strategy
DataOwnershipAccess PatternShard state machineShard thread exclusivelyNo shared accessGossip membership tableBackground thread + RCUReaders lock-free, writer copies & atomics swapMetrics (counters)Shard thread local, periodic aggregationfetch_add relaxed, batchedWAL file handlesShard thread exclusivelyAsync io_uring, no blocking
6.3 Deadlock Prevention

* Wait-for graph: No locks held across I/O (async completion-based)
* Resource ordering: If cross-shard needed, shard_id ordering enforced
* Timeout: All external operations (replication, dependency wait) have deadline

6.4 Memory Ordering
PatternOrderingRationaleShard state updatesSeq_cst within threadSingle thread, compiler barrier sufficientGossip table swapRelease (write) + Acquire (read)RCU patternCounter incrementRelaxedStatistical, no ordering dependencyWAL commit sequenceLOCK prefix / fetch_add seq_cstTotal order required
6.5 ABA Protection
StructureProtectionLock-free queues (cross-thread, rare)Tagged pointers (48-bit pointer + 16-bit sequence in 64-bit)Epoch counters64-bit, overflow impossible in system lifetime
6.6 Backpressure Propagation
Flow control levels:
  L1: Single-shard queue depth > 10k ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ stop accepting SUBMIT, return UNAVAILABLE
  L2: Total memory > 80% ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ shed non-critical tasks, prioritize COMPLETE
  L3: Disk utilization > 90% ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ read-only mode, halt new SUBMIT
  L4: Cross-shard dependency chain depth > 16 ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ reject DEP_ADD

Backpressure signal: HTTP 503 + Retry-After header
Client behavior: exponential backoff with jitter

6.7 Bounded Memory Enforcement
MechanismLimitPer-shard task queue1M tasks (configurable)Dependency graph16-depth, 64 outgoing edges per taskIn-flight executionsconcurrency_limit per shardWAL retention48 hours or 100GB, whichever first (compaction)Snapshot intervalEvery 10M records or 1 hour
OOM prevention: Pre-allocated buffers for hot paths. mlock critical WAL buffers.

PART VII ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù PERFORMANCE MODEL
7.1 Throughput Math for 100K/sec
Assumptions:
Task size: 1KB payload average
WAL record size: ~256 bytes overhead + 1KB payload = 1.25KB
Replication: 3x (1 leader + 2 followers)
Network: 10 Gbps = 1.25 GB/s
Disk: NVMe SSD, 500K IOPS, 3 GB/s sequential write

Per-shard throughput:
Paxos round-trip: 2 messages (AppendEntries + majority ACK)
Latency budget: 1ms RTT ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ 500 rounds/sec per shard (conservative)
Batching: 100 tasks per AppendEntries ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ 50,000 tasks/sec per shard

Required shards for 100K/sec: ceil(100K / 50K) = 2 shards minimum
Safety margin: 10 shards for parallelism, hotspot mitigation

Disk bandwidth:
100K tasks/sec ΟÉΤ£ΟΔβ²§βÄù 1.25KB ΟÉΤ£ΟΔβ²§βÄù 3 replicas = 375 MB/sec write
Well within NVMe capacity (3 GB/s). 
With compression (zstd-3): ~150 MB/sec.

7.2 Bottleneck Analysis
ComponentTheoretical MaxDesign LimitHeadroomNetwork (10G)1.25 GB/s375 MB/s3.3ΟÉΤ£ΟΔβ²§βÄùDisk sequential3 GB/s150 MB/s (compressed)20ΟÉΤ£ΟΔβ²§βÄùDisk IOPS500K1K (batched writes)500ΟÉΤ£ΟΔβ²§βÄùCPU (task exec)-User-definedN/APaxos latency-1ms-
Actual bottleneck: Task execution time (user code). System overhead < 5%.
7.3 Contention Analysis
ResourceContention LevelMitigationShard leadershipPer-shard10 shards, independentGlobal sequenceNonePer-shard sequenceIdempotency indexHash-partitioned within shard256 buckets, lock-free per bucketDependency graphPer-shard, rare cross-shardDepth limit, cycle detectionGossip tableRead-heavyRCU, copy-on-write
7.4 Hot Key Mitigation
Problem: Single idempotency_key ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ single shard ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ hotspot.
Mitigation:
Key space: idempotency_key || timestamp_nonce
Shard assignment: hash(key) with rendezvous hashing for rebalancing

Auto-sharding: If shard > 50K tasks/sec sustained:
  1. Split shard: create S' with half key range
  2. Migrate records in key range [a,b) to S'
  3. Update routing table (gossip-propagated)
  4. Drain and retire old shard

7.5 Tail Latency Control (p99 < 50ms)
SourceBudgetControlNetwork RTT5msSame-DC deployment, RDMA optionalPaxos replication10msBatching 100 tasks, pipeliningDisk fsync5msNVMe with polling, SPDK optionalQueueing25msAdaptive batch sizing, load sheddingExecution5msUser SLA enforcement, timeout
Techniques:

* io_uring with polling mode for sub-ΟÉ≈ΫΟ²¬Φs syscall overhead
* Task execution isolation: separate thread pool, preemption via deadline
* Priority queue: COMPLETE records prioritized over SUBMIT

7.6 Failure-Mode Degradation
FailurePerformance ImpactRecoverySingle node crash1/N shards stall for 1-2sAutomatic failoverNetwork partition (minority)Partitioned nodes unavailableMajority continuesDisk degradationAffected shard latency spikeAutomatic re-replicationHot spotSingle shard > 50K/sec ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ latency ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§Υ€Auto-sharding trigger

PART VIII ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù STORAGE ENGINE REQUIREMENTS
8.1 WAL Durability Guarantees
Write path:
  1. Serialize WAL record to thread-local buffer
  2. Copy to O_DIRECT aligned buffer (512B multiple)
  3. pwritev2 with RWF_SYNC (or fsync after batch)
  4. Wait for completion (io_uring CQ)
  5. Update in-memory sequence, release ACK

Guarantees:
  - fsync failures: retry with exponential backoff, alert if persistent
  - Power loss: records up to last completed fsync durable
  - No write caching: O_DIRECT | O_DSYNC or explicit fdatasync

8.2 Log Compaction Strategy
Level-triggered compaction:

L0: Recent WAL files (uncompacted, 64MB each)
L1: SST-like format, sorted by task_id, zstd-compressed
L2: Snapshot format, full state machine serialization

Trigger: L0 size > 1GB OR time since last snapshot > 1 hour

Compaction process:
  1. Scan L0 files, build index: task_id ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ latest record
  2. For each task: if COMPLETED/FAILED(final), emit only terminal record
  3. Write L1 files, update manifest atomically (rename)
  4. Delete L0 files referenced in new L1

Snapshot (L2):
  1. Stop the world for shard (milliseconds)
  2. Serialize state machine to checkpoint file
  3. Record snapshot sequence number
  4. Truncate WAL before snapshot

8.3 Snapshotting Model
Snapshot S at sequence s contains:
  - All tasks with sequence ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬Λ s
  - Dependency graph state at s
  - Idempotency index at s
  - Current epoch

Format: Cap'n Proto (zero-copy serialization, schema evolution)

Integrity: SHA-256 of snapshot, stored in separate .sha256 file

8.4 Recovery Algorithm Step-by-Step
function Recover(shard_path):
  1. List files: find newest checkpoint (*.chk) and WAL files (*.wal)
  2. Verify checkpoint checksum
     - If fail: try previous checkpoint
     - If all fail: crash, manual intervention required
  3. Load checkpoint ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ state machine at sequence s_chk
  4. For each WAL file with start_sequence > s_chk:
     a. Verify file header magic + version
     b. Scan records in sequence order
     c. For each record:
        i.   Verify checksum
        ii.  If checksum fail: truncate here, log warning
        iii. If sequence != expected: gap detected, halt (corruption)
        iv.  Apply to state machine (deterministic function)
        v.   Track max_epoch, max_sequence
  5. If gaps or unrecoverable checksum failures:
     a. Attempt to contact peer replicas for log repair
     b. If majority have intact log: fetch missing segment
     c. Else: promote warning to fatal, require manual recovery
  6. Set current_epoch = max_epoch + 1
  7. Open new WAL file at max_sequence + 1
  8. Replay any in-progress tasks (ASSIGNED without COMPLETE):
     a. Check external effect status (see 5.4c)
     b. Write COMPLETE or reset to READY
  9. Begin accepting requests

8.5 Corruption Detection and Mitigation
Corruption TypeDetectionMitigationTorn write (power loss)Checksum mismatch + length validationTruncate at last valid recordBit rot (silent)Checksum mismatch on readReplicate from peer; if quorum corrupt, manual restore from backupMisdirected write (wrong offset)Sequence number jumpGap detection halts; peer repairMetadata corruptionManifest parse failPrevious manifest + WAL replayCheckpoint corruptionSHA-256 mismatchPrevious checkpoint

PART IX ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù OBSERVABILITY & CORRUPTION DETECTION
9.1 Metrics (Prometheus exposition)
# Safety-critical counters
task_submitted_total{shard}
task_completed_total{shard, status="success|failure|timeout"}
task_executed_duplicates_prevented_total  # should be 0
fence_token_rejections_total              # old leader attempts

# Liveness indicators
task_queue_depth{shard}
paxos_leader_elections_total{shard, reason="timeout|step_down|transfer"}
dependency_resolution_cycles_detected_total

# Performance
task_latency_seconds_bucket{le, phase="submit|assign|execute|complete"}
wal_fsync_latency_seconds

# Resource
memory_bytes{type="heap|mlock|cache"}
disk_bytes{type="wal|snapshot|free"}

9.2 Invariant Violation Detection
Continuous validators (async, each 1s):
  V1: ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†ΟΔβÄö¬§ shard, committed_sequence == max(applied_sequence)
  V2: ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†ΟΔβÄö¬§ task, COMPLETE ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΓΟΔβ²§β³Δ all dependencies COMPLETED (graph check)
  V3: idempotency_index.entries == count(COMPLETE) + count(FAILED_final)
  V4: epoch_monotonic: current_epoch >= all historical epochs

Alert: CRITICAL if any violation detected
Action: Halt shard, trigger automatic recovery

9.3 Replay Audit Tools
Tool: wal-audit shard-path [--from sequence] [--to sequence]

1. Replays WAL into in-memory state machine
2. Compares final state to declared checkpoint
3. Reports: sequence gaps, checksum failures, state divergence

Tool: determinism-check task-id

1. Re-executes task with logged inputs
2. Compares result hash to logged result
3. Reports: deterministic or not

9.4 Determinism Verification
Strategy: Shadow execution

Production: executes normally, logs all external calls
Shadow: replay on idle node with same inputs
Comparison: external call sequence + results must match

Frequency: 0.1% of tasks (configurable)
Alert: mismatch indicates non-determinism bug

9.5 Divergence Detection Across Replicas
Background process (per shard, every 30s):
  1. Leader computes state hash: Merkle tree of task records
  2. Requests state hash from followers
  3. Mismatch triggers:
     a. Binary search for divergence point
     b. Log repair from leader
     c. If leader corrupt, step down, new election


PART X ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù TEST STRATEGY
10.1 Crash Injection Testing
Framework: deterministic simulation (TigerBeetle-style)

Technique:
  1. Record all non-deterministic inputs (RNG, timing, network)
  2. Replay with crash injected at every syscall boundary
  3. Verify: safety invariants hold, system recovers, no divergence

Coverage: every WAL operation, every network send/recv, every fsync

10.2 Partition Simulation
Tool: iptables/tc wrapper or nsenter network namespace

Scenarios:
  - Leader isolated (minority partition)
  - Follower isolated from leader but not each other
  - Complete network blackout 5s
  - Slow network (100ms added latency)
  - Packet loss 1%

Verification: 
  - Safety: no duplicate execution, no committed loss
  - Liveness: majority partition continues

10.3 Fuzz Testing State Transitions
Generator: QuickCheck/Hypothesis style
  - Random valid task DAGs
  - Random injected dependencies at runtime
  - Random crash/recovery timing

Oracle: Linearizability checker (Porcupine or custom)
  - History of client operations
  - Verify existence of sequential execution equivalent to observed

10.4 Deterministic Replay Validation
Procedure:
  1. Run workload for 1 hour, record all I/O
  2. Replay recording on fresh cluster
  3. Compare final state: task states, external effects, metrics
  4. Must be bit-identical (except timestamps)

10.5 Load Testing
Profile:
  - Sustain 100K tasks/sec for 24 hours
  - Burst to 150K for 5 minutes
  - Node failure every 10 minutes (random)
  - Network partition every 30 minutes (10s duration)

Success criteria:
  - Zero safety violations
  - p99 latency < 50ms sustained
  - Recovery time < 5s per failure

10.6 Formal Model Checking
Model: TLA+ specification of core protocol

Properties checked:
  - Safety: TypeInvariant, NoDuplication, DependencyOrder
  - Liveness: TaskEventuallyCompletes, LeaderEventuallyElected

State space: Bounded (3 nodes, 2 shards, 5 tasks)
Tool: TLC model checker

10.7 Specific Test: Crash Between WAL Write and Execution
Scenario:
  T1: Client submits task
  T2: Server appends BEGIN to WAL, fsyncs
  T3: Crash (SIGKILL)
  T4: Recovery

Verification:
  - State machine shows task ASSIGNED, not EXECUTING or COMPLETE
  - Recovery detects: BEGIN without COMPLETE
  - External effect check: none applied (lease not acquired)
  - Decision: re-execute (safe, deterministic)
  - Final state: COMPLETE with result
  - Idempotency check: second identical result accepted, no duplicate execution


PART XI ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù FORMAL CHALLENGE
11.1 Proof Sketch: Exactly-Once Safety
Theorem: For any task t with idempotency key k, at most one execution of t has observable side effects.
Proof:
Lemma 1 (Unique Assignment): At any epoch e, at most one node n holds valid fencing token for t's shard.
Proof: Paxos leader election guarantees single leader per term; epoch = max(term, recovery_count) advances only on leader change or recovery; fencing token derived from epoch. ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†ΟÖ¬Ϋ
Lemma 2 (Idempotent SUBMIT): The idempotency index I ensures at most one SUBMIT record exists for k in shard history.
Proof: SUBMIT accepted only if k ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†ΟΔβ²§¬Α I. I updated atomically with SUBMIT WAL append. WAL sequence monotonic, fsync before ACK. ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†ΟÖ¬Ϋ
Lemma 3 (Execution Commit): COMPLETE record for t exists ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΓΟΔβ²§β³Δ t's effects are committed.
Proof: COMPLETE appended only after (a) fencing token validated, (b) effects prepared, (c) external lease acquired if needed. WAL fsync before ACK to client. ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†ΟÖ¬Ϋ
Main Proof:

1. By Lemma 2, at most one task record t exists for key k.
2. By Lemma 1, at any time at most one node can BEGIN t.
3. BEGIN requires fencing token valid for current epoch.
4. If node crashes after BEGIN but before COMPLETE, recovery finds no COMPLETE.
5. New leader (higher epoch) resets t to READY; new BEGIN uses new token.
6. External effect lease (acquired at prepare phase) tied to fencing token.
7. Old token invalid; re-execution safe because lease/preimage detects prior effect.
8. If COMPLETE exists, idempotency index prevents re-BEGIN.

Therefore, at most one execution's effects are visible. ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†ΟÖ¬Ϋ
11.2 Proof Sketch: No Task Loss
Theorem: If SUBMIT(t) acknowledged, then eventually COMPLETE(t) or FAILED_final(t).
Proof:
Assumption: Client receives ACK only after SUBMIT record fsync'd with majority replication.
Case analysis:

1. 
Leader survives: Leader eventually assigns, executes, completes (task queue drains unless external dependency blocks; liveness ensures dependencies resolve or timeout).

2. 
Leader crashes after SUBMIT: Majority replication ensures SUBMIT present on at least one follower. New leader elected, replays SUBMIT, task in PENDING/BLOCKED, proceeds as case 1.

3. 
Leader crashes during execution: Recovery detects incomplete execution (BEGIN, no COMPLETE). Task reset to READY, re-executed.

4. 
Infinite retry exhaustion: After max retries, FAILED_final recorded. Task not lost, terminal state reached.


In all cases, terminal state recorded. ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†ΟÖ¬Ϋ
11.3 Why Split-Brain Cannot Cause Duplication
Scenario: Network partition separates {A, B} from {C}, where A was leader.

* A continues as leader in minority partition (cannot replicate, no progress on new tasks)
* C, D, E in majority partition elect new leader with higher term
* New leader advances epoch, invalidates A's fencing tokens
* A's subsequent ASSIGN/BEGIN attempts rejected by executors (epoch check)
* A steps down on seeing higher term in any message

Maximum hazard: A may have uncommitted BEGIN in flight. If network heals before A steps down:

* Executor receives BEGIN from A (old epoch), compares to current epoch (higher)
* Rejection: FENCE_REJECTED
* A aborts task, steps down

No execution occurs on stale authority. ΟÉ¬ΔΟ΄βÄ†ΟÖ¬Ϋ
11.4 Behavior Under Indefinite Partition
Partition ScenarioBehaviorMajority partition existsContinues processing for those shardsMinority partition onlyUnavailable for writes; nodes in SUSPECT state; clients receive UNAVAILABLE with hint to retry on healthy nodesEqual split (rare with odd replicas)Tie-break via incarnation (higher wins); if equal, both sides stall until partition heals
Liveness guarantee: Once partition heals, gossip reconciliation < 10s, leader election < 1s, normal operation resumes.
11.5 Minimal Invariant Set
IDInvariantEnforcesI_seqPer-shard sequence strictly increasingTotal order of recordsI_epochEpoch monotonic across leader changesFencing token validityI_fenceBEGIN only with token = current_epochSingle executor at a timeI_depEXECUTING ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΓΟΔβ²§β³Δ all deps COMPLETEDOrdering correctnessI_idemIdempotency index ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬Λ 1 entry per keyNo duplicate tasksI_walCommitted record survives crashDurabilityI_ackNo ACK before fsyncExternal commitment safety
11.6 Commit Point Identification
OperationCommit PointSUBMITfsync of SUBMIT record, majority replication ACKASSIGNfsync of ASSIGN record (local durability sufficient, already replicated)BEGINFencing token validation + fsync of BEGINCOMPLETEfsync of COMPLETE, external effect confirmationExternal effectIdempotency lease acknowledgment from external system
11.7 Linearizability and Serializability
PropertyStatusScopeLinearizabilityPer-shardOperations on single shard are linearizable (Paxos provides total order)Sequential consistencyCross-shardDependency edges provide partial order; no global real-time guaranteeSerializabilityTask executionSingle-shard task execution serializable by WAL order; cross-shard dependencies enforce happens-before
Formal statement: The system provides shard-linearizable task lifecycle operations with causal consistency for cross-shard dependencies (dependency edge = causal link). This is the strongest guarantee achievable without global coordination, which would violate the "no global locks" constraint.

CONCLUSION
This design satisfies all stated constraints:

The design is ready for implementation and formal verification.

then EXEC_COMMIT(tΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹) must be observed in shard(tΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ)
before tΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ transitions READY.

III. Auto-Sharding Safety Closure
Auto-sharding previously introduced unsafe key-range migration.

Correction: Deterministic Split Epoch
Shard split protocol:

Freeze shard S at sequence s_split.

Write SPLIT_RECORD(s_split, new_shard_id).

Quorum commit.

New shard replays only records ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬Ξ s_split for its key-range.

Old shard marks keys ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬Ξ split_range as FORWARDED.

Invariant:

For any task t:
Exactly one shard owns t at any sequence number.
Ownership function:

owner(t, seq) =
    if seq < s_split ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ S
    else ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ hash(t.key, new_shard_layout)
Thus ownership is sequence-dependent and deterministic.

IV. Snapshot + Compaction Refinement Invariant

State(snapshot_s) + WAL[s+1..n] ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬Γ State(full WAL[0..n])

Snapshot taken only at committed index s.

Snapshot includes:

idempotency index

dependency graph

epoch

WAL segments before s deleted only after snapshot durable and manifest fsyncΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβÄû¬Δd.

Guarantee:

No historical COMPLETE record removed unless reflected in snapshot index.

V. Zombie Executor Elimination (Formal Closure)
Add invariant:

If executor begins EXECUTING under epoch e,
and system advances to epoch e+1,
then executor must observe epoch mismatch before
external Commit phase.
Mechanism:

BEGIN record includes fencing_token.

External Prepare embeds fencing_token.

On recovery, epoch bumped before accepting new BEGIN.

Executors check current_epoch via shared atomic before Commit.

Formal guarantee:

No execution begun under old epoch can reach EXEC_COMMIT under new epoch.

VI. Leader Transfer Formal Safety Lemma
Lemma (Safe Transfer):

If leader L writes LEADER_TRANSFER at sequence s,
and follower F applies all records ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬Λ s,
then F can become leader without violating:

Sequence monotonicity

Epoch monotonicity

Single-writer property

Proof sketch:

Transfer only allowed if F.matchIndex ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ²¬Ξ s.

L steps down only after quorum commit of LEADER_TRANSFER.

Any concurrent SUBMIT after s must use new term > term(L).

I_seq ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù Sequence strictly increasing per shard.

I_epoch ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù Epoch strictly increases on leader change/recovery.

I_single_leader ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù At most one leader per term.

1. Technical Weakness Catalog
For each safety property we expose a minimal adversarial trace, the invariant that is broken, why the current design leaves the hole open, a minimal corrective amendment, and a sketch of a proof that the amendment restores the invariant without collateral damage.

Two nodes NΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹,NΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ are both eligible leaders because the membership protocol does not enforce a strict majority before election (SWIM suspicion timeout ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬ΑΟ΄βÄ† 0).<br>2. By making the term bump a prerequisite for any write, duplicate commits are impossible. The change does not affect task ordering because term monotonicity is already used in the commit point definition.
1.2 Deterministic Replay
#TraceInvariantGapCorrectionProofD11. Task T reads current wallΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€clock (now()) during execution.<br>2. EXEC_COMMIT stores result rc=0 but does not store the observed timestamp.<br>3. Crash ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ replay reads log, reΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€executes T, obtains a different timestamp, writes to external DB a different row.Deterministic replay ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ replay must produce identical external state.No capture of all sources of nondeterminism (time, RNG, external service responses).Enforce pureΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€function execution: every observable input (including now, RNG seed, external request payload) must be recorded in the log entry (e.g., deterministic_context field). The executor must be a sandbox that refuses any system call not whitelisted.By construction the replay engine reΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€injects the recorded context, guaranteeing that the pure function yields identical outputs. The invariant that the log fully determines sideΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€effects is now provable by induction on log entries.D21. Task T performs a conditional write to a KV store based on a readΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€modifyΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€write that is not logged (read is done outside the WAL).<br>2. During replay the KV store has a different value, causing a different write path.Same as D1.Missing readΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€set in the log.Extend the WAL record to include a readΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€set hash and the value read (or a version number). The executor must abort if the current store version mismatches the logged version, forcing a deterministic retry.The readΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€set inclusion makes the log a complete transaction description; replay either reproduces the same write or aborts, preserving the invariant that the external state after a committed transaction is unique.
1.3 CrossΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€Shard Causality
#TraceInvariantGapCorrectionProofC11. Task A (shardΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·0) commits EXEC_COMMIT(A).<br>2. Network partition isolates shardΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·0 from shardΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·1.<br>3. ShardΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·1, unaware of A, schedules dependent task B and commits EXEC_COMMIT(B) before receiving AΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβÄû¬Δs commit.Causal ordering ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ B must not commit before A.Dependency propagation is asynchronous (dependencyΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€resolved records are sent as bestΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€effort messages). No quorum enforcement across shards.Introduce a twoΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€phase global commit for crossΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€shard edges: PhaseΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·1 ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ shardΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·0 sends prepare to shardΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·1; PhaseΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·2 ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ shardΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·1 acknowledges before allowing B to transition to READY. The prepare must be persisted on a quorum of both shards (dualΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€raft joint consensus).Joint consensus guarantees that either both shards see the prepare or neither does. Therefore B cannot be committed without AΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβÄû¬Δs commit being durably known, preserving causal ordering. The extra phase does not affect intraΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€shard ordering because it is only taken for crossΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€shard edges.C21. AutoΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€sharding moves task T from shardΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·2 to shardΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·3 while T is in RUNNING state.<br>2. The move is performed by copying the inΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€memory state without persisting a moveΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€commit record.CrossΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€shard causality ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ tasks must not be lost or reordered during reΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€sharding.No durable marker that the move succeeded; a crash after copy but before delete leaves duplicate logical tasks.Require a moveΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€transaction logged as three records: MOVE_START, MOVE_COMMIT (on destination quorum), MOVE_DONE (deleting source entry). The source entry is only removed after MOVE_COMMIT is durably replicated.The threeΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€phase protocol is a classic distributed transaction; by persisting each phase on a quorum, any crash can be recovered to a consistent state (either preΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€move or postΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€move). No duplicate execution can arise, preserving causality.
1.4 Snapshot Correctness
#TraceInvariantGapCorrectionProofS11. Leader initiates a snapshot at log index i.<br>2. Crash occurs after some follower has persisted entries i+1 ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬Π i+K but before they are included in the snapshot.<br>3. New leader restores snapshot (up to i) and reΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€applies i+1 ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬Π i+K from its own log, but the follower that missed them loses those entries forever.Snapshot consistency ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ restored state must be a prefix of the committed log.Snapshot creation does not wait for all replicas to acknowledge inclusion of entries up to i+K.Make snapshot joint: the leader must obtain acknowledgments from a majority that they have all entries up to i+K before truncating the log. The snapshot metadata must record the highest index i_max that is known to be on a quorum.By requiring quorum acknowledgment, any entry that could be lost on a minority is never truncated. The restored state is therefore a prefix of the globally committed log.S21. Compaction removes log entries older than i while a slow follower still needs them for replay after a crash.Same as S1.Compaction is driven solely by leaderΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβÄû¬Δs local waterΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€mark.Compaction must be coordinated: a leader may truncate only after receiving replication acknowledgments from a quorum for each entry to be removed.This is exactly RaftΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβÄû¬Δs logΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€truncation rule; it guarantees that any entry that could be needed for recovery is retained on a majority.
1.5 External SideΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€Effect Atomicity
#TraceInvariantGapCorrectionProofX11. Task T writes to external DB outside the WAL transaction (e.g., via async HTTP).<br>2. Crash after DB write but before EXEC_COMMIT is persisted.<br>3. Replay reΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€executes T, causing a duplicate DB row.Atomic sideΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€effect ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ sideΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€effects must appear iff the corresponding log entry is committed.No outΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€ofΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€band coordination between external system and WAL.Enforce twoΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€phase commit with the external system: the task first writes a prepare record to the external DB (idempotent), then after EXEC_COMMIT is durably stored, it sends a finalize message. The external DB must only make the effect visible after finalize.The external system becomes a participant in the atomic transaction; its state transition mirrors the WAL commit point. By the atomicity of 2PC, the sideΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€effect appears iff the log entry is committed.X21. SideΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€effect is a message sent to a downstream queue that provides atΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€leastΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€once delivery.<br>2. Crash after sending but before commit.Same as X1.No guarantee that downstream queue can be rolled back.Wrap the downstream queue in a deduplication layer keyed by the taskΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβÄû¬Δs task_id and epoch. The queue consumer must be idempotent; the producer must include the key in every message.Idempotent consumption guarantees that duplicate messages do not change external state, satisfying the atomicity invariant without requiring rollback.
1.6 AutoΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€Sharding Safety
#TraceInvariantGapCorrectionProofA11. Load balancer decides to split shardΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·S into SΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹,SΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ based on CPU usage.<br>2. It moves half the task IDs to SΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ without updating the global hash ring atomically.<br>3. Clients concurrently hash the same task_id to different shards, causing duplicate submissions.Uniqueness of task placement ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ a logical task belongs to exactly one shard at any time.ReΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€sharding is not performed as an atomic operation on the routing metadata.Use a consistentΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€hash ring versioned by epoch; any reΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€sharding must first publish a new version, then wait for all clients to observe the new version (via a quorum read) before moving any tasks. AutoΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€sharding triggers while a leader transfer is in progress.<br>2. 1.7 Fencing Token Soundness
#TraceInvariantGapCorrectionProofF11. Leader LΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹ (termΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·t) issues epoch token eΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹ to a worker.<br>2. LΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹ crashes before revoking eΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹.<br>3. New leader LΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ (termΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·t+1) issues token eΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ (higher).<br>4. Worker holding eΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹ mistakenly believes it is still valid because it does not verify term on each operation.Fencing token monotonicity ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ a later token must invalidate all earlier tokens.Token validation is performed only at acquisition, not on every sideΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€effect.Require perΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€operation token verification: every write to external state must include the current term (or epoch) and the external system must reject any operation with a stale term.Since term is strictly increasing and external systems enforce the check, any operation using eΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹ after eΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ is issued will be rejected, guaranteeing that only the latest token can cause sideΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€effects.F21. Tokens are stored in memory only; a crash loses the tokenΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€toΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€term mapping.Same as F1.No durable record of issued tokens.Persist token issuance records in the WAL (type TOKEN_ISSUE(term, token_id)). Workers must read the latest token record before proceeding.Persistence ensures that after a crash the system can reconstruct the highest valid token, preventing stale tokens from being used.
1.8 Compaction Refinement
#TraceInvariantGapCorrectionProofCmp11. Compaction removes a DEP_RESOLVED record that is the last evidence that a predecessor has committed.<br>2. A follower that was lagging restarts, replays up to the truncated index, and believes the dependency is still unresolved, blocking the child task forever.DependencyΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€resolution visibility ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ every node must be able to infer that a predecessor is committed from its retained log.Compaction criteria are based solely on log index, not on dependency graph completeness.Compaction must be dependencyΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€aware: before truncating any DEP_RESOLVED entry, ensure that all tasks that depend on it have either (a) a later READY record or (b) a snapshot that includes the resolved flag.By guaranteeing that any truncated dependency is already reflected in a later state, a lagging node can reconstruct the dependency status from the retained prefix, preserving progress.Cmp21. Log compaction runs concurrently with a leader transfer; the new leaderΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβÄû¬Δs log truncation point is stale, causing it to propose entries that conflict with the old leaderΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβÄû¬Δs compacted suffix.Leader transfer safety ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ new leader must not propose entries that have been compacted.No coordination of compaction waterΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€marks across terms.During a leader transfer, the outgoing leader must transfer its compaction watermark to the incoming leader via a COMPACTION_POINT record that is committed before the transfer completes.The incoming leader will only propose entries with index > COMPACTION_POINT, guaranteeing no conflict with alreadyΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€compactable entries.
1.9 Leader Transfer Safety
#TraceInvariantGapCorrectionProofL11. Leader LΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹ steps down after committing up to index i but before replicating i+1 to a majority.<br>2. New leader LΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ starts from its own log (which ends at i).<br>3. LΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§≈Γ receives a client request that depends on the missing i+1 entry, leading to lost work.No loss of committed work ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ any entry that reached a quorum must survive leader change.Transfer protocol does not verify that the outgoing leaderΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβÄû¬Δs commit index is known to a quorum before stepping down.Enforce preΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€stepΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€down quorum check: LΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹ must obtain acknowledgments from a majority that they have stored entry i+1 (or higher) before relinquishing leadership. If not possible, LΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹ must remain leader until it can.This is identical to RaftΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβÄû¬Δs ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟÖβÄ€leader must have committed entry at its commitIndex before stepping downΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬ù. It guarantees that any entry that could be lost is first made durable on a quorum, preserving safety.L21. Transfer occurs while a metadata shard (holding routing tables) is being compacted.<br>2. New leader reads stale routing info, misΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€routes tasks, violating crossΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€shard causality.Metadata consistency ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ routing tables must be stable across leader changes.No explicit coupling between leader transfer and metadata snapshot.Make metadata snapshot a prerequisite for leader transfer: the outgoing leader must commit a METADATA_SNAPSHOT record that includes the full routing table; the incoming leader must load this snapshot before accepting client traffic.The snapshot is persisted on a quorum, so any leader after the transfer sees a consistent view of routing, eliminating misΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€routing.
1.10 Partition Behavior Under Adversarial Timing
#TraceInvariantGapCorrectionProofP11. Network partitions into two halves, each with a minority of nodes for a given shard (e.g., 2/5 vs 3/5).<br>2. Both halves start independent election timers with zero backΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€off, causing both to think they have a majority (because they ignore the missing nodes).<br>3. Two leaders emerge, each accepting writes, violating exactlyΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€once.Partition tolerance + consistency ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ no two leaders may exist concurrently.Election timer is not bounded below; adversary can force rapid flapping.Enforce a minimum election timeout (e.g., 150ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§Ο²¬·ms) and require majority of the configured replica set, not of the observed live set. Nodes must refuse to become candidates if they cannot see a majority of the static configuration.The lower bound prevents an adversary from arbitrarily accelerating elections; the staticΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€majority rule guarantees that a minority cannot form a quorum, thus preserving the singleΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€leader invariant.P21. Partition isolates a leader from a majority; the leader continues to accept client writes (it believes it still has a quorum because it counts its own votes).<br>2. The majority elects a new leader and also accepts writes.Same as P1.Leader does not step down when it loses quorum.Implement leader lease: the leader must receive periodic heartbeat acknowledgments from a majority; missing two consecutive heartbeats forces it to step down and reject new client writes.Lease expiration is a safety mechanism proven in Raft (leader steps down on loss of quorum). It prevents a splitΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€brain where both sides accept writes.

2. Minimal Sound Core Specification
2.1 Essential Invariants (the core)
#InvariantReason for NecessityI1Linearizable commit ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ a log entry is committed iff it is stored on a majority of replicas in the same term.Guarantees exactlyΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€once, leader safety, and snapshot correctness.I2Deterministic sideΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€effect ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ every external effect is a pure function of the deterministic_context recorded in the log.Enables deterministic replay and atomic sideΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€effect semantics.I3Dependency closure ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ a task may transition to READY only after all predecessor entries are committed.Enforces crossΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€shard causality and prevents lost work.I4Fencing monotonicity ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ each epoch token carries a strictly increasing term; external participants reject stale terms.Prevents duplicate sideΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€effects after leader change.I5Metadata quorum ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ routing tables and sharding metadata are themselves replicated via a metadata shard with the same commit rule.Guarantees autoΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€sharding safety and leaderΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€transfer correctness.I6Compaction safety ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ truncation may only occur after a quorum acknowledges the truncated prefix (including all dependencyΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€resolution records).Preserves ability of lagging nodes to recover.I7TwoΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€phase external commit ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ any sideΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€effect that cannot be rolled back must be performed only after the corresponding EXEC_COMMIT is durably stored.Guarantees external sideΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€effect atomicity.
All other mechanisms (e.g., bestΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€effort gossip, optimistic dependencyΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€resolved messages) are optimizations that can be removed without breaking the core safety proof.
2.2 StrippedΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€Down State Machine

1. Submit ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ TASK_SUBMIT (persisted).
2. Store dependencies ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ TASK_DEPS (persisted).
3. Dependency resolution ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ DEP_RESOLVED (persisted).
4. Execute ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ EXEC_START (pure log entry).
5. Commit ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ EXEC_COMMIT (persisted, triggers sideΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€effect).
6. Snapshot ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§¬†ΟΔβ²§β³Δ SNAPSHOT (persisted after quorum).

No asynchronous messages, no speculative execution, no inΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€memory caches that affect safety.
2.3 Proof of Minimality
If any invariant IΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟ²¬¹ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€IΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβ²§≈ΓΟΔβ²§¬Γ is removed, a counterΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€example can be constructed from the adversarial traces above. Therefore each is strictly necessary. All other components (e.g., gossipΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€based failure detection, optimistic task stealing) are optional because they do not appear in the safety proof and can be layered on top without affecting the core invariants.

3. Formal Boundary Statement
AspectProvided GuaranteeNot ProvidedExactlyΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€onceEvery committed task produces at most one external effect.No guarantee against client duplicate submissions; idempotent clientΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€side deduplication is required.Deterministic ReplayReplay of the persisted log reproduces the exact external state.Replay does not guarantee identical wallΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€clock timestamps unless explicitly recorded.CrossΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€Shard CausalityAll dependency edges (including crossΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€shard) are respected globally.No guarantee of realΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€time ordering; only logical causality.Snapshot CorrectnessRestored state is a prefix of the globally committed log.No guarantee that a snapshot reflects all inΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€flight sideΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€effects that have not yet been committed.External AtomicitySideΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€effects appear iff the corresponding EXEC_COMMIT is durably stored.No guarantee for external systems that do not support twoΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€phase commit or idempotent writes.AutoΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€ShardingSharding changes are atomic with respect to task placement.No guarantee of instant loadΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€balancing; rebalancing may be delayed until a new epoch.Fencing TokensTokens are monotonic and validated by all participants.No guarantee that a malicious client cannot replay an old token if it bypasses validation.CompactionTruncation never discards information required for recovery of any committed task.No guarantee of space bound; compaction may be delayed under heavy load.Leader TransferNo committed entry is lost during a transfer.No guarantee of zero pause in client request processing.Partition BehaviorA majority partition continues to make progress; minority makes no progress (CP).No guarantee of availability under permanent minority partition.
CAP TradeΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€offs

* Consistency (C) ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ linearizable commits, exactlyΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€once sideΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€effects.
* Partition tolerance (P) ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ system continues under network splits (majority side).
* Availability (A) ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ sacrificed in minority partitions; the system blocks writes until a quorum is reachable.

Thus the design is CP; the impossibility of achieving AP while retaining exactlyΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€once semantics under the crashΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€recover model is wellΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€known (the FLP impossibility extended to exactlyΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§Υ€once).
