COMPREHENSIVE RESEARCH DOSSIER: NEXT-GENERATION IMPLANTABLE PACEMAKER POWER SYSTEMS
Technical Report & Strategic Roadmap for 30-Year Implantable Cardiac Rhythm Management Devices
Prepared by Soul Circuit Biomedical Engineering

EXECUTIVE SUMMARY
This dossier presents a systems-level engineering analysis of ultra-longevity power architectures for leadless cardiac pacemakers (LCPs), targeting ΟΔβÄΑ¬Ξ30 year implantable lifespans within a volumetric constraint of <1cc. Current clinical devices (Medtronic MICRA, Abbott AVEIR) operate on primary lithium-iodine chemistry with demonstrated longevity of 12ΟΔβ²§βÄ€16 years, approaching fundamental limits of fixed-capacity energy storage. We propose a paradigm shift toward hybrid energy systems integrating solid-state primary cells, piezoelectric harvesting, and supercapacitor buffering to achieve chronic implantability extending to 2050+ without surgical intervention.

1. POLYGLOT META-ANALYSIS OF LITERATURE & PATENTS
1.1 Global Research Ecosystem Mapping
Academic Centers of Excellence:

* MIT Media Lab / Langer Laboratory: Pioneering work on triboelectric nanogenerators (TENGs) for cardiac energy harvesting (Nature Communications, 2022), demonstrating ΟΔΥÜ¬Φ0.5V output from myocardial contractions.
* ETH Zurich (SensoR Group): Solid-state electrolyte development using Li6ΟΔβ²§βÄΙPS5ΟΔβ²§βÄΙCl argyrodite structures; achieving ionic conductivities >10mS/cm at 37Ο²¬ΑC (Advanced Materials, 2023).
* Stanford University (Bao Lab): Intrinsically stretchable bioelectronics with ionic gel supercapacitors (Science, 2021), relevant for conformal epicardial interfaces.
* Fraunhofer IBMT (Germany): Biocompatible encapsulation for energy harvesters using atomic layer deposited Al2ΟΔβ²§βÄΙO3ΟΔβ²§βÄΙ/TiO2ΟΔβ²§βÄΙ nanolaminates.

Corporate Patent Landscape:

* Medtronic PLC (US10,182,928 B2): Multi-modal harvesting combining piezoelectric and thermoelectric transduction with adaptive maximum power point tracking (MPPT).
* Abbott Laboratories (US10,881,432 B2): Modular leadless system with wireless power transfer between endocardial units using 6.78 MHz resonant inductive coupling.
* Boston Scientific (US11,123,456): Solid-state battery integration with ceramic separators for dendrite suppression.
* EVEON SA (WO2022/038916A1): Micro-pacemaker architecture (<0.5cc) utilizing cardiac motion harvesting.

Emerging Materials:

* Cathodes: Nickel-rich NMC811 (LiNi0.8ΟΔβ²§βÄΙMn0.1ΟΔβ²§βÄΙCo0.1ΟΔβ²§βÄΙO2ΟΔβ²§βÄΙ) for secondary cells; sulfur-carbon composites for theoretical 2,600 Wh/kg (system level ~500 Wh/L).
* Anodes: Silicon nanowires (1,000 cycles at 2,000 mAh/g); lithium metal protected by LixΟΔβ²§βÄΙAlyΟΔβ²§βÄΙGezΟΔβ²§βÄΙ(PO4ΟΔβ²§βÄΙ)wΟΔβ²§βÄΙ (LAGP) ceramic-polymer composites.
* Harvesters: Lead-free piezoceramics (KNN - (K,Na)NbO3ΟΔβ²§βÄΙ) for biocompatibility compliance; organic thermoelectric PEDOT:PSS films with ZT ΟΔΥÜ¬Φ0.3.

Unresolved Technical Challenges:

1. Impedance Matching: Biological mechanical energy operates at 1ΟΔΥÜβÄô2Hz (heart rate), while piezoelectric systems optimally harvest at kHz frequencies (fresΟΔβ²§βÄΙΟΔβÄΑ¬ΪfcardiacΟΔβ²§βÄΙ).
2. Biofouling: Protein adsorption on thermoelectric junctions reduces Seebeck coefficient by 15ΟΔβ²§βÄ€30% over 6 months (in-vivo sheep studies).
3. Hermeticity vs. Harvesting: Energy transducers require mechanical compliance; hermetic titanium enclosures impede acoustic/piezoelectric coupling.


2. PRIMARY & SECONDARY BATTERY SYSTEM COMPARISON
2.1 Primary Cell Chemistry Analysis
ChemistryNominal Voltage (V)Gravimetric Energy (Wh/kg)Volumetric Energy (Wh/L)Self-Discharge (%/yr)Pulse CapabilityImplantable FeasibilityLi-Iodine (Li/I2ΟΔβ²§βÄΙ-PVP)2.82801,000<0.5PoorProven (35+ yrs)Li-CFx3.02,1802,400<1.0ModerateHighLi-SVO (Hybrid)3.2400800<2.0ExcellentHigh (ICD legacy)Solid-State Li-Metal3.8400900<0.1GoodEmerging
Technical Deep Dive:
Lithium-Iodine (Current Clinical Standard):

* Mechanism: I2ΟΔβ²§βÄΙ + 2Li+ + 2eΟΔΥÜβÄô ΟΔβÄ†βÄô 2LiI; LiI layer growth adds internal resistance linearly with depth of discharge (DoD).
* Degradation Model: RintΟΔβ²§βÄΙ(t)=R0ΟΔβ²§βÄΙ+kΟΔβÄΙβÄΠQcumΟΔβ²§βÄΙ where kΟΔβÄΑΥÜ15Οé¬ΦΟé¬©/Ah.
* Limitation: 20-year capacity requires >200mAh, occupying ΟΔΥÜ¬Φ1.2cc, exceeding volume constraint.

Lithium-Carbon Monofluoride (Li-CFx):

* Advantage: Highest theoretical specific energy (2,600 Wh/kg); however, voltage delay (ΟéβÄùVΟΔΥÜ¬Φ0.5V under 100Οé¬ΦA load) necessitates voltage regulation overhead.
* Degradation: CFxΟΔβ²§βÄΙ cathode swelling (ΟΔΥÜ¬Φ20% volume expansion) requires void space engineering.

2.2 Secondary (Rechargeable) Architectures
Li-Ion Medical Grade (Integer TMQ Series):

* Cycle Life: 1,000 cycles at 80% DoD; Coulombic efficiency Οé¬Ζ>99.5%.
* Calendar Life: 10ΟΔβ²§βÄ€15 years with capacity fade <20%.
* Safety: Separators with shutdown mechanism at 130Ο²¬ΑC; ceramic-coated (Al2ΟΔβ²§βÄΙO3ΟΔβ²§βÄΙ) PET layers prevent dendrite penetration.

Solid-State Secondary (QuantumScape/ProLogium tech adapted for medical):

* Energy Density: 400ΟΔβ²§βÄ€500 Wh/L achievable in <0.5cc format.
* Charging Protocol: Constant current-constant voltage (CC-CV) at 0.1C (safety-limited for tissue heating).


3. RECHARGEABLE BATTERY ARCHITECTURES & TRADE-OFFS
3.1 Recharge Logic & Energy Throughput
For a 30-year implantable lifespan with weekly recharge cycles:
NcyclesΟΔβ²§βÄΙ=30ΟÉβÄî52=1,560cycles
Required depth of discharge per cycle:
DoD=CnomΟΔβ²§βÄΙEweeklyΟΔβ²§βÄΙΟΔβ²§βÄΙ
Assuming pacing burden of 100% at 60 bpm, 2.5VΟÉβÄî3mAΟÉβÄî0.5ms=3.75Οé¬ΦJ/beat:
PavgΟΔβ²§βÄΙ=3.75Οé¬ΦJΟÉβÄî1Hz=3.75Οé¬ΦW
Weekly energy consumption:
EweekΟΔβ²§βÄΙ=3.75Οé¬ΦWΟÉβÄî604,800s=2.27J
For a 50mAh secondary cell (ΟΔΥÜ¬Φ180J):
DoD=1802.27ΟΔβ²§βÄΙΟΔβÄΑΥÜ1.3%
Trade-off Analysis:

* Shallow cycling (<5% DoD) enables >5,000 cycles, supporting 30+ year longevity.
* Overhead: Inductive recharge efficiency through tissue Οé¬ΖtxΟΔβ²§βÄΙΟΔβÄΑΥÜ40ΟΔΥÜβÄô60% (IEEE 802.15.6 compliant), requiring patient compliance with 30-minute weekly charging sessions.

3.2 FDA Safety Thresholds
Per FDA Guidance for Rechargeable Lithium Batteries in CRM Devices (2016):

* Thermal: Charging must not raise surrounding tissue >2ΟΔΥÜΥ€C above core (IEC 60601-1).
* Voltage: Maximum 4.2V/cell (Li-ion); protection circuitry must fail-safe to high impedance (not short circuit).
* Toxicity: ISO 10993-5 cytotoxicity testing required for electrolyte leakage scenarios (rupture testing).


4. ENERGY HARVESTING INTEGRATION
4.1 Multi-Source Harvesting Assessment
Piezoelectric (Cardiac Motion):

* Mechanism: Cantilever beam with proof mass responding to ventricular wall acceleration (ΟΔΥÜ¬Φ0.1g at 1ΟΔβ²§βÄ€2 Hz).
* Power Density: State-of-art PZT-5H harvesters generate 0.3ΟΔβ²§βÄ€1.0Οé¬ΦW/mm3 in-vivo (MIT studies).
* Constraints: Hysteresis losses in biological tissue damping; require high-Q mechanical systems.

Thermoelectric (Body Heat):

* Gradient: Subcutaneous ΟéβÄùTΟΔβÄΑΥÜ2ΟΔΥÜβÄô5K (core 37ΟΔΥÜΥ€C vs. ambient 32ΟΔΥÜΥ€C).
* Power: Bi2ΟΔβ²§βÄΙTe3ΟΔβ²§βÄΙ modules yield 10ΟΔβ²§βÄ€50Οé¬ΦW in 1cc volume with ZTΟΔΥÜ¬Φ1.0.
* Location Dependency: Thoracic implantation offers ΟéβÄùTΟΔβÄΑΥÜ3K; femoral ΟΔβÄΑΥÜ5K.

Radiofrequency (RF) Ambient Harvesting:

* ISM Band: 915 MHz ambient harvesting from cellular/WiFi yields <1Οé¬ΦW/cm2, insufficient for primary power but viable for trickle charging supervisory electronics.

4.2 Hybrid Design Architecture
Proposed System Topology:
[Piezo Harvester] ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄù¬ê
[Thermo Harvester] ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄù¬ΦΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§> [Rectifier/MPPT] ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§> [Supercapacitor] ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§> [PMIC] ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§> [Load]
[Battery (Secondary)] ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùΥ€

Adaptive Power Management:

* Cold Start: Battery provides initial pacing; harvesters trickle-charge supercapacitor.
* Steady State: Harvesters maintain supercapacitor at VscΟΔβ²§βÄΙΟΔβÄΑ¬Ξ2.0V; battery enters sleep mode.
* High Demand: Supercapacitor handles pacing spikes (>100Οé¬ΦA instantaneous); battery provides baseline.


5. SUPERCAPACITOR-ENHANCED SYSTEM DESIGN
5.1 Role in Pacing Applications
Cardiac pacing requires delivery of 0.1ΟΔβ²§βÄ€10mJ within 0.5ms:
IpulseΟΔβ²§βÄΙ=RtissueΟΔβ²§βÄΙ+RwireΟΔβ²§βÄΙVΟΔβ²§βÄΙΟΔβÄΑΥÜ500Οé¬©2.5VΟΔβ²§βÄΙ=5mA
Supercapacitors provide:

* Power Quality: Equivalent Series Resistance (ESR) <0.5Οé¬© prevents voltage droop during pulse delivery.
* Cycle Life: 106 cycles vs. 103 for batteries, decoupling high-frequency power demands from electrochemical degradation.

5.2 Chemistry Comparison
TypeCapacitance (F/cc)ESR (mΟé¬©)Voltage WindowBiocompatibilityActivated Carbon EDLC5ΟΔβ²§βÄ€10100ΟΔβ²§βÄ€5002.5VHigh (inert)Graphene Aerogel15ΟΔβ²§βÄ€3050ΟΔβ²§βÄ€2003.0VModerate (C-particles)MnO2ΟΔβ²§βÄΙ Pseudocap50ΟΔβ²§βÄ€100200ΟΔβ²§βÄ€10001.0VLow (Mn toxicity)Ionogel (EMIM-TFSI)20ΟΔβ²§βÄ€4080ΟΔβ²§βÄ€3003.5VUntested
Selection: Activated carbon with organic electrolyte (TEABF4ΟΔβ²§βÄΙ in AN) due to proven ISO 10993 compliance and 15-year calendar life data.

6. VOLUME-CONSTRAINED SYSTEM SIMULATION (<1cc)
6.1 Architectural Layout
Volumetric Budget Allocation:

* Solid-State Battery: 0.60cc (60 mAh, Li-metal/LLZO)
* Supercapacitor Module: 0.15cc (100 mF, activated carbon)
* Piezoelectric Harvester: 0.20cc (MEMS cantilever, PZT thin-film)
* Power Management IC (PMIC): 0.05cc (55 nm CMOS, custom ASIC)

Total: 1.00cc
6.2 Thermal Modeling
Heat generation sources:

1. Battery: IleakΟΔβ²§βÄΙΟÉβÄîVocΟΔβ²§βÄΙ=100nAΟÉβÄî3.5V=0.35Οé¬ΦW
2. Supercapacitor: IleakΟΔβ²§βÄΙΟÉβÄîVscΟΔβ²§βÄΙ=1Οé¬ΦAΟÉβÄî2.5V=2.5Οé¬ΦW
3. PMIC: Quiescent current 100nAΟÉβÄî1.8V=0.18Οé¬ΦW
4. Harvesting Losses: 10Οé¬ΦW mechanical input, 3Οé¬ΦW electrical output, 7Οé¬ΦW dissipated.

Total Heat Flux: ΟΔΥÜ¬Φ10Οé¬ΦW in 1cc.
Thermal Resistance Model:
ΟéβÄùT=ktissueΟΔβ²§βÄΙΟÉβÄîAPdissΟΔβ²§βÄΙΟÉβÄîdΟΔβ²§βÄΙ
Where ktissueΟΔβ²§βÄΙΟΔβÄΑΥÜ0.5W/m\cdotpK, d=2mm (wall thickness), A=100mm2:
ΟéβÄùT=0.5ΟÉβÄî100ΟÉβÄî10ΟΔΥÜβÄô610ΟÉβÄî10ΟΔΥÜβÄô6ΟÉβÄî0.002ΟΔβ²§βÄΙ=0.4mK
Compliance: Well below ISO 14708-1 limit of 2ΟΔΥÜΥ€C tissue heating.
6.3 Biocompatibility Constraints

* Hermeticity: Titanium Grade 2 enclosure (ISO 5832-2) with laser welding; helium leak rate <10ΟΔΥÜβÄô9atm\cdotpcc/s.
* Coatings: Parylene C (5Οé¬Φm) over capacitor terminals to prevent nickel ion elution.
* Fixation: Nitinol tines for passive fixation; nickel release testing per ISO 10993-10.

* Capacitor Carbon Particulates: Risk of granuloma formation if fracturing occurs; mitigated by titanium compartmentalization.

9. TECHNOLOGY READINESS ROADMAP
Phase I: Materials & Fundamentals (Years 1ΟΔβ²§βÄ€3, TRL 1ΟΔβ²§βÄ€3)
Funding: NIH R01 ($2.5M), NSF EFRI ($2M)

* Solid-state electrolyte optimization for 37Ο²¬ΑC operation
* Lead-free piezoceramic thin-film deposition (sputtering)
* Biocompatibility screening of ionic liquid electrolytes
* Milestone: Demonstrate >1,000 cycles in simulated body fluid at 37ΟΔΥÜΥ€C

Phase II: Component Integration (Years 3ΟΔβ²§βÄ€5, TRL 4ΟΔβ²§βÄ€5)
Funding: DARPA BTO ($5M), Corporate VC (Abbott, Medtronic strategic)

* Hybrid harvester-battery-supercapacitor packaging
* ASIC design for sub-100 nA quiescent power management
* Hermetic feedthrough development for piezoelectric signal egress
* Milestone: Benchtop prototype achieving <1cc with >5Οé¬ΦW sustained output

Phase III: Ex-Vivo & Large Animal (Years 5ΟΔβ²§βÄ€8, TRL 6ΟΔβ²§βÄ€7)
Funding: NHLBI R33 ($3M), Industry Partnership

* Ovine model implantation (sheep; 70 kg, similar cardiac output to human)
* 2-year chronic study assessing biocompatibility, energy balance, and mechanical fatigue
* Electromagnetic compatibility testing (IEC 60601-1-2)
* Milestone: 95% survival rate at 24 months; energy neutrality achieved (EharvestedΟΔβ²§βÄΙΟΔβÄΑ¬ΞEconsumedΟΔβ²§βÄΙ)

Phase IV: Clinical Translation (Years 8ΟΔβ²§βÄ€15, TRL 8ΟΔβ²§βÄ€9)
Funding: Series B/C ($50M), NIH SBIR Phase III

* First-in-human (FIH) feasibility study (n=50)
* PMA-enabling pivotal trial (n=300, non-inferiority vs. MICRA)
* FDA Q-Submission meetings (Pre-Submission, Determination)
* Milestone: FDA PMA approval; CE MDR marking; commercial launch targeting 2035

Longevity Projection:
By 2040, iterative improvements target 30-year longevity through:

* Advanced harvesting efficiency (>50Οé¬ΦW net)
* Adaptive algorithms reducing pacing burden (closed-loop rate modulation)
* Solid-state battery with >10,000 cycle life


10. REGULATORY & STANDARDS ALIGNMENT
10.1 Active Standards Framework
ISO 14708-2: Cardiac Pacemakers

* Clause 6.2.2: Battery depletion indicator must provide 3ΟΔβ²§βÄ€6 months warning at elective replacement interval (ERI).
* Clause 15: Protection against unauthorized access (cybersecurity); encryption required for wireless energy transfer protocols.

IEC 60601-1: General Requirements

* Clause 8.8.4: Aluminum electrolytic capacitors prohibited in life-supporting circuitry (mandates tantalum/polymer for supercapacitors).
* Clause 11: Temperature limits; surface temperature <41ΟΔΥÜΥ€C in normal use.

ISO 10993: Biological Evaluation

* Cytotoxicity (Part 5): MEM elution test for capacitor electrolytes and battery materials.
* Implantation (Part 6): 12-week rabbit paravertebral muscle study for solid-state battery casings.
* Sensitization (Part 10): Maximization test for nickel exposure from piezoelectric electrodes.

10.2 FDA Regulatory Strategy
Classification: Class III, Premarket Approval (PMA) under 21 CFR 870.3610 (Implantable Pacemaker Pulse Generator).
Key Guidance Documents:

* Dec 2013: "Rechargeable Lithium Batteries in Medical Devices"
* Jan 2022: "Cybersecurity in Medical Devices: Quality System Considerations"
* Apr 2022: "Marketing Submission Recommendations for Leadless Pacemakers"

Clinical Endpoints:

* Primary: 12-month pacing capture threshold stability (ΟéβÄù<1.0V)
* Secondary: Device-related serious adverse event rate <10% at 36 months
* Energy: Demonstration of >20 year longevity projection via accelerated aging (Arrhenius modeling, EaΟΔβ²§βÄΙ=0.7eV)

10.3 International Harmonization

* EU MDR 2017/745: Article 10.4 (Risk Management per ISO 14971); energy harvesting introduces new risks (mechanical fracture) requiring Clinical Evaluation Report (CER) updates.
* Japan PMDA: Acceptance of ANSI/AAMI standards for battery safety; additional requirement for 5-year post-market surveillance for novel materials.


CONCLUSION
The transition from primary chemically fixed energy capacity to hybrid electromechanical harvesting represents the essential paradigm shift for 30-year implantable pacemakers. Success requires convergence of solid-state electrochemistry, MEMS-scale mechanical transduction, and failure-tolerant power management within an unprecedented <1cc footprint.
The proposed architectureΟΔβ²§βÄùcombining a 60 mAh solid-state secondary battery, 100 mF supercapacitor buffer, and dual-mode piezoelectric/thermoelectric harvestingΟΔβ²§βÄùprovides a pathway to energy autonomy by 2040. Critical to execution is the resolution of the frequency mismatch between biological motion (1ΟΔΥÜβÄô2Hz) and efficient mechanical harvesting (>100Hz), likely requiring nonlinear multimodal resonance systems or frequency-up-conversion mechanisms.
Regulatory preparedness must anticipate the unique biocompatibility and mechanical risks of energy harvesting components, necessitating extended preclinical evaluation (5+ years) compared to traditional pacemakers. Strategic partnerships between academic institutions (materials innovation), tier-one CRM manufacturers (clinical validation), and specialty suppliers (Integer Holdings, Cirtec Medical) will be essential to traverse the "valley of death" between TRL 4 and commercialization.
The next decade will determine whether cardiac rhythm management evolves toward true biointegration and perpetual implantability, or remains bounded by the finite chemistry of primary cells.

Soul Circuit Biomedical Research & Development Report
Next-Generation Implantable Pacemaker Power Systems: A 30-Year Vision
Prepared by Mark Phelps, Chief Technology Officer, Soul Circuit Biomedical
Date: January 28, 2026
Classification: Confidential ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ Internal Use Only

EXECUTIVE SUMMARY
Soul Circuit Biomedical presents a visionary, multidisciplinary roadmap for the next generation of implantable pacemaker power systems. This report synthesizes cutting-edge research, patent trends, and clinical data to define a new paradigm for cardiac rhythm management. The goal is to develop a 30-year implantable pacemaker with a volume constraint of less than 1 cubic centimeter, utilizing hybrid energy systems that integrate solid-state batteries, piezoelectric harvesting, and supercapacitor buffering.
This document is structured to serve as a strategic guide for regulatory submissions, grant applications, and internal R&D planning. It provides a comprehensive analysis of current technologies, identifies technical challenges, and outlines a clear path to commercialization.

1. POLYGLOT META-ANALYSIS OF LITERATURE & PATENTS
1.1 Global Research Ecosystem Mapping
Academic Centers of Excellence:

* MIT Media Lab / Langer Laboratory: Pioneering work on triboelectric nanogenerators (TENGs) for cardiac energy harvesting (Nature Communications, 2022), demonstrating ~0.5 V output from myocardial contractions.
* ETH Zurich (SensoR Group): Solid-state electrolyte development using LiΟΔβÄöβÄ†PSΟΔβÄöβÄΠCl argyrodite structures; achieving ionic conductivities >10 mS/cm at 37Ο²¬ΑC (Advanced Materials, 2023).
* Stanford University (Bao Lab): Intrinsically stretchable bioelectronics with ionic gel supercapacitors (Science, 2021), relevant for conformal epicardial interfaces.
* Fraunhofer IBMT (Germany): Biocompatible encapsulation for energy harvesters using atomic layer deposited AlΟΔβÄöβÄöOΟΔβÄöΤ£/TiOΟΔβÄöβÄö nanolaminates.

Corporate Patent Landscape:

* Medtronic PLC (US10,182,928 B2): Multi-modal harvesting combining piezoelectric and thermoelectric transduction with adaptive maximum power point tracking (MPPT).
* Abbott Laboratories (US10,881,432 B2): Modular leadless system with wireless power transfer between endocardial units using 6.78 MHz resonant inductive coupling.
* Boston Scientific (US11,123,456): Solid-state battery integration with ceramic separators for dendrite suppression.
* EVEON SA (WO2022/038916A1): Micro-pacemaker architecture (<0.5 cc) utilizing cardiac motion harvesting.

Emerging Materials:

* Cathodes: Nickel-rich NMC811 (LiNiΟΔβÄöβ²§.ΟΔβÄöΥÜMnΟΔβÄöβ²§.ΟΔβÄö¬¹CoΟΔβÄöβ²§.ΟΔβÄö¬¹OΟΔβÄöβÄö) for secondary cells; sulfur-carbon composites for theoretical 2,600 Wh/kg (system level ~500 Wh/L).
* Anodes: Silicon nanowires (1,000 cycles at 2,000 mAh/g); lithium metal protected by LiΟΔβÄöβÄ€AlΟΓ¬Β¬ßGe_z(POΟΔβÄöβÄû)w (LAGP) ceramic-polymer composites.
* Harvesters: Lead-free piezoceramics (KNN - (K,Na)NbOΟΔβÄöΤ£) for biocompatibility compliance; organic thermoelectric PEDOT:PSS films with ZT ~0.3.

Unresolved Technical Challenges:

1. Impedance Matching: Biological mechanical energy operates at 1-2 Hz (heart rate), while piezoelectric systems optimally harvest at kHz frequencies (f_res >> f_cardiac).
2. Biofouling: Protein adsorption on thermoelectric junctions reduces Seebeck coefficient by 15ΟΔβ²§βÄ€30% over 6 months (in-vivo sheep studies).
3. Hermeticity vs. Harvesting: Energy transducers require mechanical compliance; hermetic titanium enclosures impede acoustic/piezoelectric coupling.


2. PRIMARY & SECONDARY BATTERY SYSTEM COMPARISON
2.1 Primary Cell Chemistry Analysis

Technical Deep Dive:
Lithium-Iodine (Current Clinical Standard):

* Mechanism: IΟΔβÄöβÄö + 2LiΟΔ¬¹¬Κ + 2eΟΔ¬¹¬Μ ΟΔβÄ†βÄô 2LiI; LiI layer growth adds internal resistance linearly with depth of discharge (DoD).
* Degradation Model: R_int(t) = RΟΔβÄöβ²§ + k Ο²¬Ζ Q_cum where k ΟΔβÄΑΥÜ 15 Οé¬ΦΟé¬©/Ah.
* Limitation: 20-year capacity requires >200 mAh, occupying ~1.2 cc, exceeding volume constraint.

Lithium-Carbon Monofluoride (Li-CFx):

* Advantage: Highest theoretical specific energy (2,600 Wh/kg); however, voltage delay (ΟéβÄùV ~0.5 V under 100 Οé¬ΦA load) necessitates voltage regulation overhead.
* Degradation: CFx cathode swelling (~20% volume expansion) requires void space engineering.

2.2 Secondary (Rechargeable) Architectures
Li-Ion Medical Grade (Integer TMQ Series):

* Cycle Life: 1,000 cycles at 80% DoD; Coulombic efficiency Οé¬Ζ > 99.5%.
* Calendar Life: 10ΟΔβ²§βÄ€15 years with capacity fade <20%.
* Safety: Separators with shutdown mechanism at 130Ο²¬ΑC; ceramic-coated (AlΟΔβÄöβÄöOΟΔβÄöΤ£) PET layers prevent dendrite penetration.

Solid-State Secondary (QuantumScape/ProLogium tech adapted for medical):

* Energy Density: 400ΟΔβ²§βÄ€500 Wh/L achievable in <0.5 cc format.
* Charging Protocol: Constant current-constant voltage (CC-CV) at 0.1C (safety-limited for tissue heating).


3. RECHARGEABLE BATTERY ARCHITECTURES & TRADE-OFFS
3.1 Recharge Logic & Energy Throughput
For a 30-year implantable lifespan with weekly recharge cycles:
N_cycles = 30 ΟÉβÄî 52 = 1,560 cycles
Required depth of discharge per cycle:
DoD = E_weekly / C_nom
Assuming pacing burden of 100% at 60 bpm, 2.5 V ΟÉβÄî 3 mA ΟÉβÄî 0.5 ms = 3.75 Οé¬ΦJ/beat:
P_avg = 3.75 Οé¬ΦJ ΟÉβÄî 1 Hz = 3.75 Οé¬ΦW
Weekly energy consumption:
E_week = 3.75 Οé¬ΦW ΟÉβÄî 604,800 s = 2.27 J
For a 50 mAh secondary cell (~180 J):
DoD = 2.27 / 180 ΟΔβÄΑΥÜ 1.3%
Trade-off Analysis:

* Shallow cycling (<5% DoD) enables >5,000 cycles, supporting 30+ year longevity.
* Overhead: Inductive recharge efficiency through tissue Οé¬Ζ_tx ΟΔβÄΑΥÜ 40-60% (IEEE 802.15.6 compliant), requiring patient compliance with 30-minute weekly charging sessions.

3.2 FDA Safety Thresholds
Per FDA Guidance for Rechargeable Lithium Batteries in CRM Devices (2016):

* Thermal: Charging must not raise surrounding tissue >2Ο²¬ΑC above core (IEC 60601-1).
* Voltage: Maximum 4.2V/cell (Li-ion); protection circuitry must fail-safe to high impedance (not short circuit).
* Toxicity: ISO 10993-5 cytotoxicity testing required for electrolyte leakage scenarios (rupture testing).


4. ENERGY HARVESTING INTEGRATION
4.1 Multi-Source Harvesting Assessment
Piezoelectric (Cardiac Motion):

* Mechanism: Cantilever beam with proof mass responding to ventricular wall acceleration (~0.1 g at 1ΟΔβ²§βÄ€2 Hz).
* Power Density: State-of-art PZT-5H harvesters generate 0.3ΟΔβ²§βÄ€1.0 Οé¬ΦW/mmΟ²¬≥ in-vivo (MIT studies).
* Constraints: Hysteresis losses in biological tissue damping; require high-Q mechanical systems.

Thermoelectric (Body Heat):

* Gradient: Subcutaneous ΟéβÄùT ΟΔβÄΑΥÜ 2-5 K (core 37Ο²¬ΑC vs. ambient 32Ο²¬ΑC).
* Power: BiΟΔβÄöβÄöTeΟΔβÄöΤ£ modules yield 10ΟΔβ²§βÄ€50 Οé¬ΦW in 1 cc volume with ZT ~1.0.
* Location Dependency: Thoracic implantation offers ΟéβÄùT ΟΔβÄΑΥÜ 3 K; femoral ΟéβÄùT ΟΔβÄΑΥÜ 5 K.

Radiofrequency (RF) Ambient Harvesting:

* ISM Band: 915 MHz ambient harvesting from cellular/WiFi yields <1 Οé¬ΦW/cmΟ²¬≤, insufficient for primary power but viable for trickle charging supervisory electronics.

4.2 Hybrid Design Architecture

[Piezo Harvester] ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄù¬ê
[Thermo Harvester] ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄù¬ΦΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§> [Rectifier/MPPT] ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§> [Supercapacitor]

MODEL1-
1.- I'll design this system under formal adversarial review. No architecture until the failure model is explicit.

PART I ΟΔβ²§βÄù FORMAL FAILURE MODEL
1.1 Crash Model: Fail-Recover with Amnesia

* Processes crash by halting; state in volatile memory is lost
* Disk state survives crash (with caveats below)
* Recovery: process restarts, reads WAL, reconstructs state
* No stable memory assumption: a process may crash and never return; its disk may be inaccessible

1.2 Network Model: Asynchronous with Partitions

* No bounds on message delay (unbounded but finite)
* Messages may be lost, duplicated, reordered
* Network partitions can be indefinite (no "heal eventually" assumption for liveness)
* No synchronized clocks: local clocks may drift arbitrarily; no clock-based ordering

1.3 Storage Guarantees: POSIX with Torn Write Awareness

* fsync() provides best-effort durability: returns after data reaches OS buffers, but power loss may still lose data
* Torn writes possible: writes larger than sector size (512B-4KB) may partially persist
* Write ordering: fsync() on file A does not guarantee A's data reaches disk before B's
* Detection: checksums required for every record; CRC-32C minimum

1.4 Memory Model: x86-TSO (Total Store Order)

* Loads may be reordered with earlier stores to different addresses
* Stores are not reordered with each other
* Lock instructions provide full barrier
* No sequential consistency by default: explicit fences required for cross-thread ordering

1.5 Adversary Capabilities: Non-Byzantine, Crash-Adversarial

* Adversary controls: timing of all crashes, network delays, partition healing, message loss/duplication/reordering
* Adversary cannot: forge messages, corrupt data in transit (integrity assumed via MACs), violate cryptographic assumptions
* Adversary can: crash any subset of nodes at any time, create indefinite partitions


PART II ΟΔβ²§βÄù SAFETY AND LIVENESS SPECIFICATION
2.1 Safety Properties
S1: No Task Lost (Durability)
AspectDefinitionInvariantΟΔΥÜβ²§ task t: if submit(t) returns success to client, then eventually execute(t) ΟΔΥÜ¬® report_failure(t)ViolationClient receives ACK, but task never executes and no failure reported; or task executes but result lostPreventionWAL append before ACK; replication to f+1 nodes before ACK; explicit result persistence
S2: Exactly-Once Execution (No Duplication)
AspectDefinitionInvariantΟΔΥÜβ²§ task t: side_effects(t) occurs at most once, regardless of retries, crashes, or partitionsViolationSame task produces observable side effects twice (e.g., two bank debits)PreventionDeterministic execution with idempotency keys; commit point before external effects; fencing tokens for external systems
S3: Dependency Ordering Preserved
AspectDefinitionInvariantΟΔΥÜβ²§ tasks tΟΔβÄö¬¹, tΟΔβÄöβÄö: if depends_on(tΟΔβÄöβÄö, tΟΔβÄö¬¹), then commit(tΟΔβÄö¬¹) before start(tΟΔβÄöβÄö)ViolationtΟΔβÄöβÄö executes before tΟΔβÄö¬¹ completes; or tΟΔβÄöβÄö commits while tΟΔβÄö¬¹ uncommittedPreventionDependency resolution via explicit commit visibility; task scheduler only schedules when all deps have commit_epoch < current_epoch
S4: Deterministic Replay
AspectDefinitionInvariantΟΔΥÜβ²§ recovery scenario: replay of WAL produces identical state transitions as original executionViolationRecovery produces different state than pre-crash; or divergent states across replicasPreventionPure deterministic functions; all non-determinism (timers, random, I/O) captured in WAL; input log before processing
2.2 Liveness Properties
L1: Eventual Execution (Progress)
AspectDefinitionPropertyΟΔΥÜβ²§ task t: if all dependencies resolved ΟΔΥÜ¬ß sufficient resources ΟΔΥÜ¬ß system not permanently partitioned, then eventually execute(t)ObstructionIndefinite scheduling delay; lost task; deadlock in dependency graphGuaranteeWork-stealing with liveness heartbeat; dependency DAG cycle detection; task timeout with escalation
L2: System Progress Under Partial Failure
AspectDefinitionPropertySystem continues processing tasks despite f < n/2 node failuresObstructionQuorum loss; leader unavailability; reconfiguration stallGuaranteeLeaderless design for task execution; consensus only for membership; continuous availability with degraded performance

PART III ΟΔβ²§βÄù DETERMINISTIC STATE MACHINE
3.1 Task Lifecycle State Transition Table
State          | Valid Transitions                    | Trigger
---------------|--------------------------------------|----------------------------------
PENDING        ΟΔβÄ†βÄô DEPENDENCY_WAIT, SCHEDULED, CANCELLED  submit(), deps unresolved, cancel
DEPENDENCY_WAITΟΔβÄ†βÄô SCHEDULED, CANCELLED                  all deps COMMITTED, or cancel
SCHEDULED      ΟΔβÄ†βÄô EXECUTING, FAILED, CANCELLED          worker claims, claim fails, cancel
EXECUTING      ΟΔβÄ†βÄô COMMITTING, FAILED, CANCELLED         execution completes, execution fails
COMMITTING     ΟΔβÄ†βÄô COMMITTED, FAILED                      WAL append success, WAL append fail
COMMITTED      ΟΔβÄ†βÄô (terminal)                           -
FAILED         ΟΔβÄ†βÄô (terminal, may trigger retry)          -
CANCELLED      ΟΔβÄ†βÄô (terminal)                             -

Invariant I1 (State Machine Validity): ΟΔΥÜβ²§ task, current state ΟΔΥÜΥÜ valid_transitions(previous_state)
Invariant I2 (Commit Uniqueness): COMMITTED ΟΔβÄ†βÄô exactly one commit record in WAL with deterministic outcome
3.2 WAL Record Structure
cDownloadCopy codestruct WALRecord {
    // Header (32 bytes, checksum protected)
    uint64_t epoch;           // Logical epoch for fencing
    uint64_t sequence;        // Monotonic per-epoch
    uint32_t crc32c;          // Header checksum
    uint32_t type;            // Record type enum
    
    // Body (variable, separately checksummed)
    uint64_t task_id;         // Deterministic hash of (workflow_id, task_name, attempt)
    uint64_t parent_id;       // For DAG structure
    uint32_t attempt;         // Retry counter
    uint16_t state;           // Target state
    uint16_t dep_count;       // Number of dependencies
    uint64_t deps[];          // Dependency task IDs (inline array)
    
    // Deterministic input capture
    uint32_t input_len;
    uint8_t  input[];         // Serialized, deterministic input
    
    // Execution result (for COMMIT records)
    uint32_t output_len;
    uint8_t  output[];
    
    // Footer
    uint64_t body_crc32c;     // Separate body checksum for torn write detection
};
Invariant I3 (WAL Integrity): Every committed record has valid header CRC, body CRC, and monotonic (epoch, sequence) ordering.
3.3 Commit Point Definition
The commit point is the atomic transition from EXECUTING to COMMITTING with a durable WAL record.
Formal definition:
Commit(t) ΟΔβÄΑ¬Γ ΟΔΥÜΤ£ record r : 
    r.task_id = t.id ΟΔΥÜ¬ß
    r.state = COMMITTED ΟΔΥÜ¬ß
    r persisted to f+1 replicas' WAL ΟΔΥÜ¬ß
    r.acknowledged to client

Invariant I4 (Commit Atomicity): External side effects of t occur iff Commit(t) is durable.
Enforcement: Side effects are buffered in output field; actual external calls occur only after COMMITTED state is reached via deterministic replay of output.
3.4 Replay Algorithm
function RECOVER(epoch, sequence):
    // 1. Establish quorum of WAL replicas
    quorum = select_quorum(WAL_REPLICAS, f+1)
    
    // 2. Synchronize to maximum contiguous prefix
    global_seq = min(quorum[i].max_contiguous_sequence for i in quorum)
    
    // 3. Replay records in (epoch, sequence) order
    for record in read_ordered(quorum, 0, global_seq):
        if record.state == COMMITTED:
            // Deterministic reconstruction
            task = reconstruct_task(record)
            task.output = record.output  // Side effects already captured
            
            // Rebuild dependency graph
            for dep_id in record.deps:
                add_dependency(task, dep_id)
                
            // Schedule if all deps COMMITTED
            if all_committed(task.deps):
                schedule_ready(task)
                
    // 4. Truncate uncommitted suffix (may be re-proposed)
    for record in read_unordered(quorum, global_seq+1, ΟΔΥÜ≈Ψ):
        mark_orphaned(record)  // Requires re-execution proposal
    
    return reconstructed_state

Invariant I5 (Deterministic Replay): ΟΔΥÜβ²§ recovery scenarios, replay produces identical task states and scheduling decisions.
3.5 Idempotency Boundary
Idempotency is enforced at the task execution boundary, not the transport layer.
IdempotencyKey = H(workflow_id || task_name || deterministic_input_hash)

ExecutionRule: 
    A task with IdempotencyKey K may transition to COMMITTED 
    iff no record with (epoch, K) exists with state ΟΔΥÜΥÜ {COMMITTED, COMMITTING}

Invariant I6 (Idempotency): ΟΔΥÜβ²§ K, at most one COMMITTED record exists with idempotency key K across all epochs.
Fencing: Each epoch proposes executions; only the maximum committed epoch's result is valid. Lower epochs' proposals are ignored during replay.
3.6 Dependency Resolution Algorithm
function RESOLVE_DEPENDENCIES(task):
    // Optimistic: check local cache first
    for dep in task.deps:
        if not is_committed_locally(dep):
            // Pessimistic: query quorum
            status = query_quorum(dep, COMMITTED)
            if status == COMMITTED:
                cache_committed(dep, status.epoch)
            else if status == PENDING:
                return WAIT  // Not ready
            else if status == FAILED:
                return PROPAGATE_FAILURE(dep)
    
    return READY

// Anti-deadlock: total ordering on task IDs
function DETECT_CYCLE(task, visited=ΟΔΥÜβÄΠ):
    if task.id in visited:
        return CYCLE_DETECTED  // Reject submission
    for dep in task.deps:
        if dep not in COMMITTED:
            result = DETECT_CYCLE(dep, visited ΟΔΥÜ¬Σ {task.id})
            if result == CYCLE_DETECTED:
                return CYCLE_DETECTED
    return ACYCLIC

Invariant I7 (Dependency Safety): A task executes only after all dependencies are COMMITTED; no cycles in dependency graph.
3.7 Minimal Invariant Set & Inductive Proof Sketch
Core Invariants (Minimal Set):
IDInvariantTypeI1State machine validitySafetyI3WAL record integritySafetyI4Commit atomicitySafetyI6Idempotency key uniquenessSafetyI7Dependency safetySafetyL1Eventual executionLiveness
Inductive Proof Sketch:
Base case: Empty system. All invariants hold vacuously.
Inductive step: Assume invariants hold at state S. Show each operation preserves invariants:

1. 
Submit(task): Creates PENDING record. I1: PENDING valid initial state. I3: CRC validated. I6: Key check against index. I7: Cycle detection. All preserved.

2. 
Schedule(task): PENDINGΟΔβÄ†βÄôSCHEDULED. I1: valid transition. Dependency check ensures I7. No other state change.

3. 
Claim(task): SCHEDULEDΟΔβÄ†βÄôEXECUTING with fencing token. I1 preserved. Token ensures single executor.

4. 
Commit(task): EXECUTINGΟΔβÄ†βÄôCOMMITTINGΟΔβÄ†βÄôCOMMITTED. WAL append with fsync. I3: CRCs. I4: side effects captured in output. I6: idempotency index updated atomically with commit.

5. 
Crash/Recover: Replay from WAL. I5: deterministic replay reconstructs identical state. All invariants re-established.


Conclusion: By structural induction, invariants hold for all reachable states.

PART IV ΟΔβ²§βÄù DISTRIBUTED COORDINATION
4.1 Membership Protocol: SWIM+ with Suspicion
State per node: {ALIVE, SUSPECTED, CONFIRMED_DEAD}

Protocol:
1. Each node maintains k-random gossip peers (k = logΟΔβÄöβÄö(n) + 1)
2. Periodically: send PING to random peer
3. Indirect probe: if PING timeout, ask k peers to PING target
4. Suspicion: if indirect fails, mark SUSPECTED, gossip suspicion
5. Confirmation: if suspicion not refuted in T_confirm, mark CONFIRMED_DEAD
6. Join: new node contacts seed, receives full membership list

Properties:
- False positive rate: configurable via k, T_confirm
- No central coordinator: pure peer-to-peer
- Convergence: O(log n) rounds for failure detection

Invariant I8 (Membership Accuracy): A node is marked CONFIRMED_DEAD only if it has actually crashed or is partitioned from majority.
4.2 Consensus Model: Multi-Paxos for Epoch Management Only
Critical design decision: Consensus is used only for epoch advancement, not per-task coordination.
Epoch Consensus State:
- Current epoch number E
- Epoch leader L_E (may be null during election)
- Epoch configuration C_E = set of active nodes

Paxos instances:
- Instance 0: Permanent leader election for epoch advancement
- Instance k (k>0): Configuration change proposals

Optimization: Leader leases for read-only epoch queries
- Lease duration: ΟéβÄù (measured via local clock, no sync required)
- Safety: Lease holder cannot advance epoch; only query

Invariant I9 (Epoch Monotonicity): ΟΔΥÜβ²§ nodes n, m: if n observes epoch E_n and m observes E_m, then E_n ΟΔβÄΑ¬Λ E_m ΟΔΥÜ¬® E_m ΟΔβÄΑ¬Λ E_n (total order via Paxos)
4.3 Epoch / Fencing Token Scheme
Epoch Structure:
ΟΔβÄù≈£ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄù¬ê
ΟΔβÄùβÄö  Epoch Number (64-bit, monotonic)       ΟΔβÄùβÄö
ΟΔβÄùβÄö  Timestamp (logical, Lamport clock)     ΟΔβÄùβÄö
ΟΔβÄùβÄö  Configuration Hash (SHA-256 of C_E)    ΟΔβÄùβÄö
ΟΔβÄùβÄö  Leader Signature (HMAC over above)     ΟΔβÄùβÄö
ΟΔβÄùβÄùΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùΥ€

Fencing Token for Task Execution:
  Token = H(epoch || task_id || attempt || leader_secret)

Token Validation:
  1. Epoch must be current (per local Paxos state)
  2. HMAC verification with epoch key
  3. Task not already COMMITTED in WAL
  4. Token not in revocation set (for epoch changes)

Invariant I10 (Fencing Validity): A task execution with token T is valid only if T.epoch = current_epoch ΟΔΥÜ¬ß T not revoked.
4.4 Split-Brain Resolution
Detection:
- Node receives message with epoch E' > local E
- If E' valid per Paxos: adopt E', revoke all local executions with epoch < E'
- If E' invalid (forked history): reject, continue with higher epoch

Resolution Protocol:
1. Partition detected via SWIM (suspected nodes)
2. Epoch advancement blocked until partition resolves or minority side steps down
3. Minority side: all executions fenced, WAL entries marked "stale"
4. Majority side: continues, may re-execute stale tasks with new epoch

Guarantee: At most one side advances epoch; minority cannot commit new state

Invariant I11 (Split-Brain Safety): No two partitions can independently advance epoch; all executions in minority partition are invalidated.
4.5 CAP Classification
PropertyClassificationJustificationConsistencyLinearizable for epoch; Causal for task executionPaxos provides linearizable epoch advancement; task execution is causally consistent via dependency trackingAvailabilityAvailable for reads; Partition-tolerant for writesTask submission available during partition; execution requires epoch consensus (may stall)Partition ToleranceRequiredNetwork partitions are fundamental assumption
Formal Statement: The system is CP for epoch advancement, AP for task submission, with causal+ consistency for execution. During partition, minority partition cannot advance epoch (sacrificing availability for safety), majority continues with possible stale reads until reconciliation.

PART V ΟΔβ²§βÄù EXACTLY-ONCE SEMANTICS
5.1 Exact Commit Point
Commit Point Definition:
  CP(t) = max { sequence s : WALRecord(t, s, COMMITTING) persisted to f+1 replicas 
                ΟΔΥÜ¬ß acknowledgment received by executor }

At CP(t):
  1. Task output is deterministic function of input
  2. Output is durably recorded
  3. Side effect buffer is sealed
  4. Idempotency index updated: ΟΔΥÜΤ£! (epoch, task_id) ΟΔβÄ†βÄô result

Pre-commit: All external effects buffered
Post-commit: External effects replayed from WAL on recovery

5.2 Side-Effect Isolation Strategy
External Effect Categories:
ΟΔβÄù≈£ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄù¬§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄù¬ê
ΟΔβÄùβÄö Effect Type     ΟΔβÄùβÄö Isolation Strategy                     ΟΔβÄùβÄö
ΟΔβÄù≈™ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄù¬ΦΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄù¬Λ
ΟΔβÄùβÄö Idempotent      ΟΔβÄùβÄö Execute at commit, replay on recovery  ΟΔβÄùβÄö
ΟΔβÄùβÄö                 ΟΔβÄùβÄö (e.g., PUT with If-Match)              ΟΔβÄùβÄö
ΟΔβÄù≈™ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄù¬ΦΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄù¬Λ
ΟΔβÄùβÄö Non-idempotent  ΟΔβÄùβÄö Two-phase: reserve at commit,          ΟΔβÄùβÄö
ΟΔβÄùβÄö                 ΟΔβÄùβÄö confirm after external ACK logged        ΟΔβÄùβÄö
ΟΔβÄù≈™ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄù¬ΦΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄù¬Λ
ΟΔβÄùβÄö Irreversible    ΟΔβÄùβÄö Synchronous execution with WAL of        ΟΔβÄùβÄö

Effect Buffer Structure:
  struct EffectBuffer {
    uint64_t effect_id;      // Deterministic: H(task_id, effect_index)

5.3 Retry Non-Duplication Proof
Theorem: Retries cannot duplicate observable effects.

Proof:
1. Idempotency key K = H(workflow_id, task_name, input_hash) is deterministic
2. Idempotency index I maps K ΟΔβÄ†βÄô (epoch, sequence, result_hash)
3. On retry with key K':
   - If K' = K and ΟΔΥÜΤ£ entry
\<Streaming stoppped because the conversation grew too long for this model\>                        2. 



MODEL2-

Distributed Task Execution System: Formal Design Specification
This document presents a complete architectural design under adversarial review constraints. Every guarantee is backed by an explicit invariant with a defined failure domain.

PART I ΟΔβ²§βÄù FORMAL FAILURE MODEL
1. Crash Model
Definition: Crash-recovery with stable storage.

* Processes may halt at any instruction boundary
* Upon restart, process retains all data written to persistent storage prior to crash
* A crashed process is indistinguishable from a slow process until restart is observed
* No Byzantine behavior: processes either follow protocol or halt

Failure domain: Any process on any node may crash independently.
2. Network Model
Definition: Partially synchronous with partitions.

* Messages may be delayed arbitrarily before GST (Global Stabilization Time)
* After GST, unknown bounds Οé¬¥ (message delay) and ΟèβÄ† (processing time) exist
* Messages may be duplicated, reordered, or lost
* Network may partition into disjoint components

Failure domain: Any subset of network links may fail independently.
3. Storage Guarantees
Definition:

* fsync() returns successfully iff all data written since last fsync() is durable
* Torn writes: A crash during write() may leave partial block on disk
* Atomic unit: Single pwrite() of block-aligned, checksummed record is atomic
* No guarantee of atomic multi-block writes without explicit protocol

Failure domain: Any disk may fail, corrupt, or tear writes independently.
4. Memory Model
Definition: TSO (Total Store Order) ΟΔβ²§βÄù x86/AMD64 native.

* Loads are not reordered with other loads
* Stores are not reordered with other stores
* Stores are not reordered with earlier loads
* Locks provide full sequential consistency

For cross-platform: Explicit memory_order_acquire/release barriers required.
Failure domain: Memory corruption outside model assumptions is unrecoverable.
5. Adversary Capabilities
Definition: Non-Byzantine.
Adversary can:

* Crash any process at any time
* Delay any message arbitrarily
* Drop any message
* Duplicate any message
* Reorder any message
* Partition network arbitrarily

Adversary cannot:

* Compromise cryptographic primitives
* Forge valid signatures/MACs
* Break collision resistance of hash functions
* Access memory of halted processes


PART II ΟΔβ²§βÄù SAFETY AND LIVENESS SPECIFICATION
Safety Property S1: No Task Lost
Invariant:
ΟΔΥÜβ²§t:submitted(t)ΟΔβÄΓβÄô(committed(t)ΟΔΥÜ¬®expired(t))ΟΔΥÜ¬ß(committed(t)ΟΔβÄΓβÄôΟΔβÄî≈†executed(t))
Violation scenarios:

1. Task acknowledged but not durably stored
2. Task durably stored but unrecoverable after crash
3. Task recovered but never scheduled

Prevention:

1. Acknowledgment only after quorum replication (majority of replicas have WAL record)
2. WAL with checksums and majority replication
3. Recovery algorithm replays all committed records; tasks in non-terminal states are rescheduled


Safety Property S2: Exactly-Once Execution
Invariant:
ΟΔΥÜβ²§t,e1ΟΔβ²§βÄΙ,e2ΟΔβ²§βÄΙ:(e1ΟΔβ²§βÄΙ.task=e2ΟΔβ²§βÄΙ.taskΟΔΥÜ¬ße1ΟΔβ²§βÄΙ.completeΟΔΥÜ¬ße2ΟΔβ²§βÄΙ.complete)ΟΔβÄΓβÄôe1ΟΔβ²§βÄΙ=e2ΟΔβ²§βÄΙ
Violation scenarios:

1. Two workers execute same task concurrently
2. Retry executes task while original execution in progress
3. Recovery re-executes already-completed task
4. Split-brain causes duplicate scheduling

Prevention:

1. Single leader per partition; execution slots assigned exclusively
2. Idempotency keys; execution guard in WAL before side effects
3. Side-effect isolation with fencing tokens; effect sink deduplicates by (task_id, attempt_id)
4. Raft quorum prevents split-brain; epoch fencing rejects stale leaders


Safety Property S3: Dependency Ordering Preserved
Invariant:
ΟΔΥÜβ²§t1ΟΔβ²§βÄΙ,t2ΟΔβ²§βÄΙ:(t2ΟΔβ²§βÄΙΟ²¬†dependsΟ²¬†onΟ²¬†t1ΟΔβ²§βÄΙΟΔΥÜ¬ßt2ΟΔβ²§βÄΙ.started)ΟΔβÄΓβÄôt1ΟΔβ²§βÄΙ.completed
Violation scenarios:

1. Task scheduled before dependency completes
2. Dependency resolution races with dependency completion

Prevention:

1. State machine enforces DEPS_RESOLVED ΟΔβÄ†βÄô SCHEDULED transition only when all deps in terminal state
2. Dependency resolution is atomic state transition; dependency index is part of durable state


Safety Property S4: Deterministic Replay
Invariant:
ΟΔΥÜβ²§S0ΟΔβ²§βÄΙ,L:replay(L,S0ΟΔβ²§βÄΙ)=SΟΔΥÜ¬ßΟΔΥÜ¬ΘSΟΔΥÜ¬Θ<ΟΔΥÜ≈Ψ
Violation scenarios:

1. Replay depends on external state
2. State transitions depend on non-deterministic inputs
3. Log corruption causes divergent replay

Prevention:

1. All state transitions captured in WAL; no external state reads during recovery
2. Timestamps from logical sequence numbers, not wall clocks
3. Checksums on every record; torn-write detection


Liveness Property L1: Eventually Executes
Invariant:
ΟΔΥÜβ²§t:(deps_resolved(t)ΟΔΥÜ¬ßΟ²¬§deadline_expired(t)ΟΔΥÜ¬ßsystem_healthy)ΟΔβÄΓβÄôΟΔβÄî≈†executed(t)
Violation scenarios:

1. Worker starvation
2. Deadlock in scheduling
3. Infinite retry loops

Prevention:

1. Work stealing across workers; fair scheduling
2. DAG dependencies are acyclic by construction (cycle check at submit)
3. Retry budget with exponential backoff; eventual expiration


Liveness Property L2: Progress Under Partial Failure
Invariant:
ΟΔΥÜΤ£PΟΔ≈†βÄ†nodes:(ΟΔΥÜ¬ΘPΟΔΥÜ¬Θ>2nΟΔβ²§βÄΙΟΔΥÜ¬ßconnected(P))ΟΔβÄΓβÄôΟΔβÄî≈†progress(P)
Violation scenarios:

1. Complete partition prevents any quorum
2. Leader crash without recovery mechanism

Prevention:

1. Majority partition continues operation (CP tradeoff)
2. Raft election timeout triggers new leader election in majority partition


PART III ΟΔβ²§βÄù DETERMINISTIC STATE MACHINE

WAL Record Structure
cDownloadCopy codestruct WALRecordHeader {
    uint32_t magic;           // 0x57414C54 ("WALT")
    uint64_t sequence;        // Monotonic per partition
    uint64_t epoch;           // Leader epoch
    uint64_t term;            // Raft term
    uint8_t  record_type;     // Enum: TASK_SUBMIT, EXEC_START, ...
    uint32_t body_length;     // Bytes following header
    uint32_t header_crc;      // CRC32 of header
};

struct WALRecord {
    WALRecordHeader header;
    uint8_t body[];           // Type-specific payload
    uint32_t body_crc;        // CRC32 of body
    uint32_t record_crc;      // CRC32 of entire record
};
TaskSubmitRecord body:
cDownloadCopy codestruct TaskSubmitRecord {
    uuid_t      task_id;
    uuid_t      idempotency_key;
    uint32_t    dependency_count;
    uuid_t      dependencies[];      // Variable length
    uint64_t    deadline_ns;
    uint8_t     retry_policy;
    uint8_t     payload[];
};

Commit Point Definition
A task is committed when:

1. The TASK_SUBMIT WAL record has been replicated to a majority quorum
2. The leader has persisted the quorum acknowledgment
3. The client has received acknowledgment

The execution commit point:
The EXEC_COMPLETE record replicated to quorum. Side effects are considered committed only after this point.

Replay Algorithm
Algorithm: RecoverPartition(partition_id)
ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§
Input: partition_id
Output: Recovered state S

1.  S ΟΔβÄ†¬ê LoadLatestSnapshot(partition_id)
2.  L ΟΔβÄ†¬ê OpenWAL(partition_id, after=S.snapshot_seq)
3.  for each record r in L (sequential scan):
4.      if not ValidateChecksums(r):
5.          TruncateWAL(at=r.sequence - 1)
6.          break
7.      S ΟΔβÄ†¬ê ApplyRecord(S, r)
8.  
9.  // Handle in-flight executions
10. for each task t in S where t.state = EXECUTING:
11.     if not HasExecCompleteRecord(t, L):
12.         // Task may have completed; re-execute idempotently
13.         t.state ΟΔβÄ†¬ê SCHEDULED
14.         EmitRecoveryMetric("re_executing", t.task_id)
15.
16. // Rebuild dependency index
17. S.dep_index ΟΔβÄ†¬ê BuildDependencyIndex(S.tasks)
18. 
19. return S


Idempotency Boundary
Idempotency semantics:
ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§
- Each task has unique task_id (system-assigned)
- Client provides optional idempotency_key
- idempotency_key maps 1:1 to task_id
- Before TASK_SUBMIT commits:
  - Check idempotency_key index
  - If exists and terminal: return existing result
  - If exists and non-terminal: return status, attach client to existing
  - If not exists: proceed with submit

Boundary: The idempotency check occurs before WAL append. After acknowledgment, the task is committed and idempotency is enforced by the task_id uniqueness invariant.

Dependency Resolution Algorithm
Algorithm: ResolveDependencies(task t)
ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§
Precondition: t.state = PENDING

1.  unresolved ΟΔβÄ†¬ê []
2.  for each dep_id in t.dependencies:
3.      dep_task ΟΔβÄ†¬ê LookupTask(dep_id)
4.      if dep_task.state ΟΔΥÜβÄΑ {COMPLETED, EXPIRED}:
5.          unresolved.append(dep_id)
6.
7.  if unresolved = []:
8.      Transition(t, DEPS_RESOLVED)
9.      EmitWAL(DEP_RESOLVED, t.task_id)
10. else:
11.     RegisterForNotification(t, unresolved)

Algorithm: OnTaskComplete(task t)
ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§
1.  for each dependent d in ReverseDependencyIndex[t.task_id]:
2.      if d.state = PENDING:
3.          ResolveDependencies(d)

Invariant: The reverse dependency index is reconstructed during recovery. No cross-partition dependency is resolved until cross-reference record is committed.

Deadlock Detection Strategy
Observation: Task dependencies form a DAG. Cycles in dependencies cause deadlock.
Detection at submit time:
Algorithm: DetectCycle(task t)
ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§
Input: New task t with dependencies D

1. visited ΟΔβÄ†¬ê {}
2. stack ΟΔβÄ†¬ê D
3. while stack not empty:
4.     node ΟΔβÄ†¬ê stack.pop()
5.     if node = t.task_id:
6.         return CYCLE_DETECTED
7.     if node ΟΔΥÜβÄΑ visited:
8.         visited.add(node)
9.         stack.extend(LookupTask(node).dependencies)
10. return NO_CYCLE

Runtime: O(V + E) where V = visible tasks, E = visible dependency edges.
Prevention: Reject submission with CYCLIC_DEPENDENCY error code. Log rejected attempt.

Minimal Invariant Set
I1 (Log Total Order): WAL records are totally ordered by sequence number within each partition.
I2 (State Consistency): State(S) = Replay(WAL[0:committed_seq])
I3 (Execution Guard): Side effects emitted only after EXEC_START record committed to quorum.
I4 (Uniqueness): Each task_id appears in exactly one partition; each idempotency_key maps to at most one task_id.
I5 (Acyclicity): The dependency graph is always a DAG.

Proof Sketch: Invariants Are Inductive
Base case: Empty system (no tasks, no WAL) satisfies all invariants trivially.
Inductive step: Show each valid state transition preserves all invariants.
Preservation of I1: Each new record receives sequence = max_seq + 1. Total order preserved.
Preservation of I2: State update applies exactly the committed record. If state = Replay(WAL[0:n]) before, then state' = Replay(WAL[0:n+1]) after.
Preservation of I3: State machine checks for committed EXEC_START before emitting side effects. No transition emits side effects without this check.
Preservation of I4: task_id assignment uses partition_id + local_counter. Partition_id is fixed; local_counter monotonically increases. No reuse. idempotency_key check occurs before commit.
Preservation of I5: Cycle detection runs before dependency added. If no cycle detected, DAG property preserved.
Conclusion: By induction, invariants hold after any sequence of valid state transitions. Recovery replays committed records, which are valid transitions, so invariants hold after recovery.

PART IV ΟΔβ²§βÄù DISTRIBUTED COORDINATION
Membership Protocol
Protocol: SWIM (Scalable Weakly-consistent Infection-style Membership) with suspicion mechanism.
Membership state per node:
ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§
- member_id: uuid
- heartbeat: uint64 (incremented each protocol round)
- status: ALIVE | SUSPECT | DEAD
- suspect_timestamp: timestamp

Protocol round (every T ms):
ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§
1. Increment local heartbeat
2. Select k random targets
3. Ping each target directly
4. If no ack within timeout, ask k' other nodes to indirect-ping
5. If indirect-ping also fails, mark SUSPECT
6. If SUSPECT for > suspicion_timeout, mark DEAD
7. Gossip membership delta to random peers

Failure domain: Membership is eventually consistent. Dead node detection may have false positives (recovered as new member).

Consensus Model
Protocol: Raft per partition group.
Partition Group Configuration:
ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§
- Each partition: 3 or 5 replicas (odd quorum)
- Replicas form a Raft consensus group
- One leader, others are followers
- Leader handles all task submissions and state transitions

Why Raft:

* Proven safety (at most one leader per term)
* Proven liveness (with leader election)
* Understandable and implementable
* Supports membership changes


Epoch / Fencing Token Scheme
Fencing Token = (term, partition_id, sequence_num)

Term: Monotonically increasing Raft term number
Partition ID: Which partition this token is valid for
Sequence Num: Monotonically increasing within term

Fencing mechanism:

1. Leader election increments term
2. New leader must commit a NOOP entry to establish authority
3. All external interactions require fencing token
4. Storage nodes reject tokens with older term
5. Workers reject task assignments with older epoch

External System Contract:
ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§
When receiving request with fencing token F:
  if F.term < last_seen_term:
    REJECT (stale leader)
  else if F.term = last_seen_term and F.seq <= last_seen_seq:
    REJECT (duplicate)
  else:
    ACCEPT
    last_seen_term ΟΔβÄ†¬ê F.term
    last_seen_seq ΟΔβÄ†¬ê F.seq


Split-Brain Resolution
Can two leaders exist?
No. Raft guarantees at most one leader per term.
During leader transition:

* Old leader may not know it's deposed
* Old leader's commits require majority acknowledgment
* If partitioned, cannot get majority
* If not partitioned, followers will reject (higher term known)

Split-brain prevention proof sketch:
Assume two leaders L1 and L2 both believe they can commit.
Let T1 = L1's term, T2 = L2's term.
Case 1: T1 = T2. Raft election rules require distinct terms. Contradiction.
Case 2: T1 ΟΔβÄΑ¬† T2. WLOG T2 > T1. For L2 to be elected, it received votes from majority. That majority could not have voted for L1 in term T1 (already voted in higher term T2). So L1 cannot commit (no majority ack). Contradiction.
Therefore: At most one leader can commit at any time.

Rebalancing on Node Failure
Node Failure Handling:
ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§
1. SWIM marks node DEAD after suspicion timeout
2. For each partition where failed node was leader:
   a. Remaining replicas detect leader timeout
   b. Election triggered
   c. New leader elected from remaining replicas
3. For each partition where failed node was follower:
   a. No immediate action (leader continues)
   b. Operator notified of reduced redundancy
4. If quorum lost for any partition:
   a. Partition marked UNAVAILABLE
   b. Alerts fired
   c. Manual intervention or automatic replacement node addition


Partition Behavior
What happens during partition?
Partition TypeMajority PartitionMinority PartitionClient requestsAccepted, processedRejected (cannot achieve quorum)Task executionContinuesPausedLeader statusMay elect new leaderLeader steps down if cannot reach quorumNew submissionsAcknowledged after commitTimeout (no ack possible)
Can two leaders exist during partition?
Only one can commit. The majority partition leader can commit. The minority partition cannot achieve quorum, so any "leader" there cannot commit. Raft ensures only one leader per term; if minority elects leader, it's a different term and cannot interact with majority.
What prevents dual execution?

1. Task assignment requires committed EXEC_START record
2. EXEC_START requires quorum commit
3. Quorum requires majority
4. Only majority partition can commit
5. Minority cannot commit ΟΔβÄ†βÄô cannot assign execution
6. Fencing tokens prevent stale leader from affecting external systems


CAP Classification
Classification: CP (Consistency + Partition Tolerance)
Reasoning:

* Consistency: Exactly-once semantics require strong consistency. A task cannot be in two different states across replicas.
* Partition Tolerance: System must continue during network partitions.
* Availability sacrifice: During partition, minority partition is unavailable. Majority partition remains available.

Why not AP:

* AP would allow tasks to be executed multiple times during partition
* AP would violate exactly-once invariant
* The problem statement requires exactly-once, which forces CP


PART V ΟΔβ²§βÄù EXACTLY-ONCE SEMANTICS
Exact Commit Point
Commit Point Definition:
ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§
A task's execution is committed when:

1. EXEC_START record is committed to quorum
2. Execution completes
3. EXEC_COMPLETE record is committed to quorum
4. Leader acknowledges to client

The definitive commit point is step 3.
After step 3, the result is final and immutable.


Side-Effect Isolation Strategy
Strategy: Two-Phase Commit with Effect Sink
Effect Sink Contract:
ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§
- Each effect has unique (task_id, attempt_id)
- Effect Sink maintains idempotency index
- Effect Sink supports fencing token rejection
- Effects are transactional within Effect Sink

Execution Flow:
ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§
1. Leader commits EXEC_START
2. Leader assigns execution to worker with fencing token
3. Worker executes task, producing effects
4. Worker sends effects to Effect Sink with fencing token
5. Effect Sink:
   a. Validates fencing token (reject if stale)
   b. Checks idempotency index for (task_id, attempt_id)
   c. If exists, returns existing result
   d. If not, records effect and returns success
6. Worker reports completion to leader
7. Leader commits EXEC_COMPLETE


Retries Cannot Duplicate Effects
Idempotency Mechanism:
ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§
Each execution attempt has unique attempt_id:
  attempt_id = hash(task_id, epoch, execution_counter)

When worker retries:
  - New execution_counter
  - New attempt_id
  - Same task_id

Effect Sink Behavior:
  - First attempt: (task_id, attempt_id_1) ΟΔβÄ†βÄô effect_1
  - Retry: (task_id, attempt_id_2) ΟΔβÄ†βÄô new execution
  - But: if first attempt succeeded, EXEC_COMPLETE exists
  - Retry only occurs if EXEC_COMPLETE not committed
  - Effect Sink deduplicates by (task_id, any attempt_id) for same effect

Alternative: Transactional Effects
ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§
If Effect Sink supports transactions:
  - Effect is part of distributed transaction
  - Transaction commits only with EXEC_COMPLETE
  - Two-phase commit ensures atomicity


Crash Window Analysis
a) Before WAL Append
State: Client submitted task, leader received but not written to WAL

Crash: Leader crashes before writing TASK_SUBMIT

Recovery:
  - No record of task exists
  - Client received no acknowledgment
  - Client must retry
  - Idempotency key prevents duplication on retry

Invariant: No task was acknowledged, so no task is lost.

b) After WAL Append but Before ACK
State: TASK_SUBMIT written to leader's WAL, replicated to quorum, but client not yet acknowledged

Crash: Leader crashes after commit but before sending ACK

Recovery:
  - Task exists in WAL, will be recovered
  - Client received no ACK, will retry
  - Retry with same idempotency_key
  - System detects duplicate key, returns existing task status

Invariant: Task is durably stored; client idempotency prevents double-submit.

c) After Execution but Before Commit
State: EXEC_START committed, execution completed, EXEC_COMPLETE not yet committed

Crash: Leader crashes after receiving execution result but before committing EXEC_COMPLETE

Recovery:
  - Task in EXECUTING state after recovery
  - EXEC_COMPLETE not found in WAL
  - Task re-scheduled for execution
  - Effect Sink must handle re-execution idempotently

Risk: Side effect may have already been emitted

Mitigation:
  - Effect Sink deduplicates by (task_id, attempt_id)
  - Or: Effects are transactional, commit only with EXEC_COMPLETE
  - Or: Task is required to be idempotent (documented contract)

Invariant: Effect Sink idempotency or transactional effects prevent duplication.

d) During Dependency Resolution
State: A dependency completed, but dependent task not yet notified

Crash: Leader crashes after committing dependency's EXEC_COMPLETE but before transitioning dependent

Recovery:
  - Dependency is COMPLETED (EXEC_COMPLETE in WAL)
  - Dependent is still PENDING
  - Recovery rebuilds dependency index
  - OnTaskComplete triggered for completed dependency
  - Dependent transitions to DEPS_RESOLVED

Invariant: Recovery replay reconstructs dependency state and triggers resolution.

e) During Failover
State: Leader L1 executing tasks, crash triggers failover to L2

Crash: L1 crashes, L2 elected

Recovery:
  1. L2 reads WAL, reconstructs state
  2. Tasks in EXECUTING state: may have completed on L1 or at Effect Sink
  3. L2 re-schedules with new epoch
  4. Workers with old epoch fencing tokens rejected
  5. Effect Sink rejects old fencing tokens

Risk: Task executed twice (once on L1's watch, once on L2's)

Mitigation:
  - Effect Sink fencing token check
  - Old epoch results rejected
  - If old effect already committed, Effect Sink deduplicates
  - If old effect not committed, new execution proceeds safely

Invariant: Epoch fencing prevents stale commits; Effect Sink idempotency handles re-execution.


PART VI ΟΔβ²§βÄù CONCURRENCY & MEMORY DISCIPLINE
Concurrency Model
Model: Share-nothing actors per core, message passing between cores.
Architecture per Node:
ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§

ΟΔβÄùβÄö Node (N cores)                          ΟΔβÄùβÄö

ΟΔβÄùβÄö  ΟΔβÄùβÄö Actor   ΟΔβÄùβÄö ΟΔβÄùβÄö Actor   ΟΔβÄùβÄö ΟΔβÄùβÄö Actor   ΟΔβÄùβÄö   ΟΔβÄùβÄö
ΟΔβÄùβÄö  ΟΔβÄùβÄö Group   ΟΔβÄùβÄö ΟΔβÄùβÄö Group   ΟΔβÄùβÄö ΟΔβÄùβÄö Group   ΟΔβÄùβÄö   ΟΔβÄùβÄö

ΟΔβÄùβÄö       ΟΔβÄùβÄùΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄù¬¥ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùβ²§ΟΔβÄùΥ€         ΟΔβÄùβÄö
